- The Magic Behind our Carte Blanche Experience | Feadship: Kind: captions Language: en Feadship is recognised the world over as the leading builder of pure custom superyachts. Where all other yacht builders only allow clients to customize from existing platforms. Feadship is unique. We create genuinely bespoke superyachts. Each design starts with a blank sheet of paper. To clarify this crucial difference, between customised and pure custom creation, we have crafted an interactive tool for the world's largest yacht shows. The Carte Blanche Experience by Feadship allows visitors and invitees to discover their unique Feadship DNA in a very special way... It all begins on a paper-thin touch screen. Visitors to the Feadship stand are presented with a carefully devised survey that uncovers simple, yet key elements of their design DNA. Choose your favourite playground If you were a car, which of these would you be? How adventurous are you? What's your favourite colour? Where would you take your Feadship? And, while we are imagining, what would be the name of your Feadship? By collecting your answers, your Feadship DNA is established. A fast and balanced system, driven by complex algorithms, selects images to create a personalised mood-video. This shows the visitor's selections from the survey in smooth motion. There are even five different versions of a custom soundtrack from which the system can select: intense, sophisticated, suave, light or lively Everything is geared to achieving the best matching video possible. To really make an impression, the dream name of the yacht that was entered into the survey is visible on the stern of the sunlit hull in tangible, solid letters. We have made absolutely sure that every single video is unique: even if two people enter the exact same set of answers, an elaborate system prevents identical videos from existing. And the system does more! It visualises the visitor's Feadship DNA in the Character Matrix. A bespoke yacht perfectly reflects the character of its owner whether this be classical or contemporary, quirky or majestic: Our Character Matrix shows what the visitor naturally leans towards. As generous as Feadship is to its fan base, it obviously goes the extra nautical mile for selected guests. In the show setting this means a more sumptuous experience is offered where a deeper interaction is key. The survey is held in a dedicated, more secluded area, using a touchscreen console flanked by two lounge chairs. During a one-on-one conversation with the visitor, a designer translates the cues by hand into a sketch, visible on a large screen. After the first result, further refinements are made. The print-out the potential client takes home radiates Feadship design and is designed to encourage people to turn their dreams into reality. And yet, there is more... Upon returning home, all visitors will find their unique Feadship video in a tailored e-mail. Moreover – there's a sweep stake connected that will excite all visitors with the possibility of getting the guest-treatment over a Skype session with Feadship! But of course every visitor is a winner when they follow the link in their tailored e-mail to a personalised online space. Here they find their spot on the Character Matrix, as well as their unique Design Theme. It is all brought into motion in the video, that is also included. All the individual characteristics, selections and preferences in a unique visitor website they can return to it at any time and share their Feadship DNA with the world. Taken as a whole it offers a fine representation of what Feadship means by pure custom creation! While the system is a complex marvel of algorithmic technology, Feadship staff have easy and smooth access to the data. In addition, the system relays the average time that visitors spend in the stand. The wealth of information gathered makes possible a fine-tuned approach to potential clients, where 300 hours worth of video-processing can be carried out and delivered stunning in variation, beautifully executed. The hardware, too, is innovative: We combined LG's 55” Oled wallpaper display with a zero-interference touch surface - pure user comfort. LG has stated, that this very combination introduced to the world the thinnest 55” touch screens possible. Taking into account the myriad of both visible and invisible factors, this system is almost as sensational as the Feadships it portrays.

Launched in 2015. >> This was the moment the industry gasped. I mean truly. >> Length 83.5 m. So that's 274 ft nearly. But we aren't here to talk about the length. >> No, >> we need to talk about what's under the hood because when Savannah launched, it was marketed as the world's first true hybrid superyacht. >> And hybrid is a buzz word now. But back then, >> right, my neighbor's Toyota is a hybrid. What does it actually mean in this context on this scale? It means a radical departure from a century of naval tradition. Conventionally, a yacht of this size has two massive diesel engines driving two shafts spinning two propellers. >> That's the standard. >> It is. It provides redundancy. If one engine blows up in the middle of the ocean, you can still limp on the other. >> Right. The old saying, two is one, one is none. Basic maritime safety. >> And Savannah has one main engine. >> Just one. One single warton L24 stroke diesel. >> Stop. You're telling me a billionaire paid hundreds of millions of dollars for a boat that has less redundancy than a Staten Island ferry? If that engine quits in the middle of the Atlantic, are they just drifting? >> That's the friction point. That's why this was such a gamble, such a brave move. >> But here's the engineering [music] logic behind it. Think about drag. Parasitic drag. >> Go ahead. When you have a twin screw yacht, you have two shafts, two struts holding the shafts, and two propellers dragging through the water all the time. >> Even if you turn one engine off to save fuel, which some boats do, >> that stationary propeller acts like a giant brake, it creates massive turbulence. It's incredibly inefficient. >> Okay, that makes sense. You're literally driving a dead anchor through the water. >> By moving to a single massive centerline propeller, feed ship eliminated that parasitic drag entirely. The hole shape becomes much sleeker at the aft, much more hydrodnamically efficient. >> But the safety question remains, what about that? >> To address your safety concern and the efficiency goal, they built a powerhouse electrical system around it. The main engine is coupled with three huge Caterpillar gen sets and a lithium-ion battery bank capable of holding 1 million watts. >> A million watts. That's I can't even picture that. That's a lot of AA batteries. >> It's a massive bank. But the secret weapon, the real genius of the system is the thruster. Behind that main propeller, there is a counterrotating azipole [music] thruster. >> Azipole. We need to unpack that. It sounds like a wrestling move. >> Think of it as a steerable electric pod that hangs off the back of the boat. It can spin 360°, so it also acts as a rudder. But the genius is in the hydrodnamics. >> Kan, >> the main propeller spins one way, right? in doing so creates a spiral of water, a vortex, a tornado of wasted energy moving backward. >> Okay, I'm with you. >> The azipole sits directly in that slipstream in that tornado and it spins the other way. [music] >> So it catches the tornado. >> Exactly. It catches that wasted rotational energy and converts it back into useful forward thrust. It's like a turbocharger for the water. It straightens out the flow >> and that [music] provides the redundancy. >> That's your redundancy. If the main diesel dies for some reason, the gen sets fire up. They power the Azipole directly and you could cruise home comfortably at [music] a good speed. >> So, it's not reckless. It's just a completely different calculated way of thinking about the problem. >> It's the breathe concept feedship had developed in 2010, but this is the first time it was brought to life and the numbers were staggering. They claimed a 30% fuel efficiency improvement over a standard yacht of this size. 30% >> 30%. In the maritime world where you fight tooth and nail for 1% or 2% gains, 30% is a revolution. It's a generational leap. >> It allows for different modes, right? You can run on just the diesel or diesel electric >> or fully electric harbor mode. And this is the real flex. Imagine arriving in Monaco or St. Barts. >> Mhm. >> Usually a super yacht arrives with this low rumble, you know, spewing a bit of diesel fume. Savannah glides in on battery power. Totally silent. Wow. >> No noise, no vibration. It's a ghost. People on the dock wouldn't even know it's moving until it's right there. >> That is the ultimate luxury. Silence. But speaking of flexes, we cannot talk about Savannah without talking about the paint. >> Oh, the paint. >> I've seen the photos. It's the seafoam metallic green. It's absolutely stunning. But in the notes here, it says the paint job was a nightmare. I mean, it's just paint. >> Saying just paint is a massive insult to what they pulled off here. Okay, >> you have to understand the chemistry of metallic paint. It has millions of microscopic aluminum flakes suspended in it, >> When you spray it, those flakes have to land flat and align in the exact same direction. That's what gives it that shimmer, that liquid metal look. >> Okay. Like shingles on a roof. They all need to be pointing the same way or it looks wrong. >> Exactly. A perfect analogy. On a car, a robot arm sprays the whole body in 90 seconds in a controlled booth. The flakes align perfectly. No problem. But Savannah is 83 m long. You can't put it in a booth, >> right? Humans have to spray it. But here's the problem. If you spray a 10 m section, >> stop, go have a sandwich, and come back an hour later to spray the next section. >> The edge of the first section has already started to dry, the flakes at that edge are locked in a different orientation from the new wet paint you're applying. >> And you get a stripe, you see a line, >> you get banding, you see a visible line where the light hits differently. It completely ruins the illusion of a solid metal object. It looks patchy. >> So to paint Savannah, Feedship had to create a single continuous moment of application. >> A single continuous perfect moment. >> What does that look like logistically? It It sounds impossible. >> It looks like a military operation. First, they built a massive climate controlled tent around the entire hall. >> The whole thing. >> The whole thing. Dustfree, humidity controlled. Then they brought in 46 specialized painters and they used electrostatic spray guns for the first time on this scale. >> What do those do? >> They give the paint particles an electrical charge and the hole is grounded so the paint is literally magnetically attracted to the hole. It ensures an incredibly even coating and less overspray. >> Okay, so they're all suited up. They have their special guns. >> And once they started, >> couldn't stop. >> They could not stop. They had to move in a synchronized wave, a human assembly line along the entire length of the hole. No brakes, no shifting shifts. >> That sounds incredibly grueling. >> It's physically demanding. You're in a full hazmat suit. You're breathing through a respirator, holding a heavy gun, sweating buckets, and you're hyperfocused on maintaining the exact [music] distance and speed, >> knowing that if you sneeze or you trip, you might ruin a multi-million dollar finish. >> The pressure on those guys must have been astronomical. And the result, >> the result is what is widely considered the largest metallic object ever painted by hand. It looks like a single piece of green liquid metal. There are no flaws. >> And it really emphasizes the design, which has this floating super structure. >> It does. >> The top doesn't look like it's connected to the bottom. How did they pull that off? >> It's clever visual trickery. They used highly polished stainless steel strips, aluminum supports, and very dark glass to hide the structural pillars. So your eye just sort of glides over the connections. >> Exactly. It looks like the white upper decks are just hovering weightlessly over that gorgeous green hall. >> Which brings us to the other impossible feature on Savannah, the glass. >> Ah, yes. >> Specifically, the Nemo Lounge. Now, I've seen underwater windows on fairies or glass bottom boats. What makes this a big deal? >> It's the scale and the location. The Nemo Lounge is on the port side aft. [music] It's a stadium-seated cinema room, but instead of a movie screen, your primary view is through these massive glass walls. >> And the water line cuts right across the middle of the glass, doesn't it? >> That's the magic. You're sitting there in a comfortable chair, and half the view is the sky and half is the underwater [music] world. >> So, you can watch the waves splash against the glass at eye level. >> You can see the fish swim by. >> And it also looks into the swimming pool, which is on the deck above. It's this complex layered intersection of views, sky, sea, and pool water all through one pane of glass. It can also convert to a cinema. A screen drops down. >> But structurally, my brain hurts thinking about this. Glass is brittle. The ocean is heavy. Waves are violent. How do you put a giant window halfway underwater without compromising the integrity of the steel hall? with incredible thickness and advanced lamination technology. The glass has to be many, many inches thick. And it has to withstand two kinds of pressure. >> Okay, >> there's hydrostatic pressure. That's the constant immense weight of the water just sitting there. But then there's the dynamic slam of a wave hitting it at 20 knots, >> a completely different force, >> a violent sharp impact. Feedship had to prove to the classification societies, the safety regulators like Lloyd's Register, that this window was for all intents and purposes as strong as the steel hall surrounding it. >> And the designer of this incredible interior, Christina Gerardi Bernardo, she wasn't even a not designer, was she? >> No. And that's the secret to its success. I think she came from high-end retail architecture. She designed stores for Dior and Kenzo. >> So, she had no preconceived notions. >> She didn't know the rules of yacht design, so she broke all of them. She hated straight lines. If you walk through Savannah, almost nothing is straight. The corridors snake and curve. The walls are rounded. The aft deck pool flows seamlessly into the main salon through huge curved sliding glass doors. [music] >> It sounds organic, like living inside a giant polished seaell. >> It completely blurred the line between inside and outside in a way that had never really been done before. It wasn't just a boat. It was a flowing inhabitable sculpture. Okay, so that's Savannah, the artistic hybrid pioneer. An incredible machine, >> a true masterpiece. >> Now, let's fast forward 6 years to 2021, and we arrive at Viva, originally known as Project 817. >> This is a different beast entirely. >> Viva is bigger, 94 m, that's 308 ft. But the story here, the core concept starts with a challenge that sounds like a logic puzzle. >> It really does. It was a repeat client for Feed Chip. [music] He already owned a 62 m feed chip. He loved it, but you know, he wanted more space. It's a common problem. >> A common problem. So he wanted a new 94 m boat that is 32 m longer in terms of volume and tonnage that is essentially double the boat. >> Okay. So he wants a bigger house. Simple enough. >> But he gave feedship a mandate, a non-negotiable condition. He said, "I want this new massive boat to have the exact same environmental footprint as [music] my old, much smaller boat." >> Wait, wait. That's That's defying physics again. You're moving twice the mass through the water. You need more energy. How do you possibly burn the same amount of fuel? >> That was the moonshot brief. >> Yeah, >> that was the challenge. And feedship had to scrape and claw for every single percentage point of efficiency gain possible. It wasn't one big magic bullet. It was a thousand small obsessive things >> like what where do you even start with a problem like that? >> Well, first the hole form. They ran computer fluid dynamic simulations for months and months, tweaking the shape of the hole by millimeters to reduce water resistance. Then the propulsion, it's an advanced hybrid system, an evolution of what they learned from Savannah. It can cruise at a very respectable 12 knots on diesel electric power alone, which is incredibly efficient. >> But engines produce heat, a lot of waste heat. >> And usually that heat is just blown out the stack, wasted into the atmosphere. On Viva, they capture it. They have a waste heat recovery system. >> How does that work? >> It takes the thermal energy from the engine exhaust and the generators and uses it to heat the swimming pool, heat the hot water for the showers, and even help run the climate control systems. >> So, they are literally recycling the energy they already paid for to create. >> Precisely. They also installed a state-of-the-art waste treatment plant that processes everything on board to drinking water quality before it's discharged. >> And the exhausts, >> they put catalytic converters and particulate filters on the exhausts. [music] It's tier 3 compliant. This 94 m giant runs cleaner than a lot of city buses. It's an incredible achievement. >> It's amazing. But looking at the pictures of Viva, it doesn't look like an eco experiment. It looks stark. It's this brilliant pearl white. Has that aggressive vertical plumbau, >> But the design philosophy is less is more. And honestly, reading the notes, it sounds more like less is more expensive. >> Minimalism is the most expensive style in the world. >> Why is that? Because you can't hide any mistakes. >> Every line has to be perfect. Every joint has to be invisible. There's no trim to cover a gap. >> Which brings us to the no doorork knobs rule. Please explain this. It sounds crazy. >> The owner was obsessed with clean, uninterrupted lines. He didn't want to see a hinge, a latch, a handle, or a doorork knob anywhere on the exterior superructure. He wanted the boat to look like it was carved from a single solid block of material. >> So, how do you get in? How do you open a door? >> Electric sliding doors everywhere. You approach, a sensor sees you, and the door just glides open silently. >> Like you're on the Starship Enterprise. >> Exactly like Star Trek. But now think about the engineering implications of that. A boat, even a massive steel and aluminum one, is a flexible structure. It's a living beam on the water. When it hits a big wave, the hole twists and bends. It hogs and it sags. >> Right? It's not perfectly rigid. >> Not at all. Mhm. >> Now, if you have a regular door with a handle and a latch, the latch has some play in it. It can wiggle a few millimeters as the frame flexes. No big deal. >> If you have a flush mounted electric sliding door with millimeter tight tolerances and the boat's frame twists, >> the door jams and now you're trapped in the lounge. >> Exactly. So, to fulfill the no doorork knobs request, Fedship had to make the entire superructure incredibly unnaturally stiff. They had to engineer the chassis so that it wouldn't flex enough to ever pinch those doors. >> That is a massive structural commitment just for an aesthetic preference. >> That's Fchip. They find a [music] way. Even the staircase is described as a work of art. A single piece of mirrored stainless steel. >> But the biggest structural headache wasn't even the doors, was it? It was the beach house concept, the glass. Again, >> this is the defining feature of Viva, even more than the ecote tech. The owner wanted the main decks to feel like an open plan beach house. So, floor to ceiling windows everywhere, >> which sounds lovely. >> It is, but he didn't just want big windows. He wanted to remove the bull works. >> Okay. For the non- sailors out there, what exactly is a bull work? >> It's the solid wall at the edge of the deck. It's usually about waist high. Its primary job is to keep you from falling overboard. Sure. But structurally, it's like the rim of a shoe box. >> The rim of a shoe box. I like that. That's a great visual. >> If you take an empty shoe box and try to twist it, [music] the rim, the little walls keep it rigid. >> If you cut that rim off, the box becomes incredibly floppy. It twists easily. On a ship, the bull works provide crucial longitudinal stiffness. They fight the twisting forces of the sea. >> And the [music] owner said, "Cut them off so I can have a better view of the ocean from my sofa." >> That's basically it. He didn't want a solid wall blocking his view. He wanted a clear glass railing or nothing at all. So, they essentially turned a rigid shoe box into a floppy flatbed truck. >> And then they had to figure out how to stiffen it all back up again without putting the walls back on. This required a complete reimagining of the internal steel and aluminum skeleton of the yacht. >> How'd they do it? >> They likely had to dramatically increase the scantlings, that's the thickness and depth of the internal beams and frames, and use higher grades of steel in critical areas to handle a torsion. Torsion being that twisting force like when a big wave hits the front corner of the boat. >> Exactly. Without the bull works, the massive glass windows themselves would have to take some of that stress. And if glass takes torque [music] stress, it shatters catastrophically. So the frame had to be so stiff that the glass never felt to twist. >> That is just wild. You're literally fighting the fundamental forces of the ocean with pure geometry and material science. >> And the result is spectacular. When you're inside, you feel like you were just floating over the water. There's absolutely nothing between you and the horizon. >> It also has a massive beam 14 m wide and that allowed them to do something crazy with the tenders, the smaller boats. It carries >> the tender garage. Yeah. Usually on a boat this size, you'll carry a very nice 8 or 9 m boat, you know, limo tender to take you to shore, >> Viva carries a 14 m tender. >> That's 45 [music] ft. That's a yacht. >> It's a yacht inside a yacht. Feedship had to design and install the largest hole shell door they had ever built just to get this thing in and out. It's like a Russian nesting doll of luxury. >> So, Viva is the minimalist technical marvel, the Eco Warrior. Now, we move to the final sister in our Trinity, Bliss. Also launched in 2021. In fact, it was built right alongside Viva in the same yard. >> They were literally neighbors in the construction hall. You could walk from one to the other. >> But they couldn't be more different in their purpose. If Viva is a sleek, modern beach house, Bliss is, what's the analogy? Bliss is a Range Rover, or maybe more accurately, a tank in a tuxedo. >> A tank in a tuxedo. I like that. >> It's 94.75 m, so effectively the exact same size as Viva, but the mission profile is completely 180° different. This wasn't built for the marinas in the south of France. This was built for the ends of the Earth. >> It's a true explorer yacht. >> It's a Polar Code certified vessel. >> Okay, let's define that term because it sounds cool, but what does it actually mean for the steel and the construction? It means the hole is seriously reinforced to withstand impact with ice. Now, we're not talking about smashing through thick pack ice like a nuclear icebreaker. We're talking about navigating in Arctic and Antarctic waters where there's a lot of floating ice growlers and bergie bits, chunks of ice the size of a car or a small house. >> And you don't want to hole your haul on one of those. >> Exactly. So, the steel plating at the water line is much thicker. The internal framing is denser and stronger. It's built to take a punch from ice and win. >> So, if you hit a chunk of ice the size of a minivan, the boat wins. >> The boat wins. It also has an incredible range of 6,000 nautical miles. That is truly trans oceanic. You can cross the [music] Atlantic and come all the way back without having to refuel. >> But the feature that really highlights the modern explorer nature for me is the dynamic positioning system. We hear this term with oil rigs. Why is it on a super yacht? >> It is crucial for responsible exploration. Imagine you are the owner of Bliss. You've taken your family to a remote, pristine atal in the Pacific. It's a marine sanctuary with delicate coral reefs. >> Oh, okay. >> Or maybe you're in a deep fjord in Norway. The water is 300 m deep. You can't drop an anchor. >> Why not? >> In the Pacific, the anchor and its chain would pulverize the fragile coral. It would cause untold damage. In the fjord, it's simply too deep. You don't have enough chain. But you want to stay right here to launch the submarine or watch the sunset, >> right? You want to park, but the wind is blowing at 30 knots. The current is pushing you sideways. Dynamic positioning is a computer system that links the boat's GPS to all of its thrusters and propellers. >> And it does what? >> It calculates the drift thousands of times a second and automatically fires the thrusters and adjusts the propellers just enough to perfectly counter the forces of the wind and current. It's like balancing a broom on the palm of your hand, but the broom weighs 3,000 tons. >> That's the perfect way to put it. It keeps the yacht inside a virtual box of just a few meters automatically for as long as you want. It allows the boat to hover without ever touching the fragile seabed. >> It's a conservation tool as much as a convenience. >> Absolutely. >> But here's the trick with Bliss. [music] I've seen photos of other Explorer yachts. They usually look rugged, almost industrial. They've got huge cranes on the deck, exposed tenders. They look like workboats. Bliss looks slick. >> That was the design brief given to Studio Devoot. The owner wanted it to be tough, but not to look tough. It's a sleeper, a wolf in sheep's clothing. >> So, all the rugged Explorer gear, the tenders, the submarines, the massive cranes needed to launch them, it's all hidden, >> all concealed behind flush fitting panels and hatches. The exterior lines are clean, elegant, classic feed ship. You would have no idea it was an iceclass vessel until you saw it pushing through a slush field in Antarctica. >> And the interior by Remy Tessier matches that contrast. It's not a Spartan industrial space inside. >> Not at all. It's extremely warm and comfortable. And the logic there is perfect. If you're spending your day outside in the brutal freezing cold of the Antarctic watching icebergs float by, >> you don't want to come inside to a cold minimalist interior. >> No, you want a womb. You want a warm embrace. >> A sanctuary. Exactly. Tessier used natural woods, soft fabrics, incredibly warm and inviting lighting, and the ceilings are unusually high. Bliss has a huge interior volume, nearly 3,000 gross tons. It feels like a grand house, not a ship. >> And because it's a family boat designed for very long expeditions far from land, it has to be a completely self-contained entertainment complex. >> Which brings us to the Swiss Army knife Helc. >> I love this feature. This is so cool. It is. It's a fully certified helipad reinforced for landing a heavy twin engine helicopter. But the moment the helicopter leaves, the deck completely transforms >> into what? >> Into a full-size sports court. You can play basketball, tennis, even soccer up there. It is recess lighting for night games. >> Playing a game of basketball in the middle of the Pacific Ocean. That's just another level. >> And below decks, there's a comprehensive wellness center, a huge gym, a spa, massage rooms. Because if you are three weeks away from the nearest port, you can't just pop ashore for a jog. The boat has to provide everything for physical and mental well-being. >> So, we have the Trinity Savannah, >> the metallic green hybrid pioneer with the mindbending paint and the underwater [music] cinema, >> the artist, >> FIA, the pearl white glass wrapped minimalist ecoarrior built on an impossible premise. The minimalist >> and Bliss, the hidden tank, the tuxedo clad family explorer capable of polar travel and endless adventure. >> The adventurer. >> Three boats, same yard, same era, radically different philosophies. >> It's an incredible display of versatility. >> Which brings us to the verdict. We started this deep dive by asking if feedship is truly the holy grail. Why? I mean, there are other great German yards, Lerson Aking, and Raasmusen that build incredible massive boats. What is the specific feedship magic? >> I think it's the synthesis. It's the ability to do all of these things at the highest level. Some yards are great at building huge volume. Some are great at building fast boats. Some specialize in explorers. Right. >> Feedship proved with this trinity that they can tackle any discipline, art, architecture, exploration, and execute it at a level that becomes the new industry benchmark. >> And there's one critical factor we haven't touched on enough. It's something you can't see in photos. The silence. >> Ah, yes. The feedship noise and vibration standard. This is an obsession for them. >> I read a report from a captain of another brand of boat, a custom line. He was bragging about how quiet his anchor winches were, and the quote was, "He said they were feedip scale." >> That's the ultimate compliment, isn't it? When your brand name becomes the adjective for quality, it's like saying something is bulletproof. You ask, "Is it feedip quiet?" How quiet are we talking about? Really? >> There's an anecdote about Savannah from its early days. When running in silent electric mode, some guests on the lower deck cabins complained about a faint rhythmic noise. The engineers went down. They brought all their sensitive acoustic equipment and they investigated. The noise was the sound of the small waves lapping against the outside of the hole. >> They were hearing the ocean. >> They were hearing the ocean because the boat itself was making zero discernable noise. That's the level they operate at. They float the floors on rubber mounts. They isolate every pump and pipe. They balance the propeller shafts to microscopic tolerances. >> And that fanatical attention to detail is what protects the value. I assume >> it does. Let's be very clear. Yachts are terrible investments. They are massively depreciating assets. They burn money for fun. >> A hole in the water you pour money into. >> Exactly. But feed ships depreciate slower than almost anything else on the water. There is a robust market for used feed chips because buyers know the bones. The core engineering is perfect. >> It's also an entry ticket to a very exclusive club, isn't it? Steve Jobs commissioned one before he passed away. Larry Ellison has owned several. It's a signal. >> It is. It signals that you didn't just buy a boat off the lot. It signals that you had the vision and the patience to create something unique from scratch and the resources to hire the [music] absolute best in the world to build it. >> It's the ultimate custom commission. >> It is. They engineer a way to say yes to things other yards would just dismiss as impossible like an underwater cinema or removing structural bull works. >> So what is the future? Savannah started the hybrid trend. Viva push the boundaries of efficiency. Where does feed ship go from here? What's the next frontier? >> Hydrogen. >> Hydrogen fuel cells on this scale. >> Project 821. It's their next big leap expected around 2025. They're building a massive yacht designed to run primarily on liquid hydrogen. The only emission from the fuel cell is pure clean water. >> That is the true holy grail then. Zero emissions luxury cruising. >> It's incredibly difficult. Hydrogen is much less energy dense than diesel. So it takes up a lot of space. It's volatile and hard to store safely at sea. But if anyone can figure out how to package that technology into a safe, reliable luxury envelope, it's this team. >> So here is my final thought for you listening. I want to take you back to that imaginary factory floor we talked about at the beginning. >> The dream factory. >> You were sitting at the table. Across from you are the Dutch engineers. They have their notebooks open. They're looking at you. You have the cart blanch, the blank check, the blank sheet of paper, >> the ultimate freedom, the ultimate question. >> Which path do [music] you choose? Do you choose the savannah path? Do you want to create a piece of floating art? a sculpture in seafoam green that pushes the boundaries of how a boat can look and feel and interact with the water. >> Or do you choose the viva path, the path of extreme discipline, the philosophy of less is more, the intellectual challenge of doing more with less, of hiding all the engineering to create a pure seamless glass beach house that floats on the horizon? >> A very different mindset. Or finally, do you choose the bliss path? The primal urge to go further, to explore, to build a beautiful, comfortable fortress that can keep your family safe and happy in the ice, in the storm, in the most remote, untouched corners of [music] the map. >> You know, it's not just a choice of boat. It's a personality test. >> It really is. Feedship proves that in the world of super yachts, the only real limit is physics. And as we've seen today with floating glass and silent engines, even physics seems to be negotiable for the Dutch. >> Only for the right price. >> Always for the right price. Thanks for diving deep with us. Until next time.

- The Journey of Feadship: History, Knowledge and Innovation | Feadship: Kind: captions Language: en Like every great work of art, each Feadship begins with a blank sheet of paper. An idea that begins in the mind and is given form by craftsmen and artists. Pure custom creation. No compromises or cutting corners. Nothing standing between dream and reality. No two yachts are ever alike. we call this carte blanche It defines who we are and forms the foundation of every yacht we've ever built by hand since day one. Carte blanche is the very core of Feadship's DNA. In 1949 a handful of Dutch shipbuilding families pooled their resources and reputations to form the First Export Association of Dutch shipbuilders, or Feadship. For Dutch shipbuilders, journeying beyond their borders was nothing new. But 1949 was different. In the aftermath of the Second World War, America had assumed pole position of the luxury yacht industry. Wooing American aristocrats and industrial titans wasn't just an opportunity. It was Feadship's strategy for survival. By 1957 the Feadship family comprised two iconic Dutch shipyards Van Lent and De Vries and the country's foremost naval architecture studio - De Voogt. They formed a signature Feadship style and a signature way of working. And by 1961 Feadship had become the most exclusive yachting brand in the world. In the history of shipbuilding being Dutch has always been a badge of honor. At Feadship, we wear this badge with pride. Over the years we've driven more than a few of our clients mad by our Dutchess. By our exactness and unveiled honesty. We'd apologize. But how do you excuse something that's in your DNA? It is part of what we call Uniquity. Our undeniable attention to all the invisible details that make every Feadship effortlessly luxurious. Uniquity focuses not only on the things you see, but also on the things you don't. Less noise. Less vibrations. Less clutter. Less is more. Building a Feadship of one’s own is a highly personal experience. In fact, it is the most intimate investment our customers will likely ever make. Which is why we don't build Feadships for our clients. We build Feadships with them. It is collaboration that spans years and often lifetimes. From the first designs and laying the keel to the final christening. Building a Feadship is a personal journey with significant milestones along the way. We do everything in our power to deliver an overwhelming experience. Transforming age-old maritime traditions into unforgettable moments and turning each visit into a tailored adventure for our clients and their families. Collaborating on a Feadship should be every bit as inspiring as sailing one. As the size and shapes of yachts continue to evolve, so do the imaginations of the men and women who want them. Yachts once built to cruise leisurely along European coastlines must now have the size, power and range to continuously cross entire oceans. Being able to deliver these supersized yachts means being on the forefront of change. Setting new industry standards. Pioneering new techniques, technologies and onboard amenities. Striking the ideal balance between innovation and tradition, age-old instinct and openness to new ideas. Innovation not for the sake of innovation but innovation as the relentless pursuit of perfection. The pure custom creation made possible by extraordinary craftsmanship and an ecosystem of shipbuilding specialists that stretches across The Netherlands. We were the first luxury yacht builder to centralise design and architecture under one roof. By treating design as an equal partner in the shipbuilding process, we opened our doors to the fresh new ideas and unorthodox influences of many of the industry’s great design icons. As today’s superyachts become ever more versatile and ever more supersized, we are unafraid to explore the future together with our clients. With our new shipyard in Amsterdam and extensive build-out in Makkum, we have expanded our expertise of ultra large Feadships up to 160 metres. And while size does matter, we will continue to seek out the materials and invest in the expertise to make tomorrow's superyachts lighter, quieter and more powerful. Yachts with unimpeded views and maximum privacy. Data-driven, motion sensitive and hybrid propelled. Yachts custom built to meet the most micro-demands of the world's macro personalities. How do we know? Because we're already building them. After all, there are yachts, and there are Feadships.

- Your Ultimate Guide to Yacht Financing in Singapore: Kind: captions Language: en if you are thinking of buying a yacht in Singapore you might be wondering how to finance your purchase there are different options available depending on your budget preferences and eligibility in this video we will explore some of the most common ways to finance a yacht in Singapore and provide some tips and advice to help you make the best decision one of the most straightforward ways to finance a yacht is to pay cash this means that you have enough savings or liquid assets to cover the full cost of the y including taxes fees and maintenance paying cash has some advantages such as avoiding interest charges having full ownership of the yacht and having more bargaining power with the seller however paying cash also has some drawbacks such as tying up a large amount of capital losing the opportunity to invest in other assets and exposing yourself to Market fluctuations and depreciation another option to finance a yacht is to take a loan this means that you borrow money from a bank or a financial institution to pay for the yacht and repay the loan over a period of time with interest taking a loan has some advantages such as spreading the cost of the yacht over time preserving your cash flow and benefiting from tax deductions on interest payments however taking a loan also has some drawbacks such as paying more than the original price of the yacht having to meet the lender's requirements and criteria and risking repossession of the yacht if you def fault on the loan a third option to finance a yacht is to lease it this means that you rent The Yacht from a leasing company for a fixed period of time and pay a monthly fee that covers the depreciation maintenance and insurance of the yacht leasing a yacht has some advantages such as having lower upfront costs having more flexibility in choice and having the option to buy the yacht at the end of the lease however leasing a yacht also has some drawbacks such as paying more than the market value of the yacht having limited control and customization of the yacht and having to abide by the terms and conditions of the lease a fourth option to finance a yacht is to join a yacht club or a yacht sharing program this means that you become a member of a club or a program that owns and operates a fleet of yachts and pay a membership fee that gives you access to use the Yachts for a certain number of days per year joining a yacht club or a yacht sharing program has some advantages such as having lower costs having more variety and convenience and having less hassle and responsibility however joining a yacht club or a yacht sharing program also has some drawbacks such as having limited availability and flexibility having to share the yacht with other members and having to follow the rules and regulations of the club or the program as you can see there are different ways to finance a yacht in Singapore and each one has its pros and cons the best option for you depends on your personal situation preferences and goals before you decide you should do your research compare the options and consult a professional advisor you should also consider the total cost of ownership which includes not only the purchase price but also the ongoing expenses such as maintenance Insurance birthing Fuel and crew buying a yacht is a big decision and you want to make sure that you can afford it and enjoy it for years to come if you're interested in buying a yacht in Singapore more we can help you find - How Do Luxury Yacht Owners Manage Dry-docking Challenges? - Luxury Life Report: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] How do luxury yacht owners manage dry docking challenges? Imagine having a beautiful yacht that needs a little TLC, but you want it back in the water as soon as possible. That's where smart planning and expert help come into play. When a luxury yacht needs to go into dry dock, it means removing it from the water so maintenance and repairs can happen safely. This process is essential to keep the yacht looking stunning and running smoothly. Owners usually book dry dock space many months ahead at top shipyards that specialize in luxury vessels. This early booking helps avoid lastminute surprises and guarantees access to the best facilities. They pick between floating dry docks, which are mobile and good for smaller yachts, and graving dry docks, which are permanent and better for larger, more complex boats. The choice depends on the size of the yacht, and what work needs to be done. Coordination is key. Owners work with specialists in hole cleaning, applying antifouling paint to prevent marine growth, fiberglass repairs, and engine servicing. These experts ensure all work meets strict safety and quality standards. Because luxury yachts are often used for private trips or charters, owners want to minimize downtime. They do this by pre-ordering parts, scheduling work efficiently, and working with experienced service teams. This way, the yacht spends less time out of the water without sacrificing quality. Safety and regulation compliance are also priorities. During dry docking, inspections are carried out to meet international maritime safety rules. This helps protect the owner's investment and keeps the vessel in good standing with authorities. Advanced equipment like heavy cranes and engineered support systems are used to lift and support the yacht during maintenance. These tools reduce the risk of damage and make repairs more precise. Once the work is finished, the yacht underos se trials and performance checks. These tests confirm everything is working perfectly from speed and fuel efficiency to onboard comfort. Many owners relying on professional yacht management companies to oversee the entire process. They handle logistics, inspections, and quality control, making sure the yacht is ready for its next adventure. Managing dry docking challenges involves careful planning, expert teamwork, and high-tech tools. It's all about keeping the yacht in top shape so owners can enjoy their luxury vessel without worry knowing it's maintained to the highest standards.

- What Challenges Face Opulent Superyacht Design? - The Rich And Famous Files: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] What challenges face opulent superyacht design? Imagine a floating palace built just for the most famous and wealthy people in the world. Creating such a vessel is a huge challenge that involves more than just making it look stunning. First, designers must find the perfect balance between eye-catching aesthetics and strong engineering. The superyacht needs to be beautiful and personalized to match the owner's style, but it also has to be seaorthy and reliable. Using lightweight materials like carbon fiber helps make the hole sleek and fast without sacrificing strength or safety. Adding luxury features like swimming pools, outdoor cinemas, dance floors, and multiple luxurious cabins is another big task. These amenities requiring complex structural work and mechanical systems that work perfectly together. They must be integrated so the yacht remains stable and easy to operate. Privacy and security are also key. High-profile owners want to feel safe and undisturbed. This means installing advanced surveillance systems, secure access points, and designing parts of the yacht to hide from outside view. Personalization is a major challenge, too. Every owner has their own taste, from interior decor to onboard technology. This often involves working closely with designers and architects to create custom acoustics, lighting, and artwork. Each dit hail must be precisely engineered to meet the owner's preferences. Environmental concerns are becoming more important as well. Modern superyachts need to reduce emissions and fuel use. Designers are now incorporating hybrid engines and innovative hull designs to make these vessels more eco-friendly while still delivering high performance. Managing a large crew adds another layer of complexity. The layout must allow crew members to work efficiently while guests enjoy privacy and comfort. This includes designing separate areas for crew and guests as well as easy access to all parts of the yacht. All these factors make designing a superyacht a demanding process. It's about creating a vessel that is not only a symbol of wealth and luxury, but also safe, functional, and environmentally conscious. Every detail must be carefully planned to meet the high expectations of the world's most famous owners.

- Sailing Culture & Boston Yacht Club | Marblehead Mini Docuseries, Episode 2: Kind: captions Language: en I'm Chris McNamera and today we're exploring the sailing soul of Marblehead through the people, the passion, and the harbor that define it. In Marblehead, sailing isn't just a pastime. It's part of the town's DNA. The harbor has shaped this community for generations, and few know that better than sailor and sale maker Tomas heras, when people think of Marblehead, they think of sailing. You grew up here, sailing in the harbor. Seems to be part of the rhythm of life in this town. Can you paint a picture of what that means to the people of Marblehead? I think the maritime history and the and the culture revolving around being on the water is incredibly strong here. Um, you know, ever since, you know, settlers, you know, began living in Marblehead, people have always been connected to the water. Uh, whether it's fishing, sailing, recreational, people of all ages, kids are out there, people in their retirement years, people racing, it's just part of the DNA in Marblehead. Um, you know, for as long as I can remember. Um there's always been racing going on. Uh you know the waterfront is is very very important to the culture here. People people look at weather forecasts, tide forecasts, wind forecasts um instead of Google Maps and traffic around here. >> Love it. >> Uh so yeah, it's definitely very important for the people of the population here. >> So we're here at the historic Boston Yacht Club. Can you describe what this place represents, not just as as a club, but also as it relates to the community? >> Yeah, the Boston Yacht Club um is a really strong foundational part of the the Oldtown Marble Head. Um it's where people congregate to get, you know, access on the water, to meet up with um old friends, make new friends, uh just do any socializing and for quick access to the water. you know, from here you have Crosby's, Mattis, State Street, the whole culture uh of Oldtown. Um, but the BYC's always been sort of a hub for uh for water access for, you know, pretty much anyone that wants to join um or any guest. I think people here are really proud of their connection with the water. Um, you know, the town's almost synonymous with with the mooring field and with the ocean and and all the activities. You know, it's rare to find such a small town with so much activity on the water. Um, usually the waterfront is is just one aspect of a town or a city. Here, it's almost the entire identity. Um, and that's really cool and people respect that and they care about it and they, you know, people volunteer, they nurture the waterfront culture and um, you know, it's part of the it's part of the genetic composition of the people here. It's very cool. And so, speaking of any summer afternoon, you can see hundreds of sails in this harbor. Um, what are we looking at? Can you tell me what's going on on any given day in the summer? Um, is it racing? Is it leisurely enjoying the the water and and where are they going? >> Well, sometimes they're not going anywhere. They're just doing laps and they're doing a like a short race or two. Um, but it's a variety of activities. I'd say, you know, some people are cruising. They're going destination sailing. They're going from Marblehead to Gloucester, Marblehead to the Southshore, Boston. Um, there's a lot of that's going on, especially if it's a nice day. Uh, but also there's kids out learning how to sail. There's kids learning how to race. There's um adults doing casual racing um you know on on most weekend days. There's also the occasional international competition that happens in Marblehead a couple times a year. Uh so you'll see that uh bring in people from around the world to race. Um so yeah, so that's what's happening, you know, kind of out, you know, outside the harbor and but also a couple miles offshore at the same time. Um, sometimes that's not visible from land, but it's definitely happening out there. >> Very cool. And you've competed in the World Championships, and that's is it often hosted in Marblehead? When was that? And what was that like sort of sailing against the best sailor in the world in your home waters? >> So, every couple years, I'd say, there's a there's a international world championship hosted by a yach club in Marblehead. Um, the most recent one was in 2022 was the International Starclass World Championship. And the Star is a type of boat. It's a double-handed um exolympic class boat, uh, which has a really strong following. And they, you know, consistently get 100 boats at their at their events. So, that was here in 2022. The one before was in 2018. Um, and then periodically, you know, every decade or so, we get like a major rigata like that. Uh, and it's really cool to have friends, you know, from other parts of the world that come and visit Marblehead. Um, it's funny actually. You go I I travel all these places in in Europe and South America and all over North America and people know about Marblehead. They've they've heard of it or they've or they've been here on a previous trip. Um, it's really cool to see that, you know, how well how well known the town is. >> Super cool. How do you feel about your performance in the 22 22 World Championship? >> You know, we were we were top 10. I think we were eighth. >> That's impressive. >> Eighth. Um >> Wow. >> And you know, it was it was good for us. You know, we sailed against a bunch of um ex Olympians and prior world champions. So, um you know, top 10 was we were very happy with. Yeah. >> I guess it's speaks to the prowess of the local marble head program. You grow up here and there's just talent. You're just you're learning from the best. And um >> yeah, >> you know, it's a hot bed for sailing talent, I guess you could say. >> Yeah, I mean, Marblehead does have a number of uh sort of icons that grew up here, uh or that call this harbor their home. Um you know, a a number of innovators in the field, you know, call Marblehead home. and and uh it's that's definitely part of the history that uh that is really interesting and attractive to me is that you know there's a lot of like important historical figures that have sailed here or you know uh competed in these waters. Nice. So changing gears for a second uh you work at Doyle Sales. It's a worldrenowned salemaking company and it has strong roots here in Marblehead. Um, how did this town become a hub for sale making and sort of maritime innovation? >> Yeah, that's a really good question. Um, yeah, so Doyle Salemakers was started by Robbie Doyle. You know, Robbie was um, uh, a Marblehead native. He worked for Ted Hood, who was known globally as the innovator of in the sort of the creator of salemaking. um uh he started uh hood sailmakers back in the 60s I believe. Um and so people know that Marblehead is sort of the birthplace of of salem um and over the years it's sort of developed into this hub um where really experienced uh craftsmen and sail makers are you know very common to come out of Marblehead. Um I'm lucky to have learned from many of them and uh you know that's what I do currently. I I uh I work at a salemaking facility and we make sales for some of the best uh racing boats in the planet which is really cool and people know that you know something that is made in Marblehead is going to be high quality. >> Nice. Do you feel there's a connection between the work you do and sort of what Marblehead stands for? You know, uh, I was thinking about this earlier, uh, and I'd say that, you know, Marblehead is is a town that was started by Puritans, by Yankees. There's a lot of that oldtime mindset. It's really hardworking people, people that care about the community, uh, care about others. Um, and it's people that have had to adapt to their environment, and that creates innovation. Um and so over the years there's been several innovators in the maritime world that have come out of here and I think it's based on you know this hardworking, smart, innovative culture um that is very highly concentrated here and it's unlike many other places in the world. Um it's just a very high concentration of of people working towards you know similar goals. Uh, and so I think that's what's made, you know, Marble Head an outlier in that. >> Awesome. So, how has sailing shaped who you are as a person and as a member of your community? >> Well, sailing has been incredibly important to my life. Um, I've ever since I was a little kid, I've just been racing every weekend. Um, it's just been part of what I do. Uh, even the cold weather, you know, when when the weather turns here in the fall, it still keeps going. Uh, believe it or not, uh, either here or, you know, traveling to Florida in the winter, it's just I can't I can't imagine a life without it. It's it's been a privilege. Uh, through going to, you know, different international events, I've I've been able to see lots of different countries, different ports, meet a ton of different people. I've been exposed to, you know, just so many different cultures, and I've, you know, I I truly value that. Um, I can't I don't think I could replace that experience with anything else. Um, and also, you know, I I my role in a boat is is fairly unique. I'm sort of skippering in sort of the captain at the moment um for these different campaigns. And it's been really valuable to learn how to sort of manage you know people and temperaments and you know get goals and objectives and and sort of managing that um has taught me a lot about how to you know how to interact with people and how to respect different you know viewpoints and um I've learned a lot. So it's been incredibly valuable for me this whole experience. >> Very cool. All right, last one for you, and I know you've touched on it, but if you had to describe the spirit of Marble Head, what would you say? >> The spirit of Marblehead, it's definitely people that want to have fun, want to have, you know, want to socialize, meet meet new friends, uh, and continue making, you know, stories and history with with old friends through, you know, through the avenue of of water access. and just always being able to at a moment's notice hop on a boat and quickly make a memory. - Erith Yacht Club: A History: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] In the late 19th century, Ith was a growing industrial town. The population was increasing rapidly and major changes such as a sewage system and electricity were being introduced for the first time. The reason for this development was simple. The river From time in memorial, Aith has relied on the rivers. It's its lifeblood. It's a hard landing place. So, it made it ideal right throughout the ages for seaman to land. For businesses in the industrial revolution, good access to the river meant cheap and easy transport for their goods. Factories sprung up along the riverside. It wasn't long before some of the business owners found a more fun and relaxing advantage to being by the river. I's excellent sailing conditions. The unique situation that it's got is that it's on a stretch of the river that has got severe bends in it so that you get good sailing conditions at all times. And secondly, the width of the river of course at Aith is a quarter of a mile which is quite wide. So it enabled the bigger yachts in the early days of racing to be able to come down to Aith and get a degree of sheltered yaching. In 1885, Mr. Frank Beedle of Beedle Brothers Coal Importers became the first chairman of the Ithtown Regata. In 1900, Beedle and the regata secretary, Mr. Stone, helped found Ith Sailing Club. The name changed to Irith Yacht Club in the following year. In these early days, the sailing club was the reserve of rich industrialists. The first Commodore, Mr. Gays, was one of the owners of the Cannon and Gays Flower Mill, which had a site so large that it blocked the view of the waterfront for the people who lived in the town. The first clubhouse was the old Corinthians clubhouse by St. John's Church. From the outset, the club was as much about the social life of the members as the sailing. It wasn't unknown for the club members to sail down river to Cami Island, Graves End. And one of the places that they frequented quite often was the lobster smack in the merry men of Ith became known for their heavy drinking and sea shanties. They'd only drink beer from court bottles. Pints were too small for them. The early 20th century was a fine time for the club with everinccreasing numbers of members, boats, and fixtures. Ith itself was booming too. And with increased activity on the river, the club found itself short of moing space. After almost three decades in the same clubhouse, Yacht Club was looking for a new home. In 1929, the club moved down river to its first floating clubhouse, an old 1860s temp's barge, the Garson. Although it was much loved by the members, it was far from a perfect solution. It would regularly flood and even sink at high tide. At one point, the committee even stopped charging locker fees as the members belongings kept getting drenched inside them. This didn't put the members off. They were too busy sailing a wide range of boats, including cruisers and large yachts. The outbreak of the Second World War transformed Yacht Club. As in the First World War, the Royal Navy Volunteer Reserves called up many of the younger members. The older members who stayed behind were charged with keeping the club going, which they did against all odds. Yacht Club during the Second World War, like all other yacht clubs, were banned from sailing. The yacht club just kept going during the war as a social function. It was too big a club and too interesting a club just to disband because the war was on. Every Saturday night at 9:00, the remaining members toasted their friends overseas. The Garson itself joined the war effort, becoming the training ship Garson. Now without even a clubhouse, the club's last lifeline became a monthly bulletin which contained news and light anecdotes alongside the names of members who were killed on active service. As the war drew to a close, the bulletin reported that the moorings, the club equipment, and even the garson itself would all need to be written off and replaced. When the war ended, the surviving members returned to Ith only to discover that the club was now a shadow of its former self. The members were now regular local men. Yacht Club had long since ceased to be the domain of rich industrialists. For £160, they purchased a new timber clubhouse. next Trinity House lightboat, which they named Garson 2. They rolled up their sleeves and set about painstakingly renovating it. The river was open for use again. And so the club restarted with a new lease of life. One of the members who had survived the war was Michael Robinson. Robbie, as he was affectionately known, was a world expert in Dutch marine art and became one of the most dedicated members of the club. Robbie would always wear uh extraordinary old clothing, I suppose. Really, he was just part of his character. Whenever you see a photograph of Robbie, it he's always wearing the same woolly hat and the and the khaki jacket. Robbie had spent his childhood in Hamble in Hampshire and was interested in the old workboats that they used down there which were known as Hamble Stars. In the 50s, these 14 ft dingies were being broken up. So, he rescued some and brought them down to Ith. In the postwar period, Robbie was instrumental in getting children involved in the club for the first time. This started for practical reasons. Robbie needed someone to crew for him on his humble star. Fred Frink was 11 at the time. He came over and saw my father and said, "I don't want an adult in the dinghy with me." He said, "Because they weigh too much." And my father sort of said, "Yes, that's okay by me." And then that's how I started sailing dingies at US. We always used to win because the cruisers so in those days were so much bigger and heavier and they weren't designed to sail that fast. It's like racing a push bike against a motorbike. I suppose I was the only kid down here and everybody else was much older. They're all ex-military cuz the war had just finished. I thought they were a load of grumpy old men. They used to moan my father if I ran so much across the deck on the club ship. But on the other hand, if they saw me tying a bowl in incorrectly or sculling incorrectly or rowing not properly, they used to pull me to one side and say, "That is the way you do it. Not like this." When Fred left to join the merchant navy, Robbie invited some 11-year-old boys from the local sea scouts to join the club, and he trained them to sail the Hamble Stars. He even had some more boats built for this purpose. These children became known as Robbie's little boys. He had this little group of sea scouts. He committed himself to uh their training and they had to commit themselves to to being trained. We were told not to misbehave and we had to keep quiet and we weren't allowed to make too much noise and that sort of thing. All of which we did, of course. The boys that that I was um mostly friendly with and and sailed against were Steven Green, Joe Lurin, and Keith French. The boys soon began racing each other in the Hamble Stars. And the young sailor they all wanted to beat was Steven Green. I always used to think that he got a better boat than me, but he always used to reckon he was a better helm. My boat was the fastest. Let's put it that way. Now whether that was me or whether it was a boat or whether it was a bit of both, I don't know. I suppose the first one I remember was I won a handicap class at Swanish regata and came home with a a large cup which was uh somewhat memorable when I was perhaps 15. And this is Tamar Riata 1958. Racers took the boys far from Arith. Steven remembers an annual race fixture against the naval training cadetses of HMS Worcester. When we first went down there, we went on board this magnificent boat. It looked as though it was new. The decks were scrubed teak and everything was and span. The brass was polished. We came from here where it was thick black mud. We would go down in tatty baseball boots, some probably some mud still clinging to them, old clothing, and we would go on on board this ship with the cadetses in their uniforms, taken down below and given a magnificent spread of tea after the events. And it was, you know, I felt a little out of place. Everyone at the club was expected to help out when it came to club maintenance. If you wanted the club to exist, you had to do an awful lot of things which couldn't be regarded as sailing. The sort of concept that if you want something out of life, you got to put something back in. There, what you got out of it was say 5% and what you had to put in was 95%. You came down with scruffy clothes, got dirty, and went home with dirty scruffy clothes. One of the recurring problems they encountered was how to get the boats in and out of the water. In the mid60s, Steven Green's father, Jimmy, came up with a solution, a unique and innovative causeway design, which the boys helped to build. My father was a m manager of one of the mills in the docks, and they would place in the sides of the docks, which were big pieces of wood. So, my father managed to get hold of these pieces of wood, and they floated them down on the e tide. These dock sides were cut with big double-handed saws, holes drilled with orugers, and then railway lines bolted on top, and then the whole thing was driven by a winch powered by an old motorcycle engine. We had people like Robbie, who was a a maritime art specialist, but there he would be up to his knees in mud finding solutions to problems that other people would have perhaps called up a special engineer. At Yacht Club, the ethos has always been to solve those problems yourself. Um, and this is something that we learned from being one of Robbie's little boys. The club helped its members to see the world. Brian Smart took a self-built boat all the way to Australia, and every year, Robbie took the boys on a club trip to Holland. Another annual event in the life of the club has been the trip of four stars to Holland for two weeks sailing on the lakes of Freezeland. The boats are towed up river to the lower pool. There's a brew up halfway. Of course, I was captivated by obviously being able to smoke. It seemed to be that I could walk into a tobaconist and and buy cigarettes and things like that without a buy or leave. We camped on the boats. What we did was put the floorboards up on the seats and put sleeping bags on those. Raise the boom a little bit and put a a tent over the top. So that was our sleeping quarters. So it was a cheap holiday. In the 60s, the club opened its doors wider to allow women to join. Newer members started to bring in some lady members. So I can remember and Possel and Frink joining the club and used to just pile in with the little boys and you know did all the things that we did and she was she was great. I was 16 when I first came down here. It was actually on my father's birthday so I've always remembered. Every Sunday we used to come down weather permitting there'd be racing there'd be sailing around just whatever people wanted to do. Irish. Throughout the 50s, 60s, and 70s, the heart of the club was the Garson, too, with the social life centered around the ship's cozy little bar. The Garson was a lovely little snug. You know, you everybody had to talk because you were all jammed in a small space together and you couldn't you couldn't have individual conversations. They were all one big conversation, you know. somebody start the singing up and we'd all join in. But there was sea shanties, rugby songs, you name it, we sang it. Wartime songs, the lady members, if they didn't like it, they could go out, but the words weren't changed. France, they were absolutely fabulous. and uh some of them I don't think perhaps mom and dad would have liked to have known that I was singing but still by the late 1970s Garson 2 was falling into disrepair. Although we got a lot of fun out of her she was a lot of work you know because of her age she was leaking we kept having to cork the hole to stop the water getting in. You could sit in the bar and you could hear the cutery going jingle jingle jingle in the galley and that was the rats in there. Honestly, I'm not joking, you know, and and you could sit in the bar and get dripped on and that was because the lady's l was overflowing. So in 1980, the members began to look for a new home for Yacht Club. Following the tradition of Garson's one and two, they were determined to keep the clubhouse on the water. This was the ship they decided on, the Folafon. Built in Norway in 1938, the Folafon was a large steel roll-on rolloff car ferry. After years of service, she had wound up in Southoun. Bought by a scrap metal dealer who was now struggling to dismantle her. He was delighted when the club took her off his hands. In 1981, she arrived in Ith and was guided into her moorings by Fred Fing. I was rode out to the tug that was towing the folafon and I was taken up to the bridge on the tug and uh bloody David said, "This is Fred. He's in the merchant nav. He's on navigating office. He'll put you in. So I put the ship in and put the folder fun into the same hole as the cast lift. The ever resourceful members renovated her. And with the more spacious and comfortable clubhouse, the club entered a new era. By the 80s, we'd started to get girls in and women. The girls wanted a bit something a bit better. We had nice toilets on board. Uh we had running water. We had good power. As always, was a social and friendly yacht club. This is a sailing club. Sailing basics for most of the people here is about racing. And they have a race, they come back and have a social. We used to have sort of a dance or a barbecue. The food, well, it was it was all cooked either open fires or a primer stove. It was good fun. And then we started a Christmas party. We'd have turkey with a whole everything, the whole lot, including Christmas pudding. But Folafon had its own quirks and idiosyncrasies. There was one year I was the MC and it was raining hard, blowing like a stink and everything I touch like the microphone or a flood light or anything like that, I got a belt, got a shot. But that was part of the fun of Yorkshire. The 80s was a lovely time. The '9s was equally good. There's always been there's never been a massive change except I would think costs started to go up. People were more responsible at work. They had to work long long long long hours. The nucleus was there. The new the little nucleus is still there. They didn't go away, but we didn't seem to be expanding so much. We still had the Christmas lunches, a little bit more sophisticated. We had plates. We got proper knives and forks which was absolutely amazing. We got a cooker in the 90s, a real electric cooker. In fact, somebody bought a microwave. In 2000, the club celebrated its 100th birthday. Both town and the club had changed in many ways over the years, but the club's spirit endured. And I joined Yach Club in 2000. My first experience of yacht club was coming here on a Sunday which happened to be a work Sunday and there were lots of members around all getting stuck in on the callsway doing things. Membership of the club is composed of practical people in the main. There are people who are former engineers, uh, barge builders, uh, experienced sailors, people who've worked on the river for most of their life. A whole range of people who are always available to help out and give guidance where required or where asked. It's always been the sort of club where everything's been handon. They're the the best people in the world at because they all help each other. We've never been a very rich club and and that's how it progressed. Although Folafon had become the heart of the club after more than 20 years service, she was starting to decline. Since the repairs she needed would have been extremely expensive, the members once again began to consider a new clubhouse. The club was given the opportunity to apply for funding for a new clubhouse with the assistance of the London Burough of Beexley. The funding required that the clubhouse should be self- sustaining, that it should provide more training opportunities, and that it should welcome a wider section of the community to the sport of sailing. And controversially, for some of the older members, it would be a concrete building rather than another floating clubhouse. We wanted another floating boat. There was no doubt about it. We didn't want to come ashore. We were very happy to stay afloat. um and we looked for alternative means. The trouble was there was no alternative means easily available. As the building came to completion in early 2010, the members said a sad farewell to their beloved clubship. Folafon made its final voyage back to Norway where she can now be seen in the Hardang Museum and Conservation Center. Now for the first time since they left the Corinthian Yacht Club building in 1920s, Yacht Club is based on shore. Unlike the Garsson and the Folafon, the clubhouse is safe, clean, modern and spacious. We got better showers facilities. We got better facilities all around as which is required in modernday life. All of these improvements are helping attract a new, younger membership. I looked around the last few months and we've got an awful lot of new new members. The club has been revitalized. There's more people on the water now than there were 5 years ago. On a Sunday there's there are youth sailors and they're sailing and they're using the clubhouse. They come and go and they're our seed call for the future. Since the 1950s, Ith has been developing its training side. And now the new clubhouse is enabling the club to nurture the skills of even more prospective sailors. For the first time, employed a club coach. So in this past year, for example, we've run training courses for children and adults on weekdays and at weekends. And we've also encouraged local schools and youth groups to come down and experience sailing for the first time. But all of these profound changes to Irid Yacht Club have greatly affected some of the older members. A lot of the old members miss Vogafon, I must say, like myself. Greatly affected. There's nothing like wandering around wallowing in mud, painting boats. Some of the members have found a way to make the new development feel like past clubouses by congregating in the boat shed. The heart of the cup, you could say, now has moved from the ship to one of the sheds where all the old people go and have drink and have tea. Like we have the closeness on the club ships. They feel that closeness if they make their own little kind of corner in a shed and while away the time by putting the world to rights. But the new clubhouse where you actually have to respect it because it's new. So, majority of people that don't like that sort of thing or don't want to they want to come down and work but don't want to have to change their shoes every time they go to the clubhouse use the workshop. We have a table in here and chairs and and a cooker and we've got a microwave. So, we're self-contained. So, the joke of the club is we've got the shed committee and the club committee. As the club has developed, so has the range of club boats. There are a number of club boats here. We've got modern laser kills, Omegas, Stratos Kes, and we've also got our fleet of Hamble Stars. There may have been many recent changes, but when it comes to the Hamble Stars, I has retained its rich heritage. I realized what a debt I owed the club and its foresight in in in keeping a class going like that, but also in allocating to young people. I didn't have to pay. The boat was just there whenever I came down. People bought new equipment for it and I didn't think about that at the time. Robbiey's little boys are all too aware of the fantastic opportunity that I Yacht Club gave them. I've been a national champion in in various classes and and sailed for Britain in international regatas. Those early lessons that we learned in sailing uh have been useful for me right the way through life and I'm still using them now. Since it was inaugurated in 1900, Year of the Yacht Club has grown, evolved, and adapted to its times. I've got grandchildren and all I can hope for is that they get as much fun out of this new club that I've had out the old one, which it really, really has been good. On the surface, the current club may be unrecognizable from the yacht club of the past, but some things have never left it. The dedication, camaraderie, and enthusiasm of its members. Let's hope they endure for many years to come. Natal.

This means maintenance could be tens of millions per year. A lot of these electronics, they get fouled up if they sit dormant for a long time.
