That keeps your turbos and your after cooler from getting gummed up, and it also helps reduce oil consumption and reduce air pollution by further combustion inside the engine. This part probably costs $60, $70 to replace, but it has immeasurable value in prolonging the longevity of your motors. Maintaining the engines on a yacht like Snowbird typically costs around $60,000 a year, and you're looking at parts, time, and the materials involved to change the oils and keep everything up and going. - [Presenter] That's just below deck. Superyacht owners also need to invest in keeping the rest of the yacht in pristine condition, like the teak flooring. Teak is typically used on yachts because it's a weather resistant luxury material, but it's also challenging to maintain. - [Josh] When we scrub the teak, oftentimes it only lasts roughly a week or two depending on the traffic on board. It wants to go back to its natural color, and, you know, between rain and rinsing it down, you kinda wash away all of that, you know, hard work that you've done. Annually the teak flooring costs about $18,000 to maintain. - [Presenter] Adding on smaller expenses, you're already looking at just over $1 million. But by far, there's one cost for maintaining superyachts that completely surpasses the others, the crew. The estimated budget for Snowbird's crew is over $800,000 per year and that's because super yachts must always have a crew even when docked. - You have to take care of the teak the paint, wash windows, machinery. Rust never sleeps, maintenance never stops. - [Presenter] Cost towards their health insurance, travel expenses, uniforms, and cell phone bills can add thousands to the budget. Adding in crew expenses brings the annual operating cost estimate to nearly 1.9 million a year, but that's only when there are no major repairs or maintenance while owners generally spend around 10% a year in maintenance costs for new superyachts. - [James] It's kind of a guideline, but as she gets three years or five years, or hits her 10 year, it's gonna be more than 10%. - [Presenter] Every five years or so, superyacht owners have to shell out for one of their largest expenses. - Paint jobs are sometimes from a hundred thousand to a million bucks, depending on your size of your boat. Paint is about $1,800 an inch, - [Presenter] Salt in the water and air corrodes the yachts paint. To repaint the entire yacht, it needs to be hauled out of the water, then it can take months to complete a fresh paint job. There are also other major potential expenses. Some yacht owners need to spend hundreds of thousands of dollars every few years on detailed inspections. And if a yacht fails in inspection, repairing or replacing it could run $200,000 or more. - Certain boats, you can't get the engines out. You have to cut a hole in the side and then reweld it, recertify the hole. Now you're into the multi-millions to change out an engine. - [Presenter] To recoup some of these costs, superyacht owners will rent out their vessels. The charter fee for this super yacht ranges from $125 to $145,000 per week, that covers the boat and the crew, but renters also need to pay for additional expenses like fuel, food, and customs fees. Plus, there's a customary tip of 18 to 25% for the crew members. At best, superyacht owners might break-even on maintenance costs with income earned from chartering. - I don't think it's a profitable situation. At best, they're covering their salaries and the boat's way as it travels. Wear and tear is always there and the money would have to go back into the vessel. Ok.... technically..... very technically....what was said was true.  Boats are expensive to maintain. 10% is accurate for ships under 10yo. However, the absurdly ridiculous numbers just thrown out there for the wow factor are for 300ft and above yachts. 
No, it doesn't take millions of dollars to fill up an 80ft boat. No, a 120ft boat doesn't have a crew of 50 who each make half a million a month. Hate to tell you but an inch of paint isn't $1800....for any boat anywhere. Bottom paint can run $1800 per gallon...not inch. That guy was a lying clown. It doesn't take a gallon of copper paint to cover an inch. 

The only way it costs $18000 per month to wash the teak deck is of the crew is using pick axes. Teak decks are washed each day with water...not champagne. They use special soap, not liquid gold. They use mops, not fur coats. $18000 per month is a bald faced lie. 

Again, boats are expensive. The bigger the more expense...just like a house. However, this is typical journalism....if you don't know... just make it up. Journalism is just story telling. This was a story...a false story

If you want one like this, which has got pretty much every option, the bigger engine, Seakeeper, air conditioning, passerelle, platforms, the lot, you're looking at about two and a half million pounds, excluding VAT. However, if you want more in depth pricing information, go and read my in depth written review. We have a value for money section where I talk all about specs, costs, and also tell you which options I think you should go for and one of those options is the largest engines. There were three engine options that were on the 62 as well, and everybody picked the biggest ones. And that's what this boat has got. And that means IPS 1350 with 1,000 horsepower per side, which really suits this style of boat very nicely indeed. The 62 topped out at 31 knots. This is more like 33 so you're getting a little bit more of the top end. Not that that really matters. What matters is obviously the cruising. And really you're looking to cruise at about the same pace, 22 to 25 knots, which is going to give you around 260 nautical miles in terms of fast cruising range. Of course, if you want to drift along at slower speeds, you can boost that range. So you have got a Seakeeper, you've got a gyroscopic stabilizer, it's not fins, so when you're moving along, you're losing a little bit of that effect. But this will be perfectly happy pottering along at 12 knots on a nice warm day. We're now at 24 knots, and it's very, very happy at this speed. Pearl said they weren't very happy with the steering on this. This is hull number one. They've got to work with Volvo to try and improve it. I think it feels great. I've got the trim assist on, the sea keeper still on, despite that, still reacts really nicely to the helm. Steering's lovely and light, feels agile, feels alive, doesn't feel too ponderous. I think if you remove trim assist and turn the Seakeeper off, it just sharpens up the turning circle a bit. But really for me, if I'm using this as an owner, I'm just leaving everything on and letting the boat settle in. It feels absolutely lovely, really nice driving environment here as well, breezy. Obviously, the windscreen is pretty shallow. They can dearly do with a perspex popup, like you see on Absolute, Sunseeker do it as well sometimes, to deflect the wind over my head, but I really like the position. They've dropped the throttles nice and low so I can slide the seat forward. I'm close to everything. The MFD's are nice and upright. And see those really easily. Now they say they're going to change this, but I really like this sort of mushroom color finish. It doesn't show all the hairline scratches that black GRP can do. I think that looks lovely. Clean, smart, nice amount of storage cup holders. View's great, obviously, and the seats are really comfortable. I've got a little foot rest too. So even when I lean back, I feel in touch with everything. It's really nice. It does take the edge off a bit to help with the wind noise. Pearl have built plenty on this platform, as I said during the tour, 25 62s so they know this formula works. They know IPS works, and it feels like a boat that has been poured over and over plenty of time. It all hangs together really well. From an engineering perspective, performance perspective, easy boat to drive at speed. It just feels familiar, comfortable, easy, no complaints really, not particularly exciting to throw around, not really in the brief for a boat like this. A Princess F58 is probably a bit more fun to handle, but for what this boat is used for, spot on up here. It's a bit chilly, into the wind. Thankfully we've got a lower helm, so let's head down there and have a go. Okay, let's get up to speed down here, shall we? Ease her up and though obviously you are more aware of the engines down here, mainly because there's zero wind noise. Their sound is still pretty well contained. You know, you come to expect that from IPS. Generally quite quiet setup, engines are set long way back in the hull. Obviously, there's plenty of insulation down there as well. We're gonna go into the engine room after I drive it, but you'll see when we get down there. But yeah, I mean, it's a nice, refined driving experience down here. Quite an interesting design on Pearls. There's a few things they do differently. Obviously, I'm in board here. You might expect the helm to be on that side, but I'm more on the center line, which is quite nice. It reduces the blind spot there. There is a big mullion down the middle of this huge glass area. But it's not too bad, because I'm further in board, and they've got that little quarter light. I'm aware of stuff coming at me from the starboard side, but the main thing is the way the windscreen stretches up and over my head. Typical piece of design from Pearl, and I think it works brilliantly. It just makes this area feel so much brighter, bigger. You don't feel like you're stuck inside when you're driving from down here. You're still in touch with the elements, loads of light, crucially, blinds as well. Though it can get pretty hot and be quite a lot of glare around, but you can quickly just put those down, and then, you know, it feels like a sort of normal arrangement down here. There's not too much sunlight glaring down on you, if you don't want there to be. The arrangement ergonomically isn't quite as good as upstairs. Everything 's just a little bit further away, but I do like this position. They've put the wheel out a long way, so I can generally sit back, again, the seat slides. And this is a really nice, comfortable position with a great view as well. I have got the trim assist on, so that's just taking care of the the trim. But the boat has a nice running attitude naturally. So I'm not having to crane to look over the bow, and again, we're at that 22 knot speed and it feels really happy here. The screens are a bit further away than upstairs, but again, they're nice and upright, so I can see them really, really clearly. Everything's just a bit more of a stretch down here. But I don't really mind that too much, because it feels like you know you're down here. This is where you are going to be doing those longer passages. Maybe at night, you're going to be sat back using the instruments, possibly cruising along a bit more slowly as well. And for that, it's really comfortable. You're joined by a navigator. There's space between for people to get in and out without disturbing each other, which is great. I also got into, you know, these split control systems, these old school mechanical glen denning split control systems. A lot of people don't know how to run these. And a lot of people are very scared of them. And to be quite honest, you should be a little scared of them if you don't know what you're doing. This is the first thing I ever learned on was the split control Glen Denning mechanical system. All this electronic fancy new stuff is really nice and makes things a lot easier. But, uh, you have your shifters here and you have your throttle here. I was explaining to him the importance of touching the throttles before the shifters when you want to stop or when you're docking. You have to make sure that if you come off plane right now, you got to pull the throttles back all the way and then shift to neutral. You cannot shift to neutral before you pull your throttles back because the RPMs will shoot so far up you're risking engine damage or transmission damage. You especially can't have the throttles up to whatever RPM, 1500,000, doesn't matter, and then shift into gear. If you do that, you're definitely risking blowing up a transmission. It will engage and clunk extremely hard. It's like revving your car up to 4,000 RPMs with automatic transmission and putting it in and putting it in drive. not good for it at all. When you're docking, you have to make sure you're throttle back before you shift. So, if you were docking and you put starboard and forward, you need a little throttle. All right? Starboard forward, starboard, throttle. You have to make sure to pull that throttle back before you shift back to neutral or reverse. Okay? That really throws a lot of people out.   14. @DavidJaxon-v2l: I see lots of people commenting on the Capt. barking orders. - Ghost Ship: The Mysterious Disappearance of the Crew and a Grim Conclusion: Kind: captions Language: en on the afternoon of September 23rd 2007 the Miami Coast Guard responded to a report from Jeff Brenham owner of the fishing vessel Joe Co the boat had not returned from a trip to bimin the closest island in the Bahamas located approximately 25 kilm East of Miami in principle there was nothing unusual about the boat not returning on time passengers might get carried away with fishing or have a bit too much to drink and need to sleep it off often asking the captain to stay out a little longer which naturally leads to delays Jeff brandham reported that six people were on board four crew members and two passengers it was an experienced team typically helping tourists catch big fish particularly tuna however the passengers this time were somewhat unusual the two men were not interested in fishing at all they had simply booked a one-way trip to bimin the captain was somewhat puzzled and directly pointed out that a short flight would cost them much less why book an expensive ocean trip instead it turned out that the men could not fly because one of their girls girlfriends had accidentally packed his passport in her luggage the girlfriends were now waiting for them on bimin in another boat the men agreed to pay $4,000 for the 2.5h hour Journey the captain was still unsure whether to take the job but his uncle and co-owner of the boat Jeff Bram convinced him to accept it was an international trip which meant the company could later advertise trips to the Bahamas in its service offerings additionally Jake and the crew could enjoy the opportunity to fish on their way back on Saturday September 22nd 2007 the Joe Cool set off from the dock another very important trip was scheduled for the following day the boat needed to be prepared in advance so it was crucial for the crew to return on time however the next morning when Jeff brandham expected to find the boat at the dock it was nowhere to be seen it was highly unlikely that the captain had intentionally extended the boat stay at Sea knowing that other clients were waiting Jeff called the port in the Bahamas and was horrified to learn that the boat had never arrived there since no issues on board had been reported he found this extremely strange and contacted the Coast Guard the Coast Guard began their search and to better understand what might have happened on the boat they investigated the crew members all four crew members Jake Branham Kelly Branham Scott Gamble and Samuel Samy kery were residents of South Florida Jake and Kelly brandham were in their early 20s a married couple raising two children their eldest daughter was 3 years old and their youngest son was just 4 months old Jake had dreamed from a young age of becoming the captain of his own boat running Fishing Charters and tourist trips luckily for him his family was very wealthy and his grandfather provided the funds to purchase his first boat and set up the business Jake acquired a 14m sport fishing vessel designed to handle rough Seas the boat was equipped with everything needed for tuna fishing making it a top-notch vessel perfect for catching big fish and hosting tourist trips Jake's Uncle Jeff brandham was listed as co-owner of the boat while Jake's cousin assisted with administrative work Jake's older brother Brother Scott Gamble and a close family friend Samuel Samy Carrey joined the crew by 2007 after just a few months in operation the business was thriving attracting many clients thanks to Jake's expertise in finding the best fishing spots Kelly Jake's wife didn't always accompany the crew on trips however this short Journey seemed too appealing to pass up so Jake and Kelly asked Jake's grandfather to babysit their kids at the scheduled time the couple boarded the boat along with the rest of the crew and their passengers and they set off on their Voyage the Coast Guard disseminated all critical information about the Joe Cool to nearby vessels including a description of the boat the number of people on board and the purpose of its trip Coast Guard Patrol boats scoured the route The Vessel was supposed to have taken but there was no sign of it they had no idea what might have happened to the boat other Sailors Friends of the missing crew joined the search effort while the families of the crew members were undoubtedly worried there was hope that everything was fine since Jake Samuel and Scott were highly experienced seamen however when the Joe Cool wasn't quickly located an official search and rescue operation was launched helicopters took to the skies and a plane flying the route between Miami and bimin was also enlisted in the search the likelihood of finding the Joe Cool was high as the area was frequently patrolled due to its reputation as a corridor for illegal activities such as the smuggling of migrants particularly from Cuba and Contraband predictably it wasn't long long before Sailors aboard a coast guard vessel spotted a fishing boat however all attempts by officers to contact the vessel went unanswered as the ship Drew closer the crew saw the name on the boat Joe cool it was indeed Jeff bram's missing vessel but the circumstances surrounding the boat baffled Rescuers the Joe Cool was drifting nearly 50 km north of Cuba about 225 km off course for a sport fishing vessel there was no need to venture that far expending unnecessary fuel to find fish the mystery deepened when they noticed the boat's 23m anchor had been lowered even though the water was over 60 M deep the boat couldn't have drifted so close to Cuba on its own this was perplexing especially considering its original course the route from Miami to bimy crosses the Gulf Stream which Flows northward at a speed of 81 km per hour if the boat had encountered mechanical issues the current would have quickly carried it North for the Joe Cool to end up so far south near Cuba someone would have had to deliberately steer it against the current so someone had brought the boat to this location and now no one on board was responding to the Coast Guard's calls to establish contact in such a situation one could imagine anything Pirates might have been hiding in the cabin for instance proceeding with great caution the officers boarded The Vessel but the boat was in fact empty however something had clearly happened the chaos that rained everywhere and the presence of blood told its own story wallets car keys and other personal belongings were still there young man still lived with his parents he didn't drink didn't use drugs and had never had any issues with the law guo was known to be polite social and considerate he worked as a security guard but All along the West Coast, from fishing charters out of Ventura to whale watching off the Olympic Peninsula in Washington, my observation is that these vessels often focus too much on guest comfort and too little(if any) on boating safety.  Skippers tend to take more of a restauranteurs approach. Life boats are covered with tarps, lashed up on the wheelhouse. Life jackets are stowed under banquette benches in salon-like cabins. There’s not one word uttered about how safety protocols should be handled. No pre-trip drill to a muster station. Nothing. I’ve been on fishing charters where 15 guys were drunk by 1pm, paying $7 a can for beers supplied by the vessel. On whale watching boats where half the guests were older than 70. Often bringing along relatives in their late 80’s or even 90’s and wheelchair bound. How is a panicked floating restaurant of seniors supposed to safely debark into life rafts from a wheelchair? - Ferretti 580 Yacht Tour & Review | YachtBuyer: Kind: captions Language: en-GB Jack Haines: There's been a flood of new 55 to 60 foot Flybridge boats onto the market, and this is Ferretti muscling in on the action. With the brand new 580, the second smallest boat in its Flybridge range, and it goes up against the likes of the Princess F55, the Absolute 56 Fly and the Galeon 560 Fly. With a pair of Volvo Penta D13, 1,000 horsepower engines it will top out about 32 knots, cruise at 27-28. So let's see what this brand new Ferretti brings to what is quite a congested part of the market. I'm Jack Haines, welcome to YachtBuyer. At the transom there is a hydraulic bathing platform, with built in steps on either side and a useful boot like hatch to stow watersports equipment. There is access up each side of the transom bench, and there's a pullout deck shower mounted inside a hatch on the starboard side. Covered stone gear is a typical Ferretti trait, and that includes a cleat, winch and drained bin for dropping loose rope tails into. The cockpit features a high-low table with opening leaves, which drops down and opens out to extend that sunbathing space on the aft deck. The backrest also moves forward, to create a bench seat when the table is being used as a dining space. On the port side, there is a third docking station, which includes throttles and controls for the thrusters. The side decks are wide and well protected by decent guardrails on their outer edge. Though a rail on the superstructure would add an extra layer of security when moving forward. The NAV lights as stylish as they are, a mounted in a place where it's all too easy to catch a shoulder. The foredeck is a great lounging area, and features a broad day bed opposite a sunpad with adjustable headrest that can move to either create a bench seat, or extended sunbathing space. Practical detailing is good, and includes teak cupholders, and useful teak top storage bins on either side. Perfect for stowing phones, suncream and other loose items. Forward, the anchor locker hatches pop open and gas struts, to reveal deep segregated voids for separating fenders and lines from the anchor chain. Up to the Flybridge then, and the first thing to point out is that you can have a hardtop with the 580. Clearly this boat hasn't got it, if you don't have it, you have an electric bimini, that pops up and gives you a nice amount of shade over this area. In terms of the layout out here, it's quite simple, really. You have all your seating around the aft end here, you have a nice big table, similar style to downstairs really. Support pops out there, and then over it comes and there you go, you've got really nice big dining space there. These tables could do with a finger hold though, because they're very heavy, and you trap your fingers when you close them. Forward of that, wet bar. Grill is an option. Clearly this owner hasn't specified it. But you can have a nice big grill there, that clears away into the sink, and then underneath you've got your usual array of cooling space, icemaker that sort of thing. Forward then to starboard, is really just sunbathing space, You've got this extension here, that's actually an option just to give you a little bit more space to stretch out. And then a lovely big swathe of sunpad there with a windscreen right forward. Which means, it probably won't offer much protection at the helm. But the design of this helm, means that actually you've got a bit of protection from the pod on top of the dash, and I really, really like this driving position. Clever to have the cupholders down here. It's small, it's neat, everything is nice and close. The view forward even when I'm sitting here is very good, but I've got a bolster option if I want it. And if you want to moor the boat from up here, okay, the hatches are on the starboard side so you can't see the bathing platform. But the all round view, the view down the side is very good indeed. As I said, two engine options here, Volvo Penta D13 1,000 or 900 horsepower, top speed of around 32 knots. And of course the option of the Xenta joystick, which combines the propellers and the thrusters to give you IPS style joystick control on a shaft drive boat. Let's head inside. Ferretti was one of the first to fit a top hinged galley window, but it's opted for a sliding mechanism on the 580. Which has the same effect of opening the galley up to the cockpit. A bar top slides out of the counter to complete the transformation, and creates a flat deck to line up drinks and nibbles for those in the cockpit. The galley is a practical shape, and has high countertops, allowing you to easily wedge yourself in if you're using the space when the boat is on the move. The fridge freezer is on the opposite side of the saloon to allow space for full height units. There are plenty of smart storage solutions to, including dedicated fiddled storage for the boats cutlery, and dinnerware. There are classic and contemporary interior decor options. The showboat being classic, and the dinette is arranged opposite this couples bench with pop up TV being mounted behind. If you want a larger screen, then you can lose the sofa, and have a bigger albeit fixed TV. At the lower helm, much like on the Flybridge, the second helm seat is an option. But, given that there aren't many forward facing seats in the saloon, it's one worth ticking. There isn't a side door, but there is at least a manual window for ventilation and communicating with crew. The 3D printed pod is home to twin MFDs, which display most of the vital information and can be controlled via a touchscreen or a separate control pad. Now let's head downstairs, and have a look at the accommodation. There are a couple of layout options down here, all with three cabins but you can have two or three bathrooms. This particular boat is the two bathroom version, which means here on the starboard side you have a twin guest cabin, which is a decent size, you've got a good amount of headroom in there. It's got a bit of hull window and opening porthole, and full height hanging wardrobe. But if we quickly head into owners cabin, this is a really key standout area of this boat. Really quite a special cabin in this sector. I love this mezzanine design, where you've got your little bureau down here, your office, a little bit of storage under here and obviously a mirror, but it's lovely because it sits overlooking the rest of the cabin. Then you've got a run of steps down into the cabin itself, where you've still got really excellent headroom. There's a bit of a step up around the bed on that side. But you can see there's also a cutout in the ceiling, so headrooms good even over there. You've got storage running along that counter there, and a big hanging wardrobe over there too, and the television is built into the mirror on this bulkhead, which is quite a nice feature. Yeah, the beds a really good size. You've got nice bedside tables. It's a really lovely space, and of course it also has a private ensuite. Now if you go for the three bathroom version, you lose this area, so for me I think this is the one to have. Let's go look at the VIP cabin now. The VIP cabin then is right forward on the way, you've got this storage here dedicated to the washer dryer. This particular owner has gone for a combo one, but you have got space to have washer above, dryer below or vice versa if you want, but this boats got the combined one and a bit more storage which is quite useful. Rightforward then we have the VIP. Again, storage is really good. You've got a nice big hanging locker there on the port side, more storage dotted around at eye level, repeater switches for the lighting and the air conditioning, and reading lights up there. A nice little shelf with a fiddle on it so you can leave your phone down there, things in the day and they're not going to slide off and end up on the floor. Again, a bit of hull window, natural ventilation both sides, you haven't got an actual skylight, but you have got an escape hatch here. And this cabin, because it is the two bathroom version. This is the day head, as well as the private ensuite for this cabin. It's a good size, but it is of course shared with twin cabin aft. The only complaint I've got, is that there isn't a huge amount of space at the end of the bed here to get changed. And because the door opens in again, that gobbles up a fair bit of space. Right that's the accommodation for guests then, let's head aft and look at the crew space and engine room. Crew Space on the 580 is optional. If you don't have it, you just have a big storage void. It isn't huge down here. The space over the bed is actually okay, but once you have come down, this is about the only place you can stand, apart from the bathroom of course, well it's a wet room. So you have a shower, toilet and sink in there. Now this sort of boat is on the cusp of being owner run or crewed. If crew space is really a big part of what you want for your boat, then you're probably better looking at something like the Absolute 56 Fly. Engine access then is through a nice big hatch here in the cockpit. You've got a teak ladder, that brings you down here, right between the engines and away from the hatch. You have to stoop if you're my sort of heights, but here you can have a nice look around and obviously it brings a lot of natural light in, as well as the artificial light. The arrangement itself is very, very tidy. It's a little bit tight to get around the outside of the engines, but getting between them, getting around the back really isn't too bad at all. And then some nice detailing down here as well, like you've got fresh water wash down in here, if there's a spillage and you have a hose inside here, it can help you clean it all out. And the seakeeper if it's optioned on the boat, is neatly mounted down here behind the ladder, and the generator is tucked in the aft corner there. It's an impressive space. There you have it, that's our tour of the Ferretti 580. - Yacht Running Costs Revealed: From 42ft Pirelli to 88ft Ferretti Superyacht.: Kind: captions Language: en [music] >> You are buying a yacht for a million dollars. In the world of yachting, a simple rule has been circulating for decades. Annual maintenance costs 10% of the yacht's purchase price. 2 million for the yacht, 200,000 a year in upkeep. Simple. The problem is this rule ignores almost everything. Where you sail, how you use her, who lays her up for the winter, and what flag she flies. Operating costs, [music] opex in industry terms, form a complex system [music] of communicating vessels. Insurance, crew salaries, authorized servicing, yard work, hull protection, communications, and [music] administration. And each of these categories shifts dramatically depending on one factor. The demanding waters of Biscayne [music] Bay. Astronomical insurance premiums. The French Riviera. Chronic berth shortages. But a mild climate and lower risk. There is one more line item that no one likes to think about. The hurricane plan in Florida or winter layup in Europe. Emergency haul out ahead of storm season, $1,500 in Miami. Winter layup on the French Riviera, 12,200 [music] euros. Pirelli 42 annual cost summary. Miami, $43,710 [music] per year. French Riviera, 27,250 euros per year. What does this mean relative to the yacht's value? Just 4 to 5%. Even after accounting for hidden costs, satellite communications, registration, and detailing, we are still well short of the 10% myth. The Pirelli 42 is [music] a perfectly optimized machine. Outboard propulsion and the absence of a permanent crew keep the budget predictable [music] and manageable. Azimut 53, 16.7 m of Italian elegance for the owner who wants to skipper her himself. The defining feature, the Volvo Penta IPS 950. Integrated drive pods extending from the hull bottom, twin counter-rotating propellers, a helm-mounted joystick. Maneuvering without a professional captain becomes entirely intuitive, but that convenience comes with a hidden technical [music] cost. One that many yacht owners overlook entirely at the point of [music] purchase. So, before we get to the cost breakdown, we need to [music] talk about the IPS drive pod. The IPS drive pod is an exceptionally complex mechatronic device permanently submerged in corrosive seawater. Its greatest enemy is water ingress into the drive [music] unit. If water ingress damages the bearings, rebuilding a single unit costs between 25,000 and 40,000 [music] A new pod, 100,000 dollars. This is why oil analysis is not an option. It is a necessity. It detects microscopic traces of metal particles or water contamination before catastrophic failure occurs. The manufacturer specified synthetic oil for the IPS system >> costs 106 dollars per gallon. And each drive unit requires 3.5 gallons. [music] Annual servicing of two IPS units at an authorized service center 4,500 dollars in Miami, 4,000 euros in France. The owner skipper model can also factor in hiring a captain on a contract basis. 21 days a year for those who prefer to have someone handle navigation, itinerary planning, and day-to-day operations during their time on [music] board. In Miami, the daily rate for a certified captain with a deckhand is 650 dollars. Over 21 days, 13,650 On the French Riviera, 450 euros per day, amounting to 9,450 euros for the full season. Another hidden line item, detailing for a vessel without a [music] permanent crew. The owner skipper rarely has the time [music] to detail a 16 meter yacht on a weekly basis. Professional detailing runs $6,000 per year in Miami and 5,000 [music] euros in Europe. Winterization and hurricane procedures, $2,500 in the USA, 2,000 euros in [music] France. Insurance for a vessel valued at 1.5 to 2 million dollars >> without permanent crew in Florida, $30,000 per year. 18,900 euros on the French Riviera. Azimut 53, $82,340 [music] in Miami. 61,950 euros on the French Riviera. Without the contract captain, however, costs drop by $13,000 in Miami and [music] 10,000 euros in France. Still below 10%. But, this is where the trap lies. Saving a thousand dollars by delaying an oil change on the IPS drive pod could leave you with a bill of $50,000. At this level of yacht, preventive maintenance is the absolute cornerstone of sound financial management. Skimping on service is not saving. It is a loan on deeply unfavorable terms. Princess F65 19.8 meters, powered by twin MAN V8 engines, each producing 1,200 horsepower. This is a critical threshold in yacht ownership and management. With a vessel of this size, the owner could technically helm her themselves. But in practice, [music] having a captain on board is far more practical. And this is where an interesting economic [music] dilemma arises. Employ him year-round or only for the four summer months? Many yacht owners opt for the seasonal model looking to reduce costs. As you'll see in a moment, those savings are largely illusory. Professional captains prefer year-round contracts that provide stability and continuity. A four-month contract means either paying a premium to compensate for the lack of continuity or taking [music] the risk of bringing on a less qualified candidate. In the American market, a seasonal captain represents a true cost of $40,000 to $50,000 inclusive of all hidden expenses. In the Mediterranean, a four-month contract including benefits runs 26,000 euros. And the greatest hidden cost of the absence of year-round supervision, for eight months, the vessel lies unattended. No pump is [music] inspected. No filter is serviced. Pumps seize. Mold takes [music] hold in the air conditioning ducts. Deep battery discharge. Galvanic corrosion from shore power. These are the surprises waiting for you when you return. The answer to the absence of a permanent crew is engaging a professional management company that conducts regular mooring checks, ventilates the interior, responds to alarms, and carries out systems inspections. For this plan, plus hurricane procedures or winter lay up on land, a total of $5,000 in Miami 4,000 euros in France. The Princess F65 approaching the 20-m threshold triggers an entirely different pricing structure in European marinas. Cannes and Antibes securing a berth here means committing to long-term contracts [music] that guarantee a place in the port. Annual berth fees from 25,000 to 55,000 euros. For the first time, >> the cost of a berth in Europe exceeds that of Florida. MAN V8 1200 engines [music] demand rigorous servicing. Annual service with a certified MAN technician plus Onan generator service $5,500 in Miami 5,000 euros in Europe. Haul out and yard work for a 40-ton vessel $5,000 in the USA, 4,000 euros in France. [music] At this level of vessel, owners are already upgrading to Starlink Maritime over Starlink Rome >> ensuring priority bandwidth during offshore passages. Annual cost $3,000 in the USA, >> 2,800 euros in Europe. Safety equipment certification two multi-person life rafts, EPIRBs, engine room fire suppression systems. Annual inspections $2,000 in Miami, 1,800 euros in France. Pre-season detailing after winter lay-up, a professional crew must restore the finish on a 20-m vessel. Full exterior and interior detailing $9,000 in Florida 7,500 euros in Europe. Princess F65, 151,800 116,100 on the French Riviera. Managing a vessel in a semi-professional capacity yields no meaningful economies of scale. By cutting [music] costs on a year-round captain's contract, you are incurring technical [music] debt. The budget is not shrinking. It is simply shifting towards higher risk insurance premiums, professional management companies, and costly detailing after every winter layup. The bill >> will still come due. Ferretti 860, 26.9 m, approaching 100 GT, formally and legally classified as a superyacht. Managing her more closely resembles running a small logistics operation than owning a recreational yacht. The private ownership model for the Ferretti 860 requires a minimum of two crew members year-round. A certified captain and a deckhand. During the 4-month summer season, a professional stewardess joins the crew. And it is precisely this commitment to a full crew that radically redefines the yacht's operating budget. Technical servicing, twin MAN engines producing 1,200 horsepower each. Seakeeper gyro stabilizer, virtually eliminating roll. Idromar watermaker, twin Cummins Onan generators rated at 27 kW each. Annual servicing across all onboard systems, $12,000. But what truly defines a superyacht budget, it is not the engines, it is the people. In the American market, a captain commands between 120,000 and 160,000 dollars per year. >> A first officer and engineer from 48,000 to 60,000 dollars. A stewardess for 4 months, close to 20,000 dollars. Total crew costs including benefits and logistics, 240,000 dollars in the USA, 160,000 euros in Europe. And here the paradox emerges. A permanent qualified crew carries out daily safety rounds, checks the bilge pumps, and tests the fire suppression systems. This is a key argument used by underwriters when setting insurance rates. Eliminating the crew on a superyacht would increase the risk of damage to such a degree that insurance premiums would consume a significant portion of any savings generated by that decision. At this scale, the era of straightforward Polish flag registration comes to an end. The Ferretti 860 falls under commercial registry requirements, or calls for prestigious private registries. The Cayman Islands, BVI, the Marshall Islands, or Malta. Watch the episode where I break down every flag option and the advantages each one offers. Tonnage registration and annual administrative fees for a prestigious flag range from 8,000 to as much as 15,000 dollars per year. Priority internet for crew and guests, Starlink maritime or business with a large data package. From 250 to 1,000 dollars per month. 6,000 dollars per year in the USA, €5,500 in Europe. In addition, the annual inspection of MED certified life rafts, EPIRB calibration, and certified servicing of the FM-200 fire suppression system come to $2,400 in Miami and €3,500 in France. Professional detailing of a vessel of this size is a task for specialized yacht teams. Budget for a full exterior wax and finish restoration, $20,000 in the USA, €10,000 in France. Add to this a hurricane plan or winter layup in a secure facility. $10,000 in the USA, €8,000 in Europe. Haul out for a vessel over 100 GT is an industrial scale operation. Travel lift with a minimum capacity of 80 tons. Pressure washing, corrosion treatment, ablative antifouling coatings, and replacement of multiple large zinc anodes. In Miami, $12,000. Yards on the Côte d'Azur, €10,000. Ferretti 860, $511,000 annually in Florida, €361,800 on the French Riviera. And only now, with the largest yacht, are we approaching the mythical 10%. In fact, we are halfway there. 6.5% to 7.5% in Florida, 5% to 5.3% in Europe. The Ferretti 860 proves something fundamental. A superyacht is driven by people, not propellers. Crew costs and insurance coverage alone account for over 60% of the entire operating budget. Time for synthesis. The numbers across all four vessels are unambiguous. The 10% rule does not apply in any of the cases analyzed. But more importantly, I've identified three distinct patterns. First pattern, the bottom of the cost curve. The Pirelli 42 with outboard propulsion and no permanent crew. 4 to 5% of the vessel's value. The absence of a captain's salary combined with straightforward onboard systems minimizes all cost variables. This is absolute economic rationality. Second pattern, the mid-range trap. Azimut 53 and Princess F65. 4.5 to 6% of the vessel's value. Owners attempt to reduce costs by foregoing a year-round crew. The budget, however, swells rapidly on account of high-risk insurance premiums, management company fees for winter layup and vessel monitoring, and costly post-layup detailing. Third pattern, superyacht economies of scale. Only in fully crewed vessels do costs come close to the mythical 10%. But they still fall short. In the most demanding Florida market, 6.5 to 7.5%. In Europe, 5 to 5.5% and the final, most significant practical conclusion. Geography is one of the most consequential financial decisions you will make. For the Pirelli 42, OPEX in Europe runs 38 to 42% lower than in Miami. For the Ferretti 860, 29%. That translates to tens or hundreds of thousands of euros per year. Before you buy a yacht, build a detailed OPEX breakdown. Breakdown engine service rates, estimate authorized labor costs, check local insurance and marina rates, and factor in flag registration and satellite communications. And don't forget to account for detailing, your hurricane plan, or winterization. The 10% rule is a mental shortcut from the 1980s that could end up costing you more than the yacht itself. What I have observed over the last 45 years or so, their ability to carry-out major repair work in remote locations, etc., have been, quite simply, outstanding. 

I had worked with many engineers & mates, who had spent time working on mega-yachts, however, in the vast majority of cases, they had returned to work in the merchant marine, mostly on account of three factors: better time-off (1:1), better monetary compensation and a decent pension plan. - The “Yachting” Trap: Why Most People Who Buy Yachts Are Broke Within 3 Years: Kind: captions Language: en A used 58-ft sportfish boat sells in North Carolina. The sale price is $1 million. The seller walks away having paid $100,000 in broker commissions he never knew were fixed. His buyer's broker, the one he thought was working for him, collected $50,000 of that paid by the seller and never disclosed it. That was the cheap part of the deal. The closing check was smaller than the first year's operating bill. This video is about the financial structure behind that moment and why it repeats itself on marina docks from Florida to the Mediterranean every single season. The yachting trap is not one thing. It is a layered system with five moving parts and every part is designed to profit from someone who does not know the math. Who actually gets rich in the yachting world and is it ever the owner? Polished hulls gleam beneath showroom lights. Brokers in pressed blazers glide between espresso bars and leather sofas speaking in the practice language of possibility. The pitch is never about the boat itself. It is about a new identity. Here, the yacht is a passport to freedom, to status, to a kind of belonging that only exists dockside. The buyer, often successful in every other arena, steps into this world with a mix of calculation and longing. The brochures promise more than a vessel. They promise transformation. Words like investment, equity, and legacy drift through the conversation, but the real hook is always lifestyle. Brokers talk about Mediterranean summers, long weekends in the Bahamas, the silent glide out of the marina at sunrise. They mention family memories, business deals sealed on the off deck, friendships cemented over sunset cocktails. There is an unspoken suggestion that this is a club with velvet ropes and the only ticket is the signature at the bottom of the contract. For a moment, the buyer can almost see it. The gentle sway at anchor, the applause of water against the hull, the quiet certainty of having arrived. The pen hovers over the dotted line. The numbers, what they mean and what they hide, are for later. For now, the dream is real and the only thing left is to step aboard. The numbers the showroom never mentions are the ones that matter most. First, the baseline. Annual operating costs run between 10 and 15% of the yacht's original purchase price. That means for a $500,000 boat, the owner should expect to spend 50 to 75,000 dollars every single year just to keep the vessel afloat, insured, fueled, and maintained. [music] No exotic breakdowns, no storms, just the routine bill for showing up at the dock. Next, usage. The US Coast Guard's most recent survey puts average recreational boat [music] use at about 55 days per year. That's less than eight weeks out of 52. Most owners get on the water for a handful of weekends and a single extended trip, if that. The rest of the year, the yacht sits idle accumulating costs while losing value. Then comes depreciation. Industry-wide data show a new yacht loses between 10 and 20% of its value in the first year alone. By the fifth year, the typical vessel has shed 40 to 50% of its original price. This is not an outlier, it's the expected curve regardless of brand or maintenance. For a $500,000 boat, that is $250,000 gone before the first serious refit. These three benchmarks, 10 to 15% in annual costs, 55 days of use, and up to half the value lost in 5 years, form the backbone of the real ownership equation. They are not worst-case scenarios, but the industry's own consensus drawn from broker guides and Coast Guard data. The dream is sold in broad strokes, but the ledger is written in fine print. Charter programs are often pitched as the rational antidote to yachting's notorious cash burn. The promise is simple. Put your boat to work, let paying guests cover the bills, and enjoy the best of both worlds. The reality is governed by thresholds that most owners never cross. According to Vital Charters internal modeling, the break-even point for a typical 60-ft motor yacht lands between 16 and 20 weeks of paid charter per year. Anything less and the owner is subsidizing every charter guest out of pocket. The national average for private use, about 55 days a year, falls well short of this threshold and few owners have the appetite or network to fill a third of the calendar with paying clients. Stretch the lens to a decade. A $3 million yacht run through a standard charter management program generates a total outflow of roughly $8.1 million over 10 years. That figure absorbs purchase price, depreciation, operating costs, management fees, and the periodic refits that come with heavy use. The number is not a worst-case scenario. It is the sum of routine invoices, insurance renewals, and the relentless march of marina rate hikes. Charter income, even at a healthy utilization rate, rarely claws back more than a fraction of this drain. The illusion comes from gross revenue. Charter brochures spotlight the top line. $30,000 a week booked solid for the season. But after management commissions, unscheduled repairs, and the high wear cost curve of commercial use, the net is a shadow of the headline figure. Meanwhile, the same $3 million placed in a broad market index fund would have doubled over the same period compounding quietly while the yacht's value eroded. The math is not subtle. Over a decade, the real contest is not between yachting and doing nothing. It is between the dream of ownership and the opportunity cost of capital. The numbers do not just favor the market, they expose the lifestyle pitch as a system designed to extract, not to build, wealth for the typical buyer. A $500,000 yacht purchase, mapped line by line, reveals the real mechanics of ownership. The initial outlay is rarely just the down payment. With a standard 25% down, the buyer wires $125,000 to the dealer on day one. The remaining $375,000 is financed, usually at 5% to 6% interest over 5 to 10 years. Year one begins with a silent deduction, depreciation. Industry data puts the first year loss at 15% of the original price. $75,000 erased before the first season ends. The boat is now worth $425,000 on paper, but the loan principal remains untouched. Operating expenses arrive monthly. The 10% rule, accepted by every major brokerage, means $50,000 vanishes over 12 months for dockage, insurance, fuel, maintenance, and the steady drip of marina fees. These are not luxury upgrades or emergencies, just the baseline for keeping the vessel ready. Add annual interest on the loan, which at 5% means roughly $19,000 in year one alone. The running total, $75,000 lost to depreciation, $50,000 spent on operations, $19,000 paid in interest, and $125,000 gone in the initial down payment. The first 12 months consumed $269,000 of cash and equity. Year two repeats the cycle. Another $50,000 for operations, another $18,000 in the loan balance declines, and an additional 12% depreciation, about $51,000, shrinks the boat's value further. By the end of year three, the owner faces a cumulative outflow approaching $400,000, not including the original purchase The boat itself, now three years older, has lost up to 40% of its value trading at $300,000 to $320,000 on the open market. A looming cost sits just over the horizon. Major refits, engines, hull, electronics arrive around the five-year mark often demanding another 10% to 20% of the original price. For many, the decision to sell comes just before this grenade goes off, locking in losses and passing the next round of expenses to the next owner. The ledger does not lie. The system extracts value from the buyer at every step and the numbers compound quietly year after year. Brokers occupy a unique position in the yachting ecosystem. Their income is tied directly to the deal. A standard 10% commission is split evenly between the listing broker and the buyer's broker. On a $1 million sale, that is $100,000, $50,000 to each side. The seller pays the commission, but the buyer's broker is presented as their advocate. In reality, both brokers are incentivized to close the transaction, not to challenge its wisdom or interrogate the numbers. The commission is not a bonus for exceptional service. It is a built-in cost baked into every contract regardless of whether the buyer sails away with a sound investment or a rapidly depreciating asset. This arrangement is no secret within the industry, but it is rarely spelled out for buyers. The International Yacht Brokers Association, or IYBA, which sets the tone for much of the US market, has historically defended the 10% split as fair compensation for expertise and deal management. Yet, the lack of transparency has led to legal scrutiny. In 2023, the Lamb versus IYBA antitrust case landed in federal court. The claim was that IYBA members conspired to fix commissions at a flat $50,000 on $1 million transactions. Internal communications cited in the complaint described the fee as fixed [music] and non-negotiable, a direct challenge to the idea of open competition. The case remains unresolved, but its existence confirms that these commission structures are not just tradition. They are protected by industry consensus and at times enforced through opaque agreements. For buyers, this means the person guiding them through the process is paid by the other side and rewarded for closing quickly, not for ensuring long-term value. The advice chain is only as honest as the incentives allow. The moment the contract is signed and the commission is wired, any alignment between buyer and broker dissolves. The system is not accidental. It is engineered to move product, not to build wealth for the person stepping onto the dock for the first time. Referrals and incentives shape every step of yacht ownership. When a buyer asks for a marine survey, the broker's recommendation often comes with quiet expectations. Surveyors who keep deals moving forward are favored, while those who flag too many issues risk being left out of future business. The inspection, paid for by the buyer, becomes less about uncovering problems and more about protecting the sale. Loyalty flows through the broker's network, not to the owner's interests. Charter management follows the same logic. The promise is that chartering will offset costs, but the contract tells a different story. Management fees, locked in at 15 to 20% of gross charter revenue, are deducted before the owner sees a dollar. For every $100,000 earned, $15,000 to $20,000 is claimed by the management company, regardless of whether the owner turns a profit. The contract spells this out, but it is rarely the focus in sales pitches. >> [music] >> The manager's priority is bookings, not the owner's bottom line. Marina contracts add another layer. Annual escalation clauses, often buried in the fine print, quietly raise berth fees by 3 to 5% each year. A $1,500 monthly slip can reach nearly $1,700 by the third year. These increases compound, are rarely discussed up front, and only become clear with the next invoice. Escalation is rarely explained at the point of sale. Tax deductions are dangled as a final incentive. The so-called second home deduction is tightly limited. The yacht must qualify as a residence, >> be used for more than 14 days, and at least 10% of rental days, and survive IRS scrutiny. Most owners do not qualify, but the myth persists at the point of sale. Deduction. Language is used to close deals, not to guarantee savings. Each mechanism, survey referral, charter fee, marina escalation, tax myth, ensures service providers get paid first. The owner absorbs the risk and rising costs. Owner A stepped into the marina with a plan that looked solid on paper. The purchase price was $600,000. The down payment was $150,000. The rest was $450,000 financed at 6% interest over 10 years. The first season brought the expected joys, family weekends, a handful of business outings, the sense of having arrived. But by the end of year one, the numbers had already started to slip out of reach. The boat's value dropped by $90,000, 15%, before the first annual service was even due. Operating costs came in right on cue at $60,000, matching the 10% rule quoted by every brokerage. Loan interest alone added $27,000 to the tally. In 12 months, $177,000 was gone, and the boat's market value had slipped to $510,000. Year two, the pattern repeated. Another $60,000 in operating costs, another $25,000 in interest, and resale value fell by another 12%, $61,000 evaporated from the resale value. By the end of year three, the boat was worth $360,000. The loan balance stood at $415,000. Selling meant cutting a check just to close out the debt. The owner's equity had been hollowed out by a system that front-loads loss and back-loads risk. This is not a cautionary tale about poor planning. It is the statistical median for financed buyers who enter at retail and exit before the first major refit. Owner B believed in the charter offset. The pitch was simple. Buy an $850,000 catamaran, sign with a management company, and let paying guests cover the bills. Year one delivered $62,000 in gross charter revenue. Management fees, set at 20%, took $12,400 off the top. Heavy charter use drove up maintenance, $28,000 spent on repairs, and accelerated wear. That left $21,600 in net income against $127,000 in total ownership costs for the year. The ledger did not lie. The charter program covered less than a fifth of the actual outflow. By year two, bookings dropped, the charter firm terminated the contract, and the owner was left with a high-hour boat, mounting expenses, and no path to break even. The promise of neutralizing costs through chartering [music] had failed, not because of a bad season, but because the math was never in the owner's favor. Both owners started with different strategies, one with a classic loan, the other with a charter plan. Both ended up in the same place, chasing sunk costs, boxed in by a system that rewards everyone except the person holding the keys. The numbers are not outliers, they are the rule, disguised by glossy marketing and the language of opportunity. Owner C walked into the marina with no illusions about financing or fast returns. This time, there was no bank involved, just a wire transfer for the full price of a lightly used sloop, $220,000 paid in cash. On paper, it looked like a safe harbor. No loan, no interest, no risk of being underwater. But the map was still wrong. The first year brought an unexpected $32,000 in deferred maintenance. The survey had missed corroded rigging and a leaking fuel tank, issues quietly overlooked in the rush to close. The owner paid out of pocket, chalking it up to bad luck, but the pattern was already set. Insurance, which had seemed like a simple checkbox, turned into a moving target. By year two, the underwriter flagged the hull's age and refused renewal without costly upgrades. The only replacement policy available came with a $15,000 annual premium, more than double the original quote. The marina, meanwhile, had its own escalation clause. The slip fee, $700 a month at signing, rose to $1,000 by the third year, a 43% jump triggered by a clause buried on page six of the contract. The owner, now locked into the berth, watched fixed costs outpace any savings from buying with cash. A three-way cross-section tells the story. Owner A, boxed in by loan payments and a shrinking asset. Owner B, squeezed by charter math and management fees. Owner C, supposedly immune, still drained by hidden maintenance, insurance hikes, and rising marina costs. The specifics differ, but the destination is the same. Whether the entry point is debt, charter, or cash, the system's incentives ensure that costs compound quietly and persistently, while value erodes. The only constant across all profiles is that the service providers, brokers, surveyors, managers, marina operators, get paid first. The owner, regardless of strategy, absorbs the risk and the loss. The pattern is not accidental. It is engineered by contracts, clauses, and a network of incentives that operate in plain sight. The promise of yachting as a rational asset dissolves when the numbers are laid side by side. What remains is a lifestyle product, sold as an investment, but structured so that the owner is always the last to get paid. Today, the illusion persists. Glossy brochures still frame yachts as assets, not consumables. The real math, depreciation rates exceeding 20% per year, operating costs rivaling mortgage payments, remains buried in fine print. In a market engineered for extraction, clarity is power. The only real windfall belongs to those who read the ledger, not just the dream. The map was never neutral. Now you know where it leads.
