- The "Yacht Ownership" Trap: Why 90% of New Owners Are Broke Within 12 Months: Kind: captions Language: en Buying a yacht feels like achieving the ultimate dream. Freedom on the water, luxury lifestyle, endless adventures. The reality is brutally different. Within 12 months, most new yacht owners face financial disaster they never saw coming. The purchase price is just the beginning. What follows is a cascade of expenses that drain bank accounts faster than anyone imagined possible. The yacht ownership trap catches nearly everyone because the true costs are hidden until after the purchase. Here is exactly why 90% of new owners are broke within 12 months. The 10% rule. Nobody mentions. Industry experts agree on one fundamental truth about yacht ownership. Annual operating costs equal 10 to 15% of the yacht's purchase price every single year. This is not optional. This is not negotiable. This is the absolute minimum to keep a yacht running. Buy a $1 million yacht and budget $100,000 to $150,000 per year in operating costs. Buy a $5 million yacht and the annual cost jumps to $500,000 to $750,000. buy a $10 million super yacht and operating costs exceed $1 million annually. These numbers shock new buyers who thought the purchase price was the main expense. The 10% rule covers everything required to operate the yacht for one year. Crew salaries and benefits, dockage and moing fees, insurance premiums, fuel costs, routine maintenance and repairs, provisioning for guests and crew, communications and technology, legal and accounting fees, reserves for periodic refits, every single expense required to keep the yacht functional. Most new buyers budget $0 for annual operating costs. [music] They drain savings or take loans to buy the yacht, believing the hard part is over. Then reality hits within the first month when bills start arriving. Marina wants slip fees. Insurance company sends the premium invoice. Crew expects salaries. [music] Fuel tanks need filling. The financial bleeding starts immediately and never stops. The dockage disaster. Marina fees represent one of the largest fixed costs in yacht ownership. Unlike fuel, which varies with usage, dockage fees [music] are constant whether the yacht moves or sits idle. The costs vary dramatically by location, but all are substantial. Florida marinas average $45 per foot per month. A 60 ft yacht pays $2,700 monthly or $32,400 annually just to sit in the water. Mediterranean marinas exceed $120 per foot monthly. That same 60 ft yacht cost $7,200 per month or $86,400 yearly for a slip. Premium locations charge even more. Monaco, Portortovo, and Izza command extraordinary fees because demand exceeds supply. A 60 m yacht in a premium Mediterranean location pays over $500,000 annually just for birthing. The yacht sits tied to a dock and the meter runs constantly. New owners discover these fees after purchase when marinas demand payment. The shock is immediate. Suddenly, tens of thousands or hundreds of thousands of dollars disappear annually for the privilege of keeping the yacht somewhere. Moving to cheaper marinas means leaving premium locations and social scenes that motivated the purchase in the first place. Winter storage adds another layer of cost. Northern climates require hauling boats out of water for winter. Haul out costs, winter storage fees, winterization services, and spring commissioning add thousands more. A 50ft yacht pays $5,000 to $10,000 just for seasonal storage and preparation. If you are realizing yacht ownership is more expensive than you thought, hit that like button and subscribe for more reality checks. The insurance nightmare. Marine insurance is mandatory and expensive. Comprehensive yacht insurance costs between8% and 2% of the yacht's value annually. The variation depends on yacht age, cruising area, claims history, and coverage limits. A $1 million yacht requires $8,000 to $20,000 in annual insurance premiums. A $5 million yacht needs $40,000 to $100,000 yearly. A $10 million super yacht demands $80,000 to $200,000 in insurance costs. These premiums are non-negotiable because lenders require insurance and operating without coverage is financial suicide. Insurance companies evaluate risk carefully. Older yachts cost more to ensure because systems fail more frequently. Yachts cruising hurricane zones or pirateinfested waters pay premium rates. Owners with claims history face higher premiums or coverage denial. The insurance underwriting process reveals problems buyers ignored during purchase. Deductibles are substantial. $5,000 to $25,000 deductibles are common. A single claim for storm damage, grounding, or collision triggers the deductible plus future premium increases. Some damage is not covered at all, leaving owners with massive repair bills. Insurance refuses to pay. The maintenance money pit routine. Maintenance costs 5 to 10% of the yacht's purchase price annually, according to industry standards. A $1 million yacht requires $50,000 to $100,000 yearly in maintenance and repairs. This is just routine upkeep, not major breakdowns or refits. Engine servicing happens annually regardless of usage. Oil changes, filter replacements, cooling system maintenance, and inspections cost thousands per engine. Twin engine yachts double the expense. Larger yachts with multiple generators and complex systems multiply costs further. The bills arrive whether the yacht ran 100 hours or sat idle all year. Whole maintenance never ends. Bottom paint protects against marine growth but degrades constantly. Annual hallouts for bottom cleaning and repainting cost $5,000 to $20,000 depending on yacht size. Antifouling paint alone runs hundreds of dollars per gallon. A large yacht requires dozens of gallons. Teak decks require constant attention. Cleaning, sanding, oiling, and eventual replacement consume enormous money. Te looks beautiful but demands fanatical maintenance. Neglect for one season causes damage requiring tens of thousands in repairs. Many owners discover this reality after the first summer when teak turns gray and splits. Systems fail constantly on yachts. Air conditioning units break. Generators quit. Water makers clog. Electronics fail from moisture. Plumbing leaks. Every system on a yacht operates in harsh saltwater environment, accelerating deterioration. Parts are marine grade expensive. Labor costs are astronomical because marine technicians charge premium rates. Unexpected repairs destroy budgets. Navigation system failure costs$10,000 to $30,000 to replace. Engine rebuild runs $50,000 to $150,000. Generator replacement costs $20,000 to $60,000. Hole damage from grounding requires $50,000 to $200,000 repairs. These emergencies happen regularly and insurance often denies coverage. Want to avoid the yacht ownership trap? Like and subscribe to learn what works. The crew cost catastrophe. Yachts over 60 ft typically require professional crew. Insurance companies mandate crew for larger vessels because owner operation creates liability exposure and maintenance problems. Crew costs represent 30 to 40% of total annual expenses for crude yachts. Captain salaries range from 60,000 to $200,000 annually depending on yacht size and experience requirements. A 50ft yacht needs a captain making 70,000 to 90,000 yearly. A 100 ft yacht requires a captain earning 150,000 to 200,000 annually. These are salaries only, not total compensation. Benefits add 20 to 30% on top of salaries. health insurance, travel expenses, training courses, uniforms, [music] and time off increase crew costs substantially. A captain making $100,000 in salary costs 120,000 to 130,000 total compensation. Additional crew multiply expenses, engineers, chefs, stewards, and deckhands each command salaries. A 60 ft yacht might operate with captain and deckhand totaling 120,000 to 160,000 annually. A 100 foot yacht needs captain, engineer, chef, and two stewards totaling 400,000 to 600,000 yearly. Super yachts employ crews of 10 to 20 people with annual costs exceeding $1 million to $4 million. Crew work year round, whether the yacht operates or sits idle. Owners using yachts 2 weeks annually, still pay crew full salaries for 52 weeks. The yacht sits empty, 11 12 months, while crew salaries run constantly. This reality shocks owners who assumed crew costs would be minimal. The fuel fiasco. Fuel consumption varies dramatically, but all yachts burn shocking amounts of diesel. A 40ft yacht consumes 40 to 80 L/ hour at cruising speed. A 60 ft yacht burns 100 to 200 L hourly. 70 ft and larger yachts consume 200 to 400 plus L/ hour. Fuel prices fluctuate, but marine diesel averages $1 to $2 per liter globally. A 60ft yacht cruising at 15 knots burns 150 L per hour. Eight hours of cruising consumes 1,200 L, costing 1,200 to $2,400. A weekend cruise costs $3,000 to $7,000 in fuel alone. Annual fuel costs depend on usage, but even light use is expensive. 30 hours monthly cruising on a 60 ft yacht burns 54,000 L annually, costing $54,000 to $18,000. Heavy use easily doubles or triples fuel consumption, pushing annual costs over $200,000. Super yachts consume fuel at terrifying rates. Long-range cruising burns $400,000 to $1 million in fuel annually. Trans oceanic passages consume hundreds of thousands of liters. The fuel bill for a single crossing can exceed the annual income of most families. The depreciation destruction. Yachts depreciate rapidly, especially during the first 5 years. New yachts lose 20 to 30% of value within 3 years. A $5 million new yacht is worth 3.5 million to 4 million after 3 years. 1 to$ 1.5 million simply evaporates. Pre-owned yachts depreciate slower but still lose value. annual depreciation of 5 to 10% is typical for yachts over 5 years old. A $2 million 10-year-old yacht loses $100,000 to $200,000 in value annually. This is not cash flowing out, but asset value disappearing, which affects net worth and borrowing capacity. Market conditions amplify depreciation. Economic downturns see yacht values collapse 20 to 40% virtually overnight. The 2008 financial crisis destroyed yacht values globally. Owners who paid $5 million saw values drop to 3 million. Some yachts became impossible to sell at any price. Brand matters enormously in depreciation. Premium builders like Feedship, Lerson and Benetti hold value better. Production builders see steeper depreciation. Unknown builders suffer catastrophic value loss. Buyers focused on purchase price often choose lesser brands than discover nobody wants to buy them later. Subscribe now to avoid making these expensive mistakes. The hidden cost avalanche beyond major categories. Dozens of smaller expenses accumulate into substantial annual costs. Each seems minor individually, but together they add 15 to 25% to annual budgets. New owners rarely budget these hidden costs causing budget shortfalls in year 1. Provisioning means stocking food, beverages, linens, [music] toiletries, and supplies. Charter level provisioning costs $5,000 to $20,000 monthly. Even private use requires thousands monthly in provisions. Annual provisioning easily exceeds $50,000 to $100,000 for active yachts. Communications and technology require constant spending. Satellite internet, navigation software subscriptions, chart updates, and equipment upgrades cost thousands annually. Modern yachts rely on complex electronics that become obsolete, quickly requiring expensive updates. Legal and accounting fees apply to yacht ownership, yacht registration, [music] flag state compliance, customs, and immigration paperwork, and tax planning require professional services. Annual legal and accounting costs range from $5,000 to $25,000 depending on yacht size and cruising patterns. Port fees and clearances add up quickly. Mediterranean cruising incurs port charges. tourist taxes and clearance fees at every stop. Annual port fees easily reach 10,000 to $30,000 for active cruising. Some premium locations charge thousands per day just to anchor in harbor. Licensing, certifications, and inspections are mandatory. Flagstate surveys, safety equipment inspections, radio licensing, and various certifications cost thousands annually. Larger yachts face more stringent requirements and higher costs. The charter income illusion. Many buyers believe charter income will offset ownership costs. Charter companies promote this idea aggressively. The reality is charter income rarely covers even half of annual expenses and comes with significant downsides. Charter income requires placing the yacht in commercial service, which means commercial insurance, crew certifications, safety equipment upgrades, and regulatory compliance. These requirements add 20 to 40% to operating [music] costs. The yacht must meet higher standards than private use requires. Charter wear and tear is brutal. Charterers abuse yachts. Equipment breaks frequently. Interiors suffer damage. Maintenance costs increase 30 to 50% on charter yachts. The extra income gets consumed by extra repairs. Charter weeks are limited. Premium yachts achieve 10 to 20 weeks of charter annually. Most yachts struggle to book even 10 weeks. Charter income on a $1 million yacht might generate $50,000 to $150,000 annually. Operating costs exceed $300,000. Charter income covers 15 to 50% of costs at best. Charter availability restrictions annoy owners. The yacht must be available for charter during prime season when owners most want to use it. Personal use conflicts with charter bookings. Many owners discover they bought a yacht they cannot use when they actually want it. The financing [music] failure. Yacht loans seem attractive until the math reveals the trap. Lenders offer yacht financing at 4 to 7% interest over 10 to 20 years. The monthly payments seem manageable, but the total cost is devastating. Finance a $2 million yacht at 5% interest over 15 years. Monthly payment is $15,800. Annual debt service is $189,600. Total payments over 15 years exceed $2.8 million. Interest alone costs $800,000. Debt service is separate from operating costs. The yacht still requires 10 to 15% annually for operations. $2 million yacht needs 200,000 to 300,000 yearly. Add debt service and total annual cost reaches $390,000 to $490,000. Few buyers can sustain this expense level. Negative equity appears quickly. The yacht depreciates faster than the loan balance decreases. Within 3 years, the yacht is worth 1.5 million while the loan balance remains 1.8 million. Owners are underwater and trapped. Selling means writing a check for $300,000 just to escape. Interest rate risk affects variable rate loans. Rising interest rates increase monthly payments. Owners budgeting for 5% suddenly face 7% which increases annual debt service by tens of thousands. The financial stress becomes unbearable. The exit trap. Selling a yacht is difficult and expensive. The market is illquid. Finding buyers takes months or years. Motivated sellers accept massive discounts. Brokers charge 10% commission. The financial loss on exit is often catastrophic. Listing costs money before any sale. Professional photos, videos, surveys, and marketing materials cost $5,000 to $20,000. Brokers want exclusive listings. The yacht sits on market for months while carrying costs continue. Buyers demand surveys revealing every defect. Survey costs $5,000 to $15,000. [music] Buyers use survey findings to negotiate price reductions of 10 to 30%. A yacht listed at 1 million sells for 700,000 after negotiations. Seller pays broker commission of 70,000, leaving 630,000. Subtract loan payoff and closing costs and the seller walks away with massive loss. Time kills deals constantly. Buyers back out. Financing falls through. Surveys reveal problems. Weather prevents sea trials. Each delay costs thousands in ongoing expenses while the yacht sits unsold. Owners desperate to exit accept terrible deals just to stop the financial bleeding. If this video is saving you from financial disaster, smash that like and subscribe button. The psychological toll. Financial stress from yacht ownership destroys relationships and health. Marriages fail under the pressure. Business performance suffers. Sleep disappears. Anxiety becomes constant. The dream yacht becomes a nightmare nobody can escape. Owners hide the financial reality from friends and family. The yacht symbolizes success, but the bank account is empty. Social pressure to maintain appearances while secretly broke creates unbearable stress. Some owners would rather continue bleeding money than admit the yacht was a mistake. Resentment builds toward the yacht. What started as a dream becomes a hated obligation. The yacht sits unused because using it costs thousands per trip. The slip fees and insurance continue regardless. Owners pay enormous sums for something they actively avoid using. The rare exceptions. Some buyers successfully own yachts long-term. They share common characteristics. Liquid wealth exceeds 10 times the purchase price. Annual income covers all yacht expenses without lifestyle impact. They own yachts under 60 ft avoiding crew requirements. They use the yacht extensively justifying the expense. They plan for the 10% rule from day one. These successful owners are rare. Most buyers have liquid wealth equal to two to four times purchase price. Yacht expenses consume significant portions of income. Lifestyle suffers. The financial strain becomes unsustainable within 12 months. The alternative path. Chartering yachts avoids the ownership trap completely. Charter exactly when desired. Different yachts every trip. No maintenance worries. No crew salaries. No insurance premiums. No depreciation losses. Walk away after each charter with zero ongoing obligations. Charter costs seem high at 20,000 to 100,000 per week. Compare this to ownership where $1 million yacht costs 150,000 annually plus all the headaches. Charter 20 weeks annually for total cost matching ownership, but with zero maintenance, crew, or hassle. Fractional ownership offers middle ground. Share ownership and costs with others. Use the yacht several weeks annually. Professional management handles everything. Costs are predictable and capped. Exit is easier than whole ownership. For most people, fractional ownership delivers better experience at lower cost. 90% of new yacht owners are broke within 12 months because they fundamentally misunderstand the costs. The purchase price is just the entry fee. Annual operating costs equal 10 to 15% of purchase price every year forever. Dockage, insurance, maintenance, crew, fuel, and countless hidden expenses drain accounts faster than anyone imagined. The 10% rule is not a guideline. It is the absolute minimum to keep a yacht functional. Most yachts cost more when accounting for unexpected repairs, depreciation, and financing costs. The total cost of ownership over 10 years easily exceeds 3 to five times the purchase price. Before buying a yacht, honestly assess whether annual income can absorb 10 to 15% of purchase price indefinitely without lifestyle impact. If the answer is anything except an enthusiastic yes, do not buy charter instead. Enjoy yaching without the financial devastation that destroys most new owners within their first year. Hit that subscribe button if this video opened your eyes to the yacht ownership trap. Share this with anyone considering buying a yacht. The information here could save them from financial disaster.

your lane you don't want to go off into the sidewalk or into the grass the same thing with the intracoastal waterways here they are marked by the united states coast guard to keep you in deep water and this is especially important when you're on a big boat however down here in miami where i'm at right now big boats small boat you could easily run aground here which means you'll go in water too shallow for your boat and you'll end up stuck or even worse damage your motor or even hurt somebody so we want to make sure that we do none of that the first concept that i want you to grab is if you see a red and a green channel marker we have a saying in boating red and green stay in between a lot of the times there's tight channels just like this one where you're able to see both the red and the green pretty much right side by side get in between those that's the easiest form of navigation now a lot of times you won't see both channel markers side by side like that in fact most of the time so you have to know what side of the boat the red one should be on and what side the green should be on so here's another term that i want you to remember another phrase that we say red write return red right return they call it the three r's it is boating 101 remember that so what that means is anytime you're coming from an open body of water that is when you're coming off the ocean coming into your home port the red is always going to be on your right red right return which means the green will be on your left when it comes to navigating the intracoastal waterway the coast guard gave us day boards these are little yellow squares and triangles that they place on navigable instruments they might not always be on a typical channel marker so what they're on doesn't really have significance the important thing is that you know what to look for and what side to keep them on so the red right return rule does apply to the intercoastal waterways as well but you got to know what your port is when you're coming off the ocean it's obvious that you're coming into port so you're coming from an open body of water red right return keep that red channel marker on your right well when you're going from new jersey now to florida where is the home port that's what's going to determine which side those day boards are going to be on the coast guard makes it easy for us pretty much if you're coming from new jersey to texas to florida you're heading in that south that southerly direction you're going to be looking for those squares and triangles on those channel markers or on you know some sort of beacon out there know your port so i'm heading south here i'm in miami let's just say i'm heading to the keys the triangles are always going to be on my right and the squares are always going to be on my left or my port side that's key now if i was heading from miami up to virginia and go pick up my dad up there and do a little cruising around i would have to do the opposite the triangles are then going to be on my and the squares are then going to be on my right or my starboard the color really isn't of importance because the united states coast guard knows that a lot of us are color blind you're looking for the shapes shapes are what you can see a lot easier and the closer you get to them you'll be able to see that just remember that the day markers are of the most importance when you're traveling through the inner coastal waterway you want to pay attention to those little squares and triangles on these aids to help you better navigate and stay in the channel when you go out of the channel you risk running your ground damaging your prop damaging your boat or somebody could even get hurt so it's of your your best interest and your responsibility of a as a captain of your boat to know how to navigate these channels and that's exactly why they're here the coast guard put them here so you can stay in deeper water and this specifically applies to all size - Boat Navigation Basics: Buoys and Channel Markers | BoatUS: Kind: captions Language: en Hey there, folks! Lenny Rudow here for&nbsp; BoatUS. We're out here on a gorgeous&nbsp;&nbsp; day and I'm looking around and I see buoys&nbsp; everywhere. And what do all these different&nbsp;&nbsp; buoys mean? The different shapes sizes and&nbsp; colors? Maybe once upon a time you knew.&nbsp;&nbsp; Maybe you just don't know. Let's go through a&nbsp; little refresher on what all those buoys mean.&nbsp;&nbsp; First off, let's point out that there are a&nbsp; million and one different buoys out there -- all&nbsp;&nbsp; kinds of buoys marking things like underwater&nbsp; cables, shoals, wrecks, speed limits. I mean you&nbsp;&nbsp; name it, there's a buoy for it. Obviously we're&nbsp; not going to get through all of those today;&nbsp;&nbsp; we're just going to cover the basics that you're&nbsp; most likely to see on a common basis. There are&nbsp;&nbsp; even special buoys for different waterways -- the&nbsp; inland waterways, the Mississippi. They have their&nbsp;&nbsp; own buoys there. The Intracoastal Waterway (ICW)&nbsp; has its own buoys. You need to study up on these&nbsp;&nbsp; if you're going to visit these areas. You can&nbsp; do so at USCGboating.org. Just look for the PDF&nbsp;&nbsp; that shows you all the different buoys -- all&nbsp; the different types, shapes, and sizes. First,&nbsp;&nbsp; let's talk a little bit about those markers.&nbsp; They're also called fixed markers or beacons.&nbsp;&nbsp; The reds are triangular and have even numbers&nbsp; on them. The greens are square and have odd&nbsp;&nbsp; numbers on them. Now, these mark channels, and&nbsp; you may have heard the old saying "red, right,&nbsp;&nbsp; returning." That's because whenever you're headed&nbsp; inland you want to keep the red off the starboard&nbsp;&nbsp; side of the boat and the green off the port side.&nbsp; Thus red, right, returning. You might notice,&nbsp;&nbsp; folks, this one has a light on top. Some of these&nbsp; fixed markers are lighted, but some are not. Generally speaking, buoys are used where it's&nbsp; too deep to put a fixed marker or where channels&nbsp;&nbsp; shift. Channels do shift and change, and in that&nbsp; case, you need something that's floating that you&nbsp;&nbsp; can move. Now in the big, deep shipping channels,&nbsp; you'll see very large buoys. Inside on smaller&nbsp;&nbsp; channels you'll see smaller buoys. The small&nbsp; cylindrical green ones are called "cans." Red ones&nbsp;&nbsp; come to a point at the top. Those are called "nun"&nbsp; buoys. OK, now you want to hear something really&nbsp;&nbsp; funny, people? Sometimes you're going to see buoys&nbsp; with both colors! Now if you see red and green&nbsp;&nbsp; horizontal bands that means you're at the junction&nbsp; of two channels. If you see red and green vertical&nbsp;&nbsp; bands, that means there's navigable water on all&nbsp; sides. Another one you'll see out there are yellow&nbsp;&nbsp; buoys. Now yellow buoys fall into that category&nbsp; we were talking about earlier -- they can indicate&nbsp;&nbsp; all kinds of stuff. To figure out what a yellow&nbsp; buoy is, you really need to refer to your chart. One you definitely want to keep your eyes peeled&nbsp; for is black and white stripes. When you see a&nbsp;&nbsp; buoy with black and white stripes that means&nbsp; there's an obstruction running from that buoy&nbsp;&nbsp; in toward shore, so make sure you go all the way&nbsp; around it. Now, what's this plain old white buoy&nbsp;&nbsp; with blue lettering on it? That's a mooring&nbsp; buoy you don't have to worry about those,&nbsp;&nbsp; but whenever you see a mooring buoy, you should be&nbsp; aware that you're probably in a slow speed zone.&nbsp;&nbsp; Often they'll be white with a blue stripe around&nbsp; them, too. What about these white cylindrical guys&nbsp;&nbsp; with the orange bands? You're going to see a lot&nbsp; of those. They indicate many different things,&nbsp;&nbsp; often slow speed zones or speed limits and when&nbsp; you get up close to them you can read what they&nbsp;&nbsp; say. They're always marked as to just what they&nbsp; indicate. This one says, "Caution. You're always&nbsp;&nbsp; responsible for your wake," because we're in a&nbsp; enclosed creek with a lot of marinas around it.&nbsp;&nbsp; OK, we've made it through the basics&nbsp; but, truth be told folks, hey,&nbsp;&nbsp; it can be confusing out there! There's&nbsp; a lot to gaining a full comprehension&nbsp;&nbsp; on what all those different nav aids&nbsp; are, so don't forget go to the Coast&nbsp;&nbsp; Guard website and look for the U.S. Aids to&nbsp; Navigation PDF. It's a great explanation,&nbsp;&nbsp; and they made it just for you so check it out.&nbsp; Well, I hope you found this video helpful,&nbsp;&nbsp; and don't forget to click on that subscribe&nbsp; button so you don't miss our future BoatUS videos.

- How To Navigate Using the Stars: Kind: captions Language: en Without clouds, about 6,000 stars are visible in the night sky, from anywhere on Earth. This number is lower in areas of heavy light pollution, but for the most of human history, this is what we had to look up to. In earlier times, there were many ideas as to what the stars were. The Greeks saw constellations, who were sentient, divine beings, awarded with the place among the stars, for some great deed done long ago. To the Paiute tribe, who lived in the Great Basin of North America, start were the children of the Father Sun and the Mother Moon, eaten everyday by the light of the Sun, and nursed back to life by the love of the Moon. In Chinese mythology, some stars represented actual people. One is Jīn Yú - "the girl who weaves the clouds". And another is Nu Lang - "the herdsman, who cares for the cattle of the Heavens". The two were in love, so the Gods places them on either side of the Milky Way, to make sure, they were never distracted from their duties. Only once a year, on the 7th day of the 7th month, the two lovers are allowed to meet, when the birds in the sky will form a bridge across the Milky Way. To the Yakut tribe of Siberia, the stars are crystal lenses, used by the Gods to watch the Earth. And for the Turk people of central Asia, the sky was one big tent, and the stars were nothing more, than small rips in it. Of course, we know now that all of these stories are wrong. And in fact, stars are giant gas clouds, glowing from the process of nuclear fusion, billions of miles away. But of these ancient understandings of the stars, one remains true to this day. And no, it isn't Zodiac readings - but navigation. Ancient travelers, and modern sailors, and pilots alike, would use the stars, to voyage through their lands, and to discover new ones. So, how did they do it? How do you navigate using the stars? Well first, a quick look at some time-lapse photography, will show, that the stars are not static in our skies. But instead, they streak across the sky, as the Earth revolves. Using a moving star as a reference point is problematic, especially for long journeys, so you can't just pick any star in they sky, and follow in its direction. The same problem is present for the Sun and Moon for navigation, as they will move across the sky in a similar way. And while many think, they may be able to use the Sun, at the very least to rise in the East and set in the West, due to the Earth's seasonal tilts, it's rare, that the Sun ever rises in true East, or sets in true West. Only on the equinoxes, at the equator, does this occur - just twice a year. So, what's needed is a reference point, that no mater the year, month, week or day, will always lead you in the same direction. One last thing before we get started, is the stars used for navigation vary, depending on which hemisphere you're in, north or south; as the stars in the northern hemisphere can't be seen from the southern hemisphere, and the other way around. So - knowing that, let's get into it. First, we'll start in the northern hemisphere, and here, we'll find perhaps the most well-known star for navigation - Polaris, the North Star. By random chance, Polaris sits virtually on top of true North, just a fraction of a degree off. And because of this, it barely moves in the sky, while all the other stars spin around it. This can be seen best in long-exposure photographs, where all the moving stars make long streaks through the sky, but at the center is Polaris, hardly moved at all. Finding the North Star is fairly simple, too. It's the brightest star in the constellation Ursa minor, the Little Dipper, or the Little Bear (yeah, I don't see it either). Polaris is the last star in the handle. If you're having trouble spotting it, you can look for Ursa major, the Big Dipper, or the Big Bear, which I find much easier to spot. Once you found the Big Dipper, you can use its end stars, called the Dubhe and Merak, and follow the path they make, straight to Polaris. If clouds are covering Polaris, but not the Big Dipper, know, that Polaris lays roughly 5 lengths of the distance between Dubhe and Merak away. Also be aware, that while Polaris stays relatively still, the Big Dipper, like the rest of the stars, will move, and sometimes may be covered by objects on the horizon, like mountains or trees. So it's best to look for Polaris on high ground. Okay, so what about for going South? Can we just find the North Star, and sail in the opposite direction? Well, no. Without a point to aim your ship at, there's no real way to ensure you're sailing in the right direction. Instead, you'll need to find another well-known constellation, Orion. And if that just looks like a bunch of random stars, don't worry. All you need to know is his belt. These three close stars in a straight line: Alnitak, Alnilam, and Mintaka. Mintaka comes up again later, so try to remember it. But from here, you'll need to find what's called Orion's sword, which hangs from his belt. It's these three stars here, made from a dim star, a fuzzy star, and a bright star. That "fuzzy star" isn't actually a star, but a huge gas cloud called the Orion nebula, where new stars are actually being made. If you follow the direction of this sword down to the horizon, this point should lead south. This gets a little inaccurate, when the constellation is lower in the sky, but should still bring you south. Okay, now we're in the southern hemisphere, and this is where things start getting a little tough. But considering most of the Earth's oceans are in the southern hemisphere, it's still very much worth learning. Here, we'll find probably the second most popular method of using stars for directions, right after Polaris. If you're Australian, Kiwi, Papua-New Guinean, Brazilian, or Samoan, you're likely familiar with it already. And that's the Southern Cross, more officially known as Crux. It can be seen as a cross, with an extra blemish star right here. Some may find this one a bit harder to find in the sky, as just about any 4 stars can make a cross in the sky. But there's a way to help you find the right one. To the side of the Crux, there will always be two bright stars, sometimes easier to spot than the Crux itself. These are called the Pointer Stars, and you'll need them again later. The Pointer Stars always point towards the Crux, and can confirm for you, if you are unsure, that you are in fact using the proper stars. After you've located the Crux, and its pointers, you're going to need to do some more line-drawing in your head. Say your sky looks like this. Using the top and bottom stars of the Crux, draw a line out from the bottom of the Cross. Then, from the two Pointer Stars, draw a line out that is perpendicular to them, going in the same direction, as the line from the Cross. Where these two lines meet, is South. So just draw a line straight down to the horizon from there, and you should be good. Again: this is not 100%, absolutely, perfect South, but within a degree or so, and it's as perfect as things get. Unfortunately, there's no good way of finding North in the southern hemisphere, as Polaris is blocked by... well, the Earth. But there are a few more tricks, that you can use in either hemisphere, depending on what time of year it is. If you're near the equator - or the celestial equator, I should say, and you need to know East and West, you'll again have to find Orion's belt. Particularly, the leading star, meaning the first one that will rise above the hirizon. This star is called Mintaka, if you remember. And the point on the horizon, where it rises, is always true East. And the point on the horizon, where it sets, is always true West. And then there's one more easy trick, no matter where you are on Earth. First, take two sticks and plant them in the ground, roughly a yard or a meter apart. Then, pick a star in the sky, and adjust yourself, so that the stakes align and line up with the star. It helps to pick a bright star, that's easy to keep track of. Then all you need to do, is wait for the star to move out of alignment with the two sticks. Depending on which way it moved, you'll know one or two details about the way you face. If the star rose, you're facing East. If it sank, the West. If it moved to the left, you're facing North. And if it went to the right, you're facing South. If it rose diagonally to the left, then you're facing North-West, and et cetera. Of course, there are many more ways to use the stars, but I think we're good for now. I hope you learned something useful, and please leave a like and subscribe, if you'd like to see more videos like this in the future. And if you know any cool tricks with stars, even if they're not related to navigation, let me know in the comments. Thanks.

As of right now and today, it's not created yet. It will be created here in a few months. One reason why I love 400 is because it is a diminishing abrasive compound. Now, what does that mean? Okay, that means that this compound is super gritty, but when it heats up and it's under pressure of the polisher, it actually turns into a fine polish and then it finishes out really, really well. So, I've had a lot of people ask me like, "Hey, this is a heavy cutting compound. Why are you using it as polish?" Because this product is so good at breaking down into a fine polish that it finishes out perfectly on boats. Now, paint is a little different. Like, if you use this product on cars, it's a little aggressive on cars and you have to come back and refinish it up. But when you're talking about gel coat or marine paint, Monzerna 400 and is an amazing product and I've yet to find a product to come close to it. Um, I'm always testing products, guys. And when I do find one, hopefully in the future, you know, maybe we could find something better. But as of right now, Merna 400 has been great because it's good to cut out swirl marks, um, you know, light oxidation, and it finishes out super well to leave a perfect perfect finish. So that's why, guys, I use Monzerna 400 a ton. pretty much in every single one of my videos. And it's gonna, like I said, it's going to remove light oxidation, light water marks, and best of all, it's going to remove your holograms and swirl marks that the rotary buffers leave behind. Now guys, what I did not film on this was me washing the boat again with Dawn dishwashing soap uh between the Menza 400 and then the Jcar step. I always do it. So, I basically did all the polishing on one day and then I came back the next day to wash it and then do the wax. So, guys, I recommend either washing it down with Dawn dishwashing soap again and this is going to help get all the oils off from the compound. After we're done polishing and after you wash it with Dawn again to get all the oils off of the boat, then we're going to go ahead and use our Jessar Power Lock to seal up the gel coat. All right, this is a sealant, guys. And if you watch any of my other videos, you know all of this, but for the sake of guys that have just found this for the first time, this product is a sealant. You put a nice thin layer on. And the reason why I say thin, guys, is because if you put too much of a thick layer, it's kind of a pain in the butt to get off. Um, a lot of people have this like this this thought of, you know, if I put more product on that means it's going to work better. No, not necessarily because you're taking it all off when you wipe it off anyway. You just need a thin layer, just enough to cover the surface and that's it. Okay. Go ahead, do your front brow. Do the exterior of the top side. And then we're going to move all the way around the boat. And then we're going to wait at least 30 minutes before we come and take off the Jessar Power Lock. That is the that is the the biggest thing with Jessar Power Lock is you want to apply it and then you want to let it sit for at least 30 minutes before you come and take it off. So what I usually do is I do the entire top side and then I come back and take it all off. Jessar is a great product because it's going to help with UV protection which is going to stop the boat from oxidizing and it's really good at water sheeting. Okay. Now, I've had people complain like, "Hey, Jess Power Lock doesn't really bead all that great." And I get it. It's not the best beading um sealant out there, but it's really really good at water sheeting. Now, what do I mean by that? Okay, water beads are great. They're cool. They're sexy. Everyone loves nice water beads. All right, I get it. But when a water beads up and it dries on the surface, that's actually what creates water marks. That's what creates water spots. When you see little spots that are dried on there, that's water marks. Now, the reason why that happens is because there's minerals in the water, like I said earlier, that if they dry on the surface, you can see their minerals basically bake into the gel coat. Now, when a product like Jessar Power Lock is a water sheeting product, that means the water hits it and then it literally sheets off. Like, it rolls off or it just comes right off. It doesn't just bead and stick on there. Okay? So, if you if you've uh detailed your boat and then a week or two later or a month later, you don't really see the water beating up super crazy, that does not necessarily mean the the Jes Power Lock is not working. It actually means it is working. It's doing what it's designed to do. And it's water sheeting. It's taking the water and taking it off of your boat versus just beating up and sitting on the surface. Heat. Hey, hey, hey. All right, guys. So, we're going to go ahead and just finish up the cockpit, get everything cleaned up, and then we're going to go ahead and wipe all of our Jessar power lock off. All right, so now we're going to move on to the final kind of detailing aspects before we uh say the boat is done and we give it to the customer. So, first we're going to do the metal. We're going to go over all of our metal with woody wax. Woody Wax is a great metal wax for all your stainless steel, aluminum, um, or any any chrome, any metal, any bright work you have. Okay, this is a good product for cleaning it and protecting it. This is a wax. It's going to protect it against corrosion, oxidation, stains, rust, that kind of thing. And it's also going to clean it. If there's light water marks, it'll take them off. If it needs light polishing, this product will do it. I love Woody Wax. I've used it for years and years and years. The owner is a great guy. Woody is awesome. Um, so guys, go ahead, do yourself a favor, pick up woody wax and use it on all of your metal. You can also use colonite. I'm not a huge fan of colonite. Most sport fishing, you know, most sport fishing owners either want woody wax or colonite. They're kind of arch enemies. I prefer woody wax over colonite. Now, we're going to go ahead and protect our seats and our captain's seats. Uh there wasn't a ton of seat cushions on this boat, so we're doing the captain's seats right here, the captain's chair. Uh all all this is 303. It's going to protect the vinyl seats. It's going to make them uh more resistant to the weather, more resistant to UV, and it's also going to kind of clean them up a little bit. So, these seats were not in terrible shape. I normally would clean them with some degreaser and stuff, but these seats were literally perfect. They stay covered up. So, all we needed in this case was 303 just to protect them. I went ahead and wiped down the varnishing wood as well, just to give the varnish and the seats a little extra protection. All right. So, now we're going to hop to our eyes and glass. In this case, the Eisenlass was literally perfect. So, all I had was one wet rag and one dry rag, and I went ahead and wiped down the Eisenlass. Uh, the Eisening glass was already beating up real nice, so it didn't need a ton of extra protection. Just use one wet rag, one dry rag to clean up the Eisenlass. There are plenty of Eisenlass protectants out there. Um, IGL makes a few. I will link one down below that I like for the Eising glass as well that you can find if you want a little bit of Eisenlass protection. But in this case, one wet rag and one dry rag did the trick. Last but not least is the non-skid sealer. This is nautical one non-skid sealer and protector. All you do is you spray it directly onto the surface and then you wipe it in and then that's it. You are done. The floors were already clean cuz I took my time and, you know, just made sure the floors weren't dirty from when I did it the first time. - The 'Asking Price' Lie: Why Your Boat Is Worth 28% Less Than You Think: Kind: captions Language: en A 2019 C Ray 320 Sundancer goes up for sale at a marina in Clearwater, Florida in the spring of 2025. The owner has kept it clean for 4 years, added a new chart plotter and a biminy top, [music] and priced it at $87,000, $4,000 below what the family paid at the peak of the pandemic buying frenzy. He called his broker, who looked at similar boats on Yachtworld, confirmed the price looked competitive, and put the listing up. What neither of them discussed was that none of those comparable listings had actually sold. 11 months later, after three price reductions and two buyers who walked following the survey, the boat clears for $63,000. The broker collected 10%. That was the cheap part. This video is about the mechanism behind that moment and why it runs on a loop in marinas from Tampa Bay to Puget Sound year after year. Regardless of what the listing says, the asking price is not the selling price. The gap is not random negotiation, bad luck, or a single boat's condition issues. It is documented, consistent, and predictable. It is tracked across more than 30,000 actual marine transactions by a brokerage firm that logs every listing price, every price reduction, and every final negotiated sale. The gap is 28% for motorboats and 22% for sailboats. On a $100,000 boat, that is $28,000 the buyer refused to pay and $28,000 the seller did not know had already vanished before the first showing. This video covers how that gap is created, who profits from keeping it invisible, and the three calculations. Every boat owner needs to run before they name a price. For a long time, the story was beautiful. You did your research, found the right boat, handed over the down payment, and walked away with something that felt like more than a purchase. The slips at the marina were full of boats that had been there for 20 years, owned by people who bought them for half the price and watched them hold that value through recessions, oil shocks, and every financial storm the economy threw their way. The old salts at the fuel dock told you boats were different from cars. A fiberglass hull, properly maintained, could outlast its third owner. The culture around boat ownership carried a quiet financial assumption sewn into it that a well-maintained vessel was an asset you could pass down or at minimum sell back at something close to what you paid. You stood on the dock with a coffee watching the morning light come off the water and felt the particular satisfaction of someone who had made a sound decision. The numbers, what they mean and what they hide, are for later. For now, the dream is real. Six data points define what that dream actually costs, and everyone comes from the industry's own transaction records. The Boatshed Brokerage Network tracks every listing price, every price sale across more than 30,000 completed transactions over 25 years. and it found that motorboats sell for 28% below their asking price on average. On a $100,000 motorboat, that is $28,000 that never changed hands. Sailboats clear for 22% below asking on a $150,000 cruising sloop. The gap between what the owner asked and what the buyer paid is $33,000. The Boats Group, which operates Yachtworld, Boat Trader, and Boats.com, published its 2025-year endmarket index in March 2026, and found that the average North American boat spent 209 days on the market in 2025, up 37 days from the year before. The National Marine Manufacturers Association reported that [music] new US powerbo retail sales fell 8 to 10% in 2025, landing at approximately 215,000 to 225,000 units, down nearly 30% from the 2020 pandemic peak of 310,000 units. Infolink Technologies, which tracks every registered recreational vessel in the United States, reported that the median age of the American boat owner [music] reached 60 years old at the end of 2024, with more registered boat owners now in their 70s than in their 40s. And that same boat shed depreciation data set shows a new boat losing 15% of its value per year, compounded. So, a vessel purchased for $100,000 is worth approximately $44,000 after 5 years. These six benchmarks, the listing gap, the days on market, the unit sales decline, the financing headwind, the aging ownership base, and the depreciation curve, form the backbone of the real boat ownership equation. These are not worst case projections drawn from edge cases or market disasters. The industry produced every one of them from its own transaction data. Mark bought a 33- ft bento oceanis in the spring of 2021 for $78,000. He put down 25% $19,500 and financed the remainder at $4.3% over 10 years. In the first 12 months alone, the boat consumed $21,900, $7,750 in marina fees, insurance, and maintenance, $2,900 in loan interest, and $11,700 in depreciation against the purchase price. year 1 before Mark had taken the boat anywhere worth going. By the spring of 2024, 3 years in, the Oianis had depreciated to approximately $48,000 in actual market value. He still owed $43,000 on the loan. Selling at that point after the broker's commission would have required writing a check rather than receiving one. He held on over four years in total. His ownership costs for marina fees, insurance, hallouts, bottom paint, engine service, and a set of sales he had been deferring totaled $31,000. In March 2025, Mark listed the boat at $66,000. His broker searched Yachtworld, found comparable asking prices in the mid $60,000 range, and confirmed the number looked reasonable. What the broker did not pull was the soldboat database, a broker only subscription service logging actual transaction prices, which showed Bentois 33 models from 2018 to 2020, clearing in the low to mid $50,000 range. After 171 days on the market and two buyers who walked after their surveys, Mark accepted $52,000. He still owed $38,500 on the loan. The net from the sale after settling the remaining balance was $13,500. Against his down payment, four years of monthly loan service, and $31,000 in operating costs, Mark spent approximately $83,000 over 4 years to own a boat. He recovered $13,500. The loss was not built at the moment of sale. It was built the day the listing was priced against what other boats were asking rather than what they were selling for. The mechanism that made that outcome possible has four moving parts and all four depend on the same condition that the seller cannot easily find out what boats actually transact for. Unlike real estate, which requires public disclosure of sale prices through MLS [music] filings, marine transactions have no equivalent public record. Sale prices are reported voluntarily to a subscription-only platform, soldboats.com, which costs approximately $1,200 per year and is accessible primarily to licensed brokers and marine surveyors. The average private seller benchmarks their asking price against yachtworld listings. Yachtworld listings are not sold prices. They are opening positions what sellers hope to receive before negotiation. surveys and financing reality reduce that number by an average of 22 to 28%. The broker who takes the listing has access to [music] the actual transaction database. The broker also earns a percentage of whatever the boat sells for, whether that sale takes place 3 weeks after listing or 18 months later after three price cuts. The arrangement is only as transparent as the incentives allow. The second mechanism is older and deeper than the current market correction. Jack Ellis of Infolink Technologies speaking to the National Marine Manufacturers Association in early 2025 framed it without ambiguity. Many of the people who owned boats 25 years ago are the same people who own boats today, but they are 25 years older. The cohort that built recreational boating in the 1990s and in the early 2000s is now in its exit phase. The generation expected to replace those sellers, millennials and generation Z, is not arriving in equivalent numbers or with equivalent purchasing intent. They favor boat clubs, peer-to-peer rentals, and shared access models over the annual cost of full ownership, which runs $8,000 to $12,000. Supply grows as the ownership cohort ages out. The replacement [music] buyer poolool is not keeping pace. The third mechanism arrived in a congressional filing on January 28, 2026. Representative Nancy Mace introduced the No Tax on Boat Loan Interest Act of 2026, a bill that would allow buyers to deduct up to $10,000 annually in interest paid on Americanmade boat loans. The Marine Retailers Association of America endorsed it immediately. Frank Hugelmier, the president of the National Marine Manufacturers Association, called it legislation that will provide needed relief to American families seeking to purchase boats. A market in which the sellers own trade association is lobbying Congress for buyer subsidies because loan rates averaging 8.40% APR in the fourth quarter. 2025 have made financing prohibitive is not a sellers market. There is a fourth mechanism operating quietly beneath the other three. Group Bento, the world's largest sailing boat manufacturer, announced 66 [music] new models in 3 years, a 50% increase over its previous rollout pace. Every new model release resets what the market calls current, which means a 2019 hull competes against a 2024 version at similar price points. With the newer boat carrying factory financing terms, the used boat can never match. Faster model cycling does not generate new demand. It accelerates the obsolescence of everything that came before it. Linda bought a Lagoon 42 catamaran in the summer of 2021 for $385,000 with a charter management agreement already in place. The charter broker projected $68,000 in gross annual revenue. The math, he said, would practically pay for the boat. In year 1, the gross charter income came in at $61,000. The charter management company deducted 40% at source, $24,400 gone before Linda received a statement. The remaining $36,600 barely covered the accelerated wear that charter use generates, two hallouts per year instead of one, engine hours running at triple the rate of a private owner, and salon upholstery replacement after 18 months of rental guests. Her net contribution from charter against a $38,500 annual ownership cost was negative in year 1, year 2, and year 3. In 2024, the charter management company was acquired and renegotiated the contract terms to 45%. Linda listed the catamaran in early 2025 at $340,000. After 218 days and a [music] survey flagging $31,000 in Charter Accelerated wear items, the boat sold for $276,000. Of her $385,000 purchase price, Linda recovered $72 on the dollar before 4 years of carrying costs totaling $86,000. Mark started with a financed [music] purchase and a conviction that asking prices on Yacht World reflected real market value. Linda started with a charter income projection that promised to neutralize the ownership costs entirely. Both ended up chasing sunk costs boxed in by a system that rewarded the charter management company, the broker and the boatyard and extracted money from the person holding the keys. The convergence is not a coincidence. It is the architecture of a market built on asymmetric information, preserved by a compensation structure that profits from it and deepened by a demographic shift no one in the chain has any incentive to explain. Three calculations will protect you from it. First, pull actual sold comps, not asking prices. Any licensed broker has access [music] to the sold boat database by boats group. Request completed transactions for your specific make, model, and year range from the past six months. That number is your market. Yachtworld listings are starting positions, not transaction records. Second, apply the clearing adjustment before you list. The boat shed data shows motorboats clearing at 22 to 28% below, asking across 30,000 sales. A boat priced to the clearing range from day one sells in weeks rather than months. Third, calculate carry cost before deciding to hold. Annual ownership in the United States runs 8 to 12% of boat value per year. A one-year hold that seeks $5,000 more but costs $10,000 in annual carrying costs is a loss with a weight attached. The arithmetic is not complicated. The transaction data exists. 30,000 completed sales, annual market indexes, [music] registration records on every recreational vessel in the United States. None of it reaches the seller at listing because no party in the transaction chain has a structural incentive to deliver it to the person who needs it most. The ledger does not lie. The asking price always did. What did your boat actually sell for compared to what you listed it for? Drop the number in the comments. Let's build the data. that the industry is not going to publish.

- 30% of Catamarans Listed Right Now Will NEVER Sell (Caraibe Yachts Data): Kind: captions Language: en A boat is sitting on a dock in Fort Lauderdale. The owner bought it in 2021 for $1.2 million. He listed it in March at $850,000. It dropped to $720,000. He is at $680,000 now. Three brokers, [music] zero offers. He keeps refreshing YachtWorld to check whether someone else's identical boat sold, and they all show the same asking price. None of them sold, either. This is not bad luck. This is happening to thousands of boats at once. And the reason it will not stop is something the industry has known about for 2 years and refused to say out loud. Caribee Yachts December 2025 market report delivers a number that should send a chill through anyone watching the used catamaran market. 30% of the boats currently listed are projected never to sell at all. This is not a throwaway estimate or a back-of-the-envelope guess. The figure comes from a forensic analysis of BoatWizard data, which covers more than 90% of brokered listings across the Caribbean and United States. The methodology is clear. Every active catamaran listing scraped from the major portals was tracked for time on market, sale status, and price movement. The survival analysis, borrowed straight from actuarial science, shows that as of the latest inventory snapshot, nearly one in three boats will age out of the market unsold, even after rounds of price reductions and broker swaps. The scale of the problem is visible in the raw numbers. On May 5th, 2026, YachtWorld returned 1,836 used [music] catamarans in its public search API. These are not theoretical listings or phantom boats. They are live, time-stamped entries, each with a hull, a price, and an owner waiting for a buyer who may never materialize. Caribee's projection is not a forecast about some distant future. It is a verdict on the current market reality. As of today, over 500 catamarans on YachtWorld alone are already in the unsold cohort, flagged by months, sometimes years, of zero movement. Their owners are trapped in a holding pattern, watching as the same models linger, disappear, and reappear, with only the asking price changing. The magnitude of this inventory overhang is the foundation for everything that follows. A market where oversupply is not a phase, but a structural feature, >> [music] >> and where the odds are stacked against the seller from the moment the listing goes live. BoatShed's analysis of 30,000 closed catamaran transactions leaves little room for optimism. On average, sellers are forced to cut their asking prices by 28% to 30% before a deal closes. That is The data set covers every major brokerage, every size class, and every region tracked by the BoatShed system. The pattern holds whether you are listing a 10-year-old Lagoon or a nearly new Fountaine Pajot. Owners who bought during the peak years, between 2015 and 2020, face even steeper losses. Over half of those resale prices end up slashed by more than 50% within a single ownership cycle. A million-dollar catamaran bought in 2019 often fetches less than $500,000 when it finally sells, if it sells at all. The pain is not limited to individual sellers. Manufacturer financials are flashing red. Fountaine Pajot, the French builder responsible for some of the most popular models on the market, reported a 12.8% revenue drop in the first half of fiscal year 2025 to 2026. This is not a blip. It is the sharpest contraction since the COVID lockdowns. Catana Group, builder of the Bali line, posted an even steeper decline at 23.8% over the same period. These are not just numbers on a spreadsheet. They are the direct result of oversupply, relentless discounting, and a market that no longer believes in the old price floor. The consequences are plain. Price erosion has become structural, not cyclical. Brokers are watching as used catamarans linger unsold, manufacturers cut production and slash prices, and owners absorb losses that would have been unthinkable just a few years ago. The evidence is overwhelming. This is not a temporary soft patch, but a full-scale market reset. Scarcity was the pitch, and freedom was the product. In 2020 and 2021, catamaran ads did not just show boats, they sold the dream of escape. Brokerages and manufacturers filled their marketing with soundbites about freedom to roam, private islands, and live anywhere, work everywhere. With international travel lockdown, the message hit like a drug. Suddenly, buyers who had never set foot on a multihull were wiring deposits for million-dollar vessels, convinced there would never be another chance. Waiting lists stretched into 2022. Dealers posted screenshots of backlogs and sold-out banners on model pages. Social media was flooded with stories of buyers flipping contracts for quick profits, feeding the sense that catamarans were a one-way ticket to a new life. The numbers tell the story. By late 2021, the typical purchase price for a new Lagoon 46 or Fountaine Pajot Elba 45 had surged to $1 million, sometimes $1.5 million, with barely a pause for negotiation. Brokers pushed the narrative that supply chain chaos made every hull precious, every slot a golden ticket. But the scarcity was engineered. Builders throttled production, citing delays, while quietly reserving inventory for charter fleets and preferred dealers. Broker portals like YachtWorld and BoatShed became echo chambers, showing lists of sold boats and fast-rising prices. The portal mirage was complete. Buyers saw only the top of the market, never the growing backlog of unsold inventory hidden by expired or relisted ads. This was not a passive bubble. It was a dream sold on purpose, with full-page ads and YouTube influencers repeating the mantra that catamarans only go up. The marketing was relentless, and the incentives were aligned. Dealers collected bonuses for new-build contracts, while brokers pocketed higher commissions on inflated sticker prices. The result was a psychological stampede, not just for boats, but for a story. One that would leave hundreds of owners trapped in a market that was [music] never as tight as it seemed. Charter companies didn't just participate in the pandemic catamaran surge, they drove it to extremes. From 2020 to 2022, commercial operators signed contracts for more than 700 new four-cabin cats, often buying entire production runs at once. These were not families dreaming of blue-water escapes. They were fleet managers timing deliveries to match five- and seven-year charter cycles, locking up supply before private buyers even had a chance. Builders like Lagoon and Fountaine Pajot shifted their lines to churn out charter layouts, pushing owner versions to the back of the queue. Wait lists stretched from months into years. But the true engine was the dealer incentive system. Factory holdback programs, delayed rebates paid only when a boat shipped and registered, turned every new-build order into a payday. Internal memos from 2021 show holdbacks of 15,000 euros to 25,000 euros per hull on 45-foot boats, with extra bonuses for bulk charter deals. A single 12-boat order could trigger an 80,000 euros payout, plus volume rebates buried in quarterly reports. For big Caribbean dealers, the difference between selling a new charter fleet and brokering a used boat wasn't just about commission, it was survival. New-build commissions hit 7% of manufacturer suggested retail price, plus incentives, while used brokerage fees rarely topped 5% [music] and often less after splits. The math was simple. Brokers could triple their earnings by steering buyers toward new builds instead of 3-year-old resales. Charter operators secured fleet pricing 8% to 12% below retail, when these boats hit resale in bulk. The order backlog, once a sign of strength, became a looming threat. Most 2020 to 2022 deliveries are set to exit charter service between 2025 and 2027, meaning hundreds of nearly identical four-cabin cats, optimized for maximum berths, not comfort, will soon flood a market already straining under oversupply. Dealer incentives, charter contracts, and factory rebates all converged, creating a boom that data now shows cannot be unwound. Charter fleets run on a clock that doesn't care about resale values or private owner dreams. The majority of four-cabin catamarans ordered in the pandemic boom, boats delivered in 2020, 2021, and 2022 are now staring down the end of their commercial service life. Five to seven years is the standard window for a charter hull. After that, the maintenance curve steepens, insurance premiums jump, and the economics of keeping the boat in fleet collapse. Operators cut their losses by dumping entire batches onto the used market in a synchronized wave. The calendar is not a theory. It's a schedule, and the next 3 years are circled in red. The numbers are not abstract. Industry filings and charter company disclosures confirm that between 2025 and 2027, nearly 1,004 cabin catamarans, mostly Lagoon, Bali, and Fountain Pajot, will exit charter fleets in the Caribbean and Mediterranean. These are not scattered one-off listings. They are bulk retirements timed by lease contracts and fleet renewal cycles. In 2020 alone, charter operators ordered more than 200 new four-cabin units. The following 2 years saw another 500-plus delivered. Now, as these contracts expire, the market is bracing for a deluge of near-identical hulls, all hitting the portals within a narrow window, all chasing the same shrinking pool of buyers. This is where the configuration mismatch becomes fatal. The resale market is already saturated with four-cabin charter-spec layouts, boats optimized for maximum berths, not private comfort. Private buyers, when they appear at all, are hunting for three-cabin owner versions. Caribee Yachts 2025 analysis found just 223-cabin Lagoon 42s on the market compared to 764-cabin variants. That ratio gets worse as the charter dump accelerates. The result is a mechanical oversupply of the least desirable spec, with each new wave of ex-charter hulls pushing the previous batch further down the price curve and deeper into listing limbo. This is not just a question of volume. It is a structural mismatch that cannot be solved by price cuts alone. Four-cabin hulls linger, unconverted, unwanted, while three-cabin models sell quickly at [music] a premium. The absorption rate collapses. Data shows that with every month, the unsold inventory grows more lopsided. By the end of 2027, the used market will be bottom-heavy with four-cabin charter relics, many of which will never sell at any price. The flood is not coming. It's already begun, and the water is rising one contract expiration at a time. Discounts that once seemed like a rare opportunity are now the main force dragging the entire used catamaran market downward. In 2025, dealer advertisements for new Lagoon 46 boats began to appear with sticker prices slashed to $850,000, down from the official factory list of $950,000. These are not isolated cases or end-of-season clearances. They are the public face of a new reality. Manufacturers are using factory-to-dealer discounts as a primary tool to clear unsold inventory and keep production lines moving. The impact is immediate and brutal for anyone trying to sell a used boat. When a buyer can walk into a dealership and order a brand new Lagoon for less than a 3-year-old model listed on YachtWorld, the resale market freezes. Sellers are forced to chase the new price down, cutting their own listings again and again, often with no result. The numbers from the builders are damning. Katanagroup, the company behind the Bali line, reported a 23.8% revenue drop [music] in their 2024 to 2025 financial filings. Fountain Pajot's own statements show a 12.8% decline in the first half of fiscal year 2025 to 2026. These are not cyclical fluctuations. They are the direct result of new build discounts rippling through the market, erasing the old price floor and leaving existing owners stranded above the waterline. Dealer inventory has climbed to nearly 6% of total capacity, up from just over 2% before the pandemic, exposing how much unsold stock is now clogging the system. The discount domino effect is visible in the brokerage data. Hedonic regression models built on more than 2,000 used catamaran listings show that every 10% discount on a new build shaves another 4% off the average used list price within 3 months. The effect is even worse for ex-charter boats, where the price pressure deepens to nearly 6% per discount wave. The result is a market where price cuts do not clear inventory, they just reset expectations lower and lower, trapping more sellers with every cycle. For private owners, the consequences are stark. Boats bought during the boom are now competing with factory-fresh models offered at prices that would have been unthinkable just 2 years ago. The resale market is not just soft, it is paralyzed. This is not a temporary dip. The numbers confirm a structural shift, one that leaves hundreds of catamarans functionally unsellable. Their value dictated not by age or condition, but by the next round of factory incentives. The cost burden of ownership, soon to be revealed, is only part of the story. The real damage is done every time a builder decides to move inventory at any price, resetting the market floor and leaving yesterday's buyers with nowhere to go. The annual cash drain of catamaran ownership is not a rounding error. It is a line-item shock that hits from the first dockage bill. >> In Fort Lauderdale, a 45-foot catamaran requires a wide-beam slip that runs between $1,600 and $2,000 per month. That is nearly double what a monohull of the same length pays for marina space, and over 12 months, the difference adds up to $10,000 or more just to keep the boat tied up. Insurance is the next blow. Underwriters charge catamaran owners anywhere from 62% to 220% more than comparable monohull policies, citing dual engines, larger rigs, and higher replacement costs. For a typical 45-foot catamaran, annual premiums land between $7,200 and $9,600 compared to $3,600 to $4,400 for a monohull. Routine maintenance is another silent siphon. Twin diesels mean double the service intervals for oil changes, impellers, filters, and spare parts. Yearly mechanical care averages $3,500 to $4,500. Haul-out costs climb as well, since many yards cannot accommodate a 12-meter beam, forcing owners to wait or pay a premium for cat-capable facilities. Each of these numbers is a hard recurring cash outflow. When owners realize the true cost of holding, the rush to sell is not a surprise, it is a financial necessity. A Lagoon 51 purchased for 1.2 million euros in May 2025 lands on the resale market just 5 years later. By August 2029, after months of price cuts and broker rotations, the boat finally changes hands for around 700,000 euros. Half a million euros gone. On paper, a 41% loss before even counting the cost of ownership. The numbers are not isolated or theoretical. Sale records from Caribee Yachts and BoatWizard confirm that this is now the norm for late-model catamarans, not the exception. But the ledger does not stop at the sale price. Over those 5 years, the owner paid roughly 10,000 euros per year in marina fees and another 8,000 euros annually for insurance, each expense set by the realities of wide-beam slips and higher-risk underwriting. Routine maintenance and haul-outs added another 4,500 euros per year, with the yard premium climbing as cat-capable dry docks hit capacity. In some regions, the wait for a haul-out stretched past 12 months, forcing owners to either pay extra or risk skipping critical work. Add it all together, 50,000 euros in marina fees, 40,000 euros in insurance, 22,500 [music] euros in mechanical upkeep, and at least 10,000 euros in haul-out surcharges. The total cost of ownership over 5 years exceeds 120,000 euros on top of the 500,000 euros lost to depreciation. For many, the dream of owning a flagship catamaran has ended not in freedom or adventure, but in a financial crater from which there is no easy escape. 30%, not a forecast, an audited number from Caribee Yachts built on 90% of the active listings. The portal mirage is still live, but the correction isn't finished. From 2025 to 2027, the market will test every remaining illusion. You were sold a dream on purpose, but the sovereign sailor knew only the math holds the wheel. What's your plan? Drop your perspective below.

  2. @doublebtrucking [5 likes]: My 2 pennies on yachts.
First off, "boat clubs" as I understand them to be are basically like the "time share" scams. Sounds good until they hit you with maintenance fees, marina fees, only accessible certain times ect. ect. 
2nd: people bitching about fuel cost make me laugh! The average usage is about 150 hours a year(we actually average closer to 400). Let's be generous and say they use 4 gallons an hour and diesel goes up an extra $2 a gallon, that's only an extra $1200 A YEAR!! $100 A MONTH! If they can't afford that they have no business owning a yacht! We budget $15k a year just for ANY UNUSUAL (not regular) maintenance expenses that might occur on 400k-500k yacht and what we don't use rolls too the next year anticipating a repower eventually. 
3rd: Yachts in the 45-65 foot range are dropping like a rock or sitting and decaying. The covid boom has cycled and their poor maintenance is killing them, sitting on the dock paying $$$ monthly for a yacht they can't sell  or afford, at or near what they paid and can't/won't keep up on the maintenance. 
4th: From my experience  buc value -10% is usually a good starting offer, then minus survey issues. Issue is these idiots payed 400k for a 300k yacht that's now worth 250k in a "perfect" survey but needs 80k in deferred maintenance and don't understand why they're getting $150k offers! (Because their brokers blowing smoke up their ass and it's costing them money every month!)They need to do another line of coke, clearly like this guy, and just dream another dream, keep paying their marina fees and loosing maintenance costs every month. While I respect Current Yachts and will probably consider you when we go to a large yacht, mainly because "most" brokers are crooks, please don't pull the same BS they do. Play the long game with honesty. More bees with honey than vinegar.
On a side note, I do enjoy most of your videos but recently they are becoming VERY much like AI so I hope you start making them a little more personal.
