Shipyard's brokers&nbsp; and marketing departments are asking you to pay&nbsp;&nbsp; a massive premium for yachts that are lighter,&nbsp; stiffer, and supposedly almost indestructible.&nbsp;&nbsp; They talk about technology straight from Formula&nbsp; 1 and aviation, insisting that a carbon fuselage&nbsp;&nbsp; is the absolute ideal. Let's take a look at the&nbsp; market. The uh Sun Reef brand successfully pumps&nbsp;&nbsp; this trend by announcing the construction of&nbsp; fully carbon editions of its luxurious Sun&nbsp;&nbsp; Reef 80 catamarans. Wally shipyard has proudly&nbsp; promoted its iconic units for years such as&nbsp;&nbsp; the over 32m Wally wind 110 built entirely from&nbsp; carbon composits. Meanwhile, Pushing in its ATX&nbsp;&nbsp; model boast about a carbon revolution where the&nbsp; hyper light carbon hull is supposed to guarantee&nbsp;&nbsp; aerospace precision and speeds exceeding 40 knots.&nbsp; Listening to this, one might think that carbon&nbsp;&nbsp; fiber is the holy grail. But today, we are taking&nbsp; off the marketing blinders. Instead of reading&nbsp;&nbsp; advertising brochures, let's look at the facts.&nbsp; Today, I will show you why shipyard's fixation&nbsp;&nbsp; on extreme weight reduction of the whole kills its&nbsp; long-term durability. You will understand how the&nbsp;&nbsp; ocean destroys these yachts from the inside&nbsp; out through invisible resin fatigue and why&nbsp;&nbsp; surveyors miss it. And finally, we will answer&nbsp; the most important question. Why do these super&nbsp;&nbsp; fast yachts ultimately become uninsurable,&nbsp; fragile financial traps? Let's dive in. The&nbsp;&nbsp; um entire premise of of carbon holes rests&nbsp; on a fundamental flow, treating a yacht like&nbsp;&nbsp; an airplane. Indeed, carbon fiber is brilliant in&nbsp; aviation where loads are predictable, aerodynamic,&nbsp;&nbsp; and consistent. But the ocean is not air. Carbon&nbsp; fiber, unlike common thick fiberglass or metals,&nbsp;&nbsp; is extremely brittle. Metals such as marine&nbsp; grade aluminum or steel have a crucial property,&nbsp;&nbsp; the capacity for plastic deformation.&nbsp; Long before a steel hole cracks, it bends,&nbsp;&nbsp; giving you a clear visual warning. Carbon doesn't&nbsp; do that. When it crosses its stress limit,&nbsp;&nbsp; there is no plastic face. It just fails suddenly&nbsp; and catastrophically. The hole of a motorboat,&nbsp;&nbsp; which constantly battles the elements, needs&nbsp; flexibility to absorb the impact of waves. And&nbsp;&nbsp; carbon simply does not possess this flexibility by&nbsp; nature. This brings us to the most closely guided&nbsp;&nbsp; secret in marine engineering, the phenomenon&nbsp; of resin fatigue. Remember that term. Let's&nbsp;&nbsp; test out. Let's get one thing straight. A carbon&nbsp; hull is not a monolith. It consists of incredibly&nbsp;&nbsp; strong yet hurt in carbon strands suspended in a&nbsp; relatively soft epoxy resin matrix. The resin is&nbsp;&nbsp; essentially just a glue. Here, when you are moving&nbsp; at high speed, the hull violently hits the water,&nbsp;&nbsp; which at those speeds feels like solid concrete,&nbsp; thousands of times in a process called slamming.&nbsp;&nbsp; The stiff carbon fibers easily withstand&nbsp; the tension but they are unable to absorb&nbsp;&nbsp; these transverse impacts. Therefore, this entire&nbsp; gigantic momentum must be absorbed by the soft&nbsp;&nbsp; resin. With each successive strike of the yacht&nbsp; against the wave, micro cracks form in the resin&nbsp;&nbsp; matrix. Chemical bonds around the fiber simply&nbsp; break. The consequence, the yacht slowly and&nbsp;&nbsp; imperceptibly loses its out ofthe-box rigidity.&nbsp; This phenomenon was first extensively documented&nbsp;&nbsp; in racing yachts which after a few seasons of&nbsp; brutal punishment on the water became structurally&nbsp;&nbsp; soft and lost their competitive edge. Today that&nbsp; exact same merciless process is unfolding beneath&nbsp;&nbsp; the decks of multi-million dollar motor yachts.&nbsp; But the true destructive drama begins when the&nbsp;&nbsp; yacht operates in salt water. As fatigue induced&nbsp; microcracks appear in the resin, water inevitably&nbsp;&nbsp; seeps in. And from a physics standpoint, water&nbsp; is an incompressible fluid. When the yacht slams&nbsp;&nbsp; down off a wave, the water trapped inside these&nbsp; microcracks acts as a powerful hydraulic wedge,&nbsp;&nbsp; generating hundreds of bars of pressure, it&nbsp; violently tears through the subsequent layers of&nbsp;&nbsp; super expensive carbon composite from the inside&nbsp; out. This process is irreversible damination.&nbsp;&nbsp; The worst part of it all is that your shiny,&nbsp; impeccably painted hull still looks perfect from&nbsp;&nbsp; the outside. The destruction progresses silently&nbsp; deep within the structure. You often only discover&nbsp;&nbsp; it years later when the bottom of the hull&nbsp; suddenly fractures around the engine mounts. Uh,&nbsp;&nbsp; at this point, you might be asking, "If carbon&nbsp; is so brittle, why don't the wings snap off the&nbsp;&nbsp; new Boeings?" The answer is brutal. Aviation&nbsp; carbon and yacht carbon are two completely&nbsp;&nbsp; different worlds. In aviation, panels are cured&nbsp; with micrometric precision in sterile conditions&nbsp;&nbsp; using powerful autoclaves and vacuum systems that&nbsp; eliminate every single air bubble under immense&nbsp;&nbsp; pressure. Meanwhile, no one cures massive thame&nbsp; yacht hulls in autoclaves. It is both physically&nbsp;&nbsp; impractical and economically unfeasible. Shipyards&nbsp; rely on the resin infusion process in standard&nbsp;&nbsp; factory conditions. While it is an advanced method&nbsp; in such a massive manually prepared layup, there&nbsp;&nbsp; are almost always inherent manufacturing defects,&nbsp; microvoids, dry spots, and fiber misalignment. In&nbsp;&nbsp; aviation, such an element goes to the trash. In&nbsp; the yacht industry, these microscopic gaps become&nbsp;&nbsp; triggers for the resin fatigue and damination&nbsp; described a moment ago. But wait, you might think,&nbsp;&nbsp; I'll just hire a marine surveyor before buying&nbsp; a yacht on the secondary market. Unfortunately,&nbsp;&nbsp; when it comes to full carbon constructions,&nbsp; traditional marine surveyors are technologically&nbsp;&nbsp; blind. A standard prepurchase survey relies&nbsp; on electronic moisture meters and tap testing&nbsp;&nbsp; the hull with a hammer. When it comes to carbon,&nbsp; these methods are completely useless. Because um&nbsp;&nbsp; carbon is electrically conductive, it interferes&nbsp; with the signals of traditional moisture sensors&nbsp;&nbsp; resulting in completely unreliable and distorted&nbsp; data. Secondly, the extreme rigidity of the carbon&nbsp;&nbsp; laminate combined with a thick insulating core&nbsp; muffles any acoustic signs of resin failure making&nbsp;&nbsp; it impossible to detect by ear. So the industry&nbsp; is turning towards expensive non-destructive&nbsp;&nbsp; ultrasonic diagnostics in short entity. Sounds&nbsp; great, but that's where the biggest trap lies.&nbsp;&nbsp; Large carbon hulls are built as robust sandwich&nbsp; structures. They consist of an outer carbon skin,&nbsp;&nbsp; a thick structural foam core in the middle, and&nbsp; an inner carbon skin. Ultrasound is excellent for&nbsp;&nbsp; examining thin aerospace laminates. But when&nbsp; we try to scan a thick yacht hull, the sound&nbsp;&nbsp; waves are scattered and heavily attenuated by the&nbsp; soft foam core. It results from a phenomenon that&nbsp;&nbsp; engineers call a false negative. The equipment&nbsp; shows that the hull is clear and the expert&nbsp;&nbsp; issues a clean bill of health. You are buying&nbsp; a six-year-old yacht for several million euros,&nbsp;&nbsp; relying on this report, while in reality&nbsp; its carbon structure may be shattering&nbsp;&nbsp; from within. You are blind. Your surveyor is&nbsp; blind. And worst of all, your insurer is blind,&nbsp;&nbsp; too. Does all of this mean that carbon fiber is&nbsp; just a marketing gimmick and should be avoided at&nbsp;&nbsp; all costs? Absolutely not. Uh, it's a remarkable&nbsp; material with one crucial caveat. You must know&nbsp;&nbsp; exactly how and where to apply it on a vessel. The&nbsp; laws of physics are absolute in the ocean. The the&nbsp;&nbsp; underwater sections, the areas that daily endure&nbsp; thousands of tons of dynamic hydraulic pressure,&nbsp;&nbsp; docking impacts and wave slamming must have the&nbsp; ability to dissipate energy. The best material&nbsp;&nbsp; for this remains solid proven fiberglass GRP or&nbsp; in the case of mega yaks, aluminum or steel. In&nbsp;&nbsp; Neville architecture, carbon fiber should be&nbsp; treated like a surgical scalpel. Fortunately,&nbsp;&nbsp; the industry's biggest players understand this&nbsp; perfectly and have moved away from the risky&nbsp;&nbsp; trend of building motor yacht hulls entirely out&nbsp; of carbon. Take a look at the revolutionary smart&nbsp;&nbsp; performance series by San Lorenzo. Models like&nbsp; the SP92 or SP 110 are perfect examples of this&nbsp;&nbsp; balanced approach. Uh consider Alber aimut&nbsp; carbonte line or the powerful Fereti 1000,&nbsp;&nbsp; the secret behind these masterpieces. a smart&nbsp; hybrid philosophy. While the whole remains solid,&nbsp;&nbsp; dependable GRP, carbon fiber is reserved for&nbsp; the high altitude components, roofs, masts, and&nbsp;&nbsp; superructures where reducing weight matters most&nbsp; for stability. Why is this approach so effective?&nbsp;&nbsp; Shaving off significant weight from several&nbsp; meters above the water line drastically lowers&nbsp;&nbsp; the vessel's center of gravity. This minimizes&nbsp; rolling, making the yacht incredibly stable.&nbsp;&nbsp; At the same time, the carbon components are safe&nbsp; from fatigue because they are isolated from direct&nbsp;&nbsp; wave impacts. This is engineering that makes real&nbsp; sense and it's a premium worth paying for. Let's&nbsp;&nbsp; talk about your money now because this is where&nbsp; the industry can be at its most brutal. Many of&nbsp;&nbsp; you will ask, "Wait a minute. Do these sky-high&nbsp; insurance premiums simply exist because all&nbsp;&nbsp; carbon hulls are usually found on high performance&nbsp; yachts? You know, the ones hitting 60 or 70 knots&nbsp;&nbsp; that are much more likely to strike a submerged&nbsp; object." The answer is yes, that is part of the&nbsp;&nbsp; truth. Extreme speed equals extreme risk. At 70&nbsp; knots, a captain's reaction time is slashed and&nbsp;&nbsp; the kinetic energy of an impact is staggering.&nbsp; Most standard recreational policies impose&nbsp;&nbsp; strict limits or outright exclusions for speeds&nbsp; exceeding 45 or 50 knots. High performance boats&nbsp;&nbsp; built for offshore racing are by definition in a&nbsp; completely different insurance league. But beware,&nbsp;&nbsp; your biggest investment problem lies not only in&nbsp; the speed itself. The real issue for the insurer&nbsp;&nbsp; is the material. is a literal carbon tax imposed&nbsp; on almost every full carbon vessel. Even on luxury&nbsp;&nbsp; cruisers like the large Persing yachts or cruising&nbsp; catamarans that sail at much more modest speeds&nbsp;&nbsp; in the London insurance markets, fully carbon&nbsp; yachts have even acquired their own grim nickname,&nbsp;&nbsp; fragile liabilities. Why? It comes down to pure&nbsp; mathematics and the cost of restoration. If you&nbsp;&nbsp; run a ground or hit a rock with a conventional GRP&nbsp; yacht, the repair is straightforward. grind out&nbsp;&nbsp; the area, apply vinylester resin with glass fiber&nbsp; reinforcements, and finish with gel coat. The&nbsp;&nbsp; process is cost- effective, repeatable, and widely&nbsp; understood. However, if you damage a carbon hull&nbsp;&nbsp; in a highload area such as the engine room, repair&nbsp; costs skyrocket into the stratosphere. Carbon&nbsp;&nbsp; fiber cannot be patched in a dusty shipyard with&nbsp; a resin roller. Repairing structural delimination&nbsp;&nbsp; requires encapsulating the yacht in sterile&nbsp; chambers using vacuum pumps and applying precision&nbsp;&nbsp; thermal curing all overseen by aerospace engineers&nbsp; who must perfectly recreate the original fiber&nbsp;&nbsp; orientation. Uh the cost of such an operation can&nbsp; be astronomical and insurers are well aware of&nbsp;&nbsp; it. Consequently, annual premiums for a full&nbsp; cabonata significantly higher by definition.&nbsp;&nbsp; Underwriting syndicates live in constant fear&nbsp; of a single collision. One where the repair&nbsp;&nbsp; bill could easily equal the total insured value&nbsp; of the vessel. However, the real investment drama&nbsp;&nbsp; unfolds on the brokerage market. When a carbon&nbsp; yacht approaches the 10-year mark, insurance&nbsp;&nbsp; companies begin to panic. Fully aware of hidden&nbsp; resin fatigue and the limitations of NDT, they&nbsp;&nbsp; refuse to renew the most sought-after coverage,&nbsp; the agreed value policy. Instead, they push owners&nbsp;&nbsp; toward far less favorable terms. Insurers are&nbsp; increasingly compelling owners of aging carbon&nbsp;&nbsp; yachts to transition to actual cash value ACV&nbsp; policies. This means that in the event of a claim,&nbsp;&nbsp; the insurer will aggressively apply deductions&nbsp; for depreciation and wear and tear of the fatigue&nbsp;&nbsp; structure. When you attempt to sell, savvy buyers,&nbsp; fully aware of the unquantifiable technical risks,&nbsp;&nbsp; will use this insurance hurdle to slash your&nbsp; asking price. In the end, you are forced into&nbsp;&nbsp; massive price concessions. Your lightweight&nbsp; carbon bullet, once a technological marvel,&nbsp;&nbsp; depreciates far more aggressively than its solid&nbsp; whole counterparts made of fiberglass or steel.&nbsp;&nbsp; You end up paying twice. First, by buying into the&nbsp; mythical marketing in the showroom and years later&nbsp;&nbsp; by losing a fortune on resale value and insurance&nbsp; premiums. To summarize this lesson in engineering&nbsp;&nbsp; and economics, carbon fiber is a technological&nbsp; marvel. In professional racing and aerospace, it&nbsp;&nbsp; is absolutely indispensable. However, in a luxury&nbsp; yacht, forcing fragile carbon into the lower hole&nbsp;&nbsp; sections to withstand thousands of impacts&nbsp; from the swell is primarily a marketing ploy.&nbsp;&nbsp; It serves only to justify astronomical profit&nbsp; margins on new builds, unconditionally shifting&nbsp;&nbsp; the risk of invisible polymer fatigue and drastic&nbsp; depreciation straight onto your shoulders and your&nbsp;&nbsp; wallets. Don't let the gloss of a brochure dictate&nbsp; your investment. Look for smart hybrid solutions.&nbsp;&nbsp; Demand a hole that can handle the sea and a carbon&nbsp; top that masters the role. Take a page from the&nbsp;&nbsp; playbook of industry leaders like San Lorenzo and&nbsp; Fereti. And before you commit millions to a dream,&nbsp;&nbsp; hire the right experts to ensure it doesn't become&nbsp; a nightmare. At Wak Monaco, we show you the world&nbsp;&nbsp; as it truly is. We provide uncompromising&nbsp; technical audits and investment consulting&nbsp;&nbsp; to ensure you sail safely while your assets&nbsp; remain protected. Buy consciously. Subscribe&nbsp;&nbsp; for more technical truths from the closed&nbsp; world of super yachts. See you on the water.

CF almost always fails in compression, in tension the fibers are immensely strong, and the epoxy/fiber boundary is not stressed as it is in compression. In compression the limiting factor is the bond between the fiber and the resin, how well the the resin can keep the fibers in-column. A flaw in the construction process may leave a weak point that may expand with usage. CF bicycles have similar problems.

- Why This 1985 Hatteras 58 Feels Better Than New Boats: Kind: captions Language: en All right, guys. Welcome back to the harbor. Today's video is a little bit different. You've already seen the full walk-through on this boat. So, instead of pointing at every cabinet and switch, I just want to slow things down and talk about what this boat really is, where it came from, and why something like this still matters today. We're on a 1985 Hatteras 58 long-range motor yacht, longtail. And this one in particular is about as clean and well-kept as you're ever going to find. I mean, you can tell right away this isn't just another old boat that's been sitting. This one has been lived with, cared for, and actually understood. And that's important with a boat like this. Because Hatteras, especially in the '80s, wasn't building disposable boats. These things were built to last decades. And if you take care of them, they will. Now, a little background on Hatteras as a builder. Hatteras got its start back in the late '50s in North Carolina, and they really changed the game early on. They were one of the first companies to go all in on fiberglass when everyone else was still building in wood. And not only that, they overbuilt everything. Thick hulls, heavy layup, conservative engineering. That's why you still see so many of these boats around today. They didn't cut corners, and they weren't trying to make the lightest or fastest boats on the water. They were building something solid, something that could take a beating offshore, and still feel like a home inside. And that philosophy carried right into the '80s when this 58 was built. This era of Hatteras, mid-'70s into the '80s, is kind of a sweet spot. You get the classic lines, the heavy construction, but also more refined interiors, better layouts, and boats that were really designed for extended cruising and living aboard. And when you step back and look at the boat overall, it just has that classic Hatteras presence. Wide beam, over 18 ft, low, solid stance in the water, and a profile that just looks right. It doesn't try to be flashy, it's not trendy, it's just a good, honest motor yacht. Now, let's talk about size and feel, because numbers don't always tell the whole story. On paper, it's 58 ft, but in reality, this boat feels bigger. That 18-ft beam carries all the way through the boat, and you feel it immediately when you're inside. Big salon, wide walkarounds, roomy staterooms, it's not cramped anywhere. And that's one of the reasons people love these for live-aboard use. You're not squeezing into it, you're actually living in it. Now, this particular boat, captain maintained for 24 years, that alone tells you a lot. But boats don't stay like this by accident. That's years of someone staying ahead of things, keeping systems working, keeping everything clean, and not letting little issues turn into big ones. And you see it everywhere. The paint, freshly all gripped, that's not cheap, and it's not easy to do right. But when it's done properly like this, it completely transforms the boat. Gives it that deep, clean shine, and protects everything underneath. The varnish work, all that wood, rails, trim, it's not peeling, it's not faded. That takes time and effort to maintain. You just can't ignore it. Same with the cushions. Everything just looks cared for. And that's really the theme of this boat. It hasn't been owned, it's been maintained. Now, let's get into how this thing actually runs. Twin Caterpillars, about 660 horsepower's each, around 7 3,700 hours. For engines like this, that's not scary hours at all, especially if they've been maintained properly, which it looks like they have. This isn't a high-speed boat, obviously, and not trying to be. Cruising speed is around 11 knots, and you can push it closer to 18 if you want, but that's really not the point of this boat. This is all about steady, comfortable cruising. You pick a direction, settle in, and go. And with over 1,000 gallons of fuel on board, you've got serious range. This is a boat you can actually travel on. Not just hop around locally, but make real trips across the Gulf, run down the Keys, head up the coast, whatever you want to do. And while you're doing it, you've got Naiad stabilizers on board. That's a big deal. If you've ever been on a boat this size without stabilizers in a beam sea, you know it can get uncomfortable quick. Stabilizers take that roll out and just smooth everything down. It turns a long trip from something tiring into something enjoyable. Add in bow and stern thrusters, now you've got control. For a 58-ft, 79,000-lb boat, being able to move it around easily in tight spaces makes a huge difference. Whether you're docking, backing into a slip, or dealing with wind and current, those systems make it manageable. And again, that's part of what makes this a good liveaboard or cruising boat. It's not just capable, it's usable. Now, one thing I always like to talk about with boats like this is how they age. Because let's be honest, this is a 1985 boat. That's 40 years old. But, a well-built boat doesn't age the same way a cheap one does. The structure, the hull, the bones of the boat, that's still solid. What changes over time are the systems, the finishes, the soft goods. And this one, those things have been updated, maintained, and kept up. So, instead of feeling old, it feels classic. There's a difference. Classic means it holds up. It still works. It still makes sense. And that's exactly what this is. Now, as I'm walking through here, you can see the layout. And even though we're not doing a complete walk-through, this is worth mentioning of how practical it is. Big salon area, plenty of room to sit, relax, entertain. Galley that actually works, not just for show. Multiple staterooms, so you can have guests, family, or just spread out. And everything is laid out in a way that feels natural. Nothing is cramped. Nothing feels like an afterthought. That's something Hatteras was really good at, making boats that were comfortable to live in, not just to look at. Now, who is this boat really for? It's not for someone looking for speed. It's not for someone who wants something brand new and shiny with top trims everywhere. This is for someone who appreciates solid construction, classic design, real cruising capability, and a boat that's already proven itself over time. It's also for someone who understands maintenance. Because even though this is one that is in great shape, boats like this still need care. They reward you if you take care of them, and they will punish you if they if you don't. But, if you stay on top of it, you end up with something special. Something that's not being made anymore. Now, as we're moving through the boat here, you can see how quiet and calm everything feels. That's another thing about these heavier boats. They don't rattle, they don't feel flimsy, they just have solid, planted feel. Whether you're tied up or running. And when you're out on the water, especially on longer trips, that makes a big difference. Now, I'll say this, too. There's something about being on a boat that this like this that's just different from newer production boats. It feels more intentional. Less about trends, more about function. Less about showing off, more about actually using it. And I think that's why a lot of people are starting to come back around to these older Hatters boats. They realize you can get something like this with this kind of quality of build, this kind of space for a fraction of what a new boat would cost. test the waters number one the price of yachts varies Avast Lee an entry-level yacht can cost around $100,000 and the most expensive yacht to date is the Eclipse valued between 700 million and 1.5 billion dollars if you want to be mingling with the high rollers though you'll need to drop at least 20 million dollars on a private yacht like Tiger Woods did when he bought his one hundred and fifty five foot yacht privacy the general rule of thumb is you'll need to put aside ten percent of the cost of the yacht to ensure it's kept running at its optimum each year so in the case of Tiger Woods yacht privacy the additional cost per year would be in the region of two million dollars these costs would vary greatly depending on the purchase price where the yacht is docked how often it's being used the length of the yacht and how old the yacht is as well as what upgrades you've invested in number two registration fees need to be considered these fees can differ depending on the country or state you registering your yacht in international registries will charge the following for a yacht that's 24 meters or 78 feet or less in length in this case a one-off registration fee of five hundred and fifty dollars and then an annual tonnage tax of 750 dollars needs to be paid if the yacht measures more than 24 meters the registration fee is $1500 and the gross tonnage fee of up to 399 tons is seven hundred and fifty dollars four hundred to nine hundred and ninety nine tons is $1,000 and 1012 hundred and fifty dollars and choosing the earth's flag state is a very important decision because that flag state holds the jurisdiction over that specific yacht and in tax obligations privacy regulations liability in international waters and whether the yacht can be used for commercial endeavors number three your yacht needs a place to rest its head where you store your yacht will greatly influence your annual cost if you want people to see you on your pricey yacht then you best choose to to dock your super yacht there if that's out of your budget you could opt for a port to can france which will cost you in the region of 770 dollars a day there are plenty of marina maps available to help you decide the best place to moor your super yacht a less desirable marina would cost roughly $1,200 a month but your additional mooring fees would be incurred when you used your yacht for its intended purposes travelling the globe some marinas fees also consider length and width of your super yacht so expect some fluctuations there as well number four your yacht and your toys will need fuel budgeting your annual fees for fuel can be tricky each yacht is different and the way each yacht is handled is also different if you're cruising at around 8 to 10 knots your fuel usage will be lower than someone going at a faster speed let's assume you have a 60-foot yacht which can hold 1,200 gallons of fuel and the current average price of fuel is roughly two dollars fifty cents a gallon your yacht will cost three thousand dollars to fill out your fuel costs will fluctuate depending on how often you're in the water and also where you're filling up for example the average fuel price in France is roughly four dollars a gallon and in South Africa it's roughly $3 30 cents number five regular wear and tear needs to be factored in like any vehicle purchased regular wear and tear is par for the course to ensure longevity of your yacht regular maintenance is imperative experts estimate that maintenance will be around two percent of the boat's value in the first year and around 7.5% if the yacht is ten years and older your yacht needs to be washed and cleaned every two weeks the bottom of the boat needs to be scrubbed once a month your systems need to be checked regularly every three to six months your yacht should be waxed and every 12 to 24 months you need to repaint the bottom of your boat as the paint layers thin quickly and deteriorate expect to pay around $50 a foot for giving the hull a fresh coat of paint periodic refits are also to be expected you want to look the part all the time so when your yacht is looking a little tired you might want to consider refitting her to the former glory of giving her a much-needed facelift again these expenses can be as high as you're willing to go engines also wear out over time and equipment will need upgrading however there is a way to recoup some of your costs and will tell you how a bit later number six a yacht can't sail all by itself and unless you're able to captain your own ship you'll need a crew on board to do that job for you a 115 foot yacht would need a captain a mate two stewardesses and a chef the salary for each crew member will also fluctuate depending on the size of the yacht the yacht crew salary guide for 2019 recommended the following salaries for yachts measuring between 70 to 100 feet on a yearly basis the captain between sixty-five thousand and one hundred and ten thousand dollars a chef between thirty six thousand two fifty two thousand dollars and a stewardess between thirty to forty eight thousand dollars and if you don't meet your crew full-time you'll need to pay them a daily rate which will be roughly the following on a yacht of the same size the captain between three hundred and three hundred and seventy five dollars a day the chef between 200 to 250 and the stewardess between 175 to 250 dollars a day over and above their salary your crew will need food uniforms transport on board expenses such as linen soap laundry services and so forth if your crew has been with you for a few months you're also liable for their dental and health insurance and full-time crews need four to six weeks of paid holidays each year number seven fine dining is expected on your yacht and these costs can be vastly different if you're hosting parties with canapes and fine champagne your budget will be substantially larger than someone who's making a family tray lasagna with a few beers and a bucket of ice your crew will also need to eat so their dietary requirements need to be taken care of too on a super yacht it's estimated the food beverages and flowers can cost the owner between one and three million dollars a year and if you like to throw the occasional party remember to factor in a deejay decor extra drinks and staff to keep your guests 100% satisfied number eight insurance is non-negotiable your yacht won't be allowed to sail without it the annual insurance fees will be influenced by a few factors including where you keep the yacht the size of the vessel and of course the amenities on board stacks insurance brokerage have been insuring yachts for years and they estimate that a 60-foot yacht insured in the Northeast will cost between 6,000 and 8,000 dollars a year but some will insure the same yacht in Florida and the rate increases between 10,000 and 12,000 dollars and that's only if the yacht is used for private use if you intend to charter it the costs will rise substantially now that you're getting a good feel for the real price of owning a yacht have a look at our video the top 10 most expensive yachts in the world to get a bigger picture of what these billionaires are paying for their luxury vessels number 9 communication is key when you're out in the open waters you need to ensure your communication systems are functioning perfectly a temporary Authority ship radio station license is $100 and a full term ship radio station license is 200 over and above that most modern yachts have high-tech navigational electronics air conditioning satellite TV Wi-Fi 24 hour video and water purification systems in place these all need to be maintained and many will have monthly fees which like other things will vary depending on location number 10 safety is paramount every yacht must have life jackets on board throwable floatation devices fire extinguishers visual signaling devices and sound signalling devices lifejackets can cost anywhere from $12 right up to 350 dollars plus if you have a yacht that has space for 50 crew members and 30 guests and were opting for the priciest brand of lifejacket that's a total of 28 thousand dollars number 11 pirates are always a safety issue yacht owners have to be constantly thinking of new ways to protect their guests crew and assets and all of these are costly endeavors again this cost can fluctuate and you can either use the services of a company that specializes in yacht protection or you can try and do it yourself there are many options when it comes to anti pirate technology or ATP for starters you could purchase a long range acoustic device this non-lethal device uses pain inducing sound beams to release a sound that is unbearable for humans but it won't come cheap for a basic handheld model you can expect to pay roughly $5,000 and the more expensive models can range from 100,000 to 190,000 dollars number 12 yachting is not complete without some that others aspire to own number 13 is owning a yacht a good investment in short no from the moment your brand-new yacht enters the water it loses value others may argue that anyone owning a yacht no it's not a financial investment but rather an investment in status and respect number-14 try before you buy a piece of sound advice from the experts is to give yachting a try before purchasing your own seek the help of a charter broker and go to look at various yachts available step inside them feel them out and if possible charter them for a few days head to a local Boat Show and ask a variety of people the same questions and make a judgement call from their respective answers after you've done all of your research sleep on it and if you still think it's for you you know you've made the right decision you might also consider sharing the costs with a friend and splitting the use of the yacht a great way to still enjoy your time on the water without all the heavy burden of the cost yourself number fifteen there is a way of recouping some of your financial outlay if you look into chartering your yacht do note though that marine regulations have a limit on how many passengers you can have on board so you can't rent out your yacht and cram it full of travelers to attempt to make a quick buck the limit is between 12 and 36 passengers regardless of how large the yacht is however it's been noted that when the guest list is higher than 12 it's not technically classified as a charter anymore but rather a passenger ship there's also stiff competition when it comes to chartering - 790 Million Lawsuit Against the WIDOW & Bayesian Yacht Owner | SY News Ep562: Kind: captions Language: en This is Superyacht News in association with Go Earth boating, navigating boat buyers to better deals. Hi, welcome back to the channel. So, the Italian Sea Group has filed a lawsuit against Rev, the company that owned the Basian superyacht. The company has filed a €456 million euro or $790 million claim in a court in Sicily alleging that the crew of the yacht were responsible for the sinking. The sailing yacht Basian sank after a freak storm in Sicily in August 2024 just outside the small fishing port of Porticello with seven fatalities. Following is based on reporting by the Telegraph in London who claimed to have seen the lawsuit. Now the claim is against Revton, the Isle of Man registered company that owned the yacht. It also names James Cufffield, the captain, and crew members Timothy Eaton and Matthew Griffiths. The lawsuit asserts that Revto is liable for the actions of the crew. The Italian Sea Group, which is majority owned by Giovani Costantino, claims that the crew's incompetence and negligence caused the yacht to capsize and sink. The lawsuit states that the yacht was unsinkable, but sank because the crew failed to shut hatches, ignored weather warnings, and did not lower the keel. It claims the captain and crew committed an incredible and unspeakable series of very serious errors and omissions that prevented the vessel from writing itself. The Italian Sea Group claims it has lost hundreds of millions of euros in sales as a direct consequence of blame for the tragedy. The lawsuit says that the Italian Sea Group suffered a ruinous loss of revenue and profits, including a slump in its share price, a collapse in the value of Peruni Na'vi brand. Peri Navi yacht sales falling to zero since the accident. The company states it's been unable to sell a single Perini branded yacht and has received no expressions of interest from brokers. Our source close to the Lynch family criticized the lawsuit as cynical, desperate, opportunistic, and in bad faith. The article contrasts the lawsuit with findings by the UK's Marine Accident Investigation Branch, which reported the O had vulnerabilities unknown to the crew. Angela Picarus Lynch declined to comment on the lawsuit. The article notes that the Italian Sea Group previously sued the New York Times over reporting that questioned the yacht's design showing a pattern of litigation related to the disaster. It should be noted that the Basian yacht was built by Pirini Na'vi, a company that was independent at the time and not at that time part of the Italian Sea Group. And the company had declared bankruptcy in 2021 and was bought by the Italian Sea Group. Now, you might remember if you watch the channel since this happened, that there was a lawsuit filed in September 2024, less than a month after the sinking by a law firm representing the Italian Sea Group. And in that suit, they were looking for €165 million in compensation. Again, blaming the crew for the disaster. The following day, the Italian Sea Group put out a statement saying that the lawsuit had been filed in error and they'd fired that law firm that was representing them. They said that they were simply looking into the feasibility of a lawsuit and the company filed it without their permission. The lawsuit has been filed by the Italian Sea Group of which Giovani Constantino is the CEO. This is the same person who came out the day after the sinking while search and rescue was still in progress and blamed the crew for the sinking without any evidence support to support his claims. Seeming to want to control the narrative of the sinking, he claimed that the crew had left open the rear port side shell door, causing the yacht to flood. The crew denied the shell door had been left open. However, when the hull was recovered, the hull's shell door was shut. Now, whether or not that door was dogged is yet to be confirmed. The company also released a PDF file to all the news media companies in Italy with a disputed version of events and omitted details of the sinking. Now, we were unable to get a copy of this despite asking repeatedly, eventually being given a copy by another source. The file and Mr. Constantino appeared to rely heavily on AIS data showing how the vessel turned to port before sinking to allege the crew had left the rear shell door on the port side rear open. However, we proved the AIS data was missing vital gyro information that called into question those last moments. The gyro information within an AIS signal gives the heading of the vessel at any given time. And without that gyro data, AIS websites such as Marine Traffic, as shown here, effectively they guess as to what's happening with the vessel because they don't know which way the vessel is pointing. If the vessel moves, the AIS without the gyro data assumes the vessel is moving forwards. So in this case, it drew a yacht turning to port. Although evidence now points to the yacht actually turning to starboard as she sank. Now, the Mib, the Marine Accident Investigation Branch, interim report states that the SIB, the stability information book that is written by the yacht builder, Brun Navi, and given to the skipper of the yacht on delivery, omitted vital data for operating in motoring conditions where the centerboard was raised and no sales were up. The Wolson unit study which was commissioned by the Mib and carried out physical modeling studies of Basian determined that the 72 m tall mast accounted for about 50% of the total wind healing moment of the vessel when the wind was exactly on the beam and the remainder was predominantly made up of the rigging and the fill sails. The profile of the mast also produced a degree of effective lift delivering an increase in the healing moment applied by the wind peaking in winds of about 20° off the bow. So basically what they were saying there is not only was the majority of the windage of the vessel created by that giant mast but that it effectively lifted the boat out of the water increasing the center of gravity. Now, every time we make a video about this subject, the same comment is repeatedly made. And that is along the lines of why was the centerboard in the raised position or why didn't they lower the centerboard? And although we've addressed it on a number of occasions, it still comes up even now on all videos. So, we'll address it here again. Now, the centerboard is in the raised position on this yacht when at anchor for a number of reasons. But firstly, what is the centerboard? Centerboard is a retractable hole appendage which pivots out of a slot in the hole of the sailboat. And the retractability allows the centerboard to be raised to operate in shallow waters to move the center of lateral resistance to reduce drag when the full area of the centerboard is not needed or when removing the boat from the water. At the time of the sinking, the centerboard was in the raised position. And this is because of a number of factors, but mainly because the stability information book or SIB, which is written by Perini Nav'i and provided to the vessel upon its delivery, as I mentioned earlier, said that the centerboard was only to be lowered when the vessel was under sail or was more than 60 nautical miles from the coast, regardless of whether the sails were in use or not. There are a few reasons why a centerboard in the lower position can be dangerous. Uh, one is that the anchor chain can become entangled on the centerboard when at anchor if the wind has a sudden change and can cause the vessel to list dangerously. Another reason is that if the yacht had a lowered centerboard and the vessel starts to drag anchor, which which is what happened in this case and was pushed towards a shallow area, the centerboard can make contact with the seabed and cause the vessel to list over. So the next question that comes up usually is why didn't they lower the centerboard when they realized they were in trouble. Now we spoke at length to well a number of people but one of them in particular a former engineer who worked on Perini Navi yachts and he said that the lowering of the centerboard was time consuming in a in a case of an emergency and involved a number of crew and at that point of time the last thing they would be thinking about was to lower the centerboard. The more pertinent thing to do would be to get the generators running and to turn the vessel into the wind and this is what was happening according to testimony from the crew that was given to the MEIB. Now the MEIB also put a caution on the interim report and it read as as follows. Readers are cautioned that there is the possibility that new evidence may become available that might alter the circumstances as depicted in this report. Let me know what you think uh in the comments and uh yeah, we'll uh we'll take it from there. Now, I've got one one more thing before I go. Um that milliondoll plus luxury yacht at Motach Double Shot, which grounded in the Exumas on the 26th of December is still grounded just outside the Emerald Bay Marina. The vessel at this point has been abandoned uh with no action by anybody involved with the yacht ownership. The island gave a 10-day grace period for the owner to retrieve the vessel, which he has not done. Furthermore, we've been unable to contact anyone who owns that vessel via the company in the US. The company also has been ordered to pay unpaid mooring fees in Miami, and an order has been placed on the yacht to seize the vessel and sell it at auction to recover those fees. Now, we've got video footage here taken by somebody on the island just yesterday showing the vessel is in the same position. Unfortunately, the authorities on the island did not have the equipment or the manpower.
